suspicious_text: To determine the refractive index $n$, it is necessary to examine the square root of the complex value $n^{2}=\varepsilon\mu_{M}$. This yields two possible square roots, $n=\pm\sqrt{|n^{2}|}e^{i(\phi/2)}$, where $\phi$ is the argument of $n^{2}$, given by the sum of the arguments of $\varepsilon$ and $\mu_{M}$. The appropriate root is selected based on the criterion that the real part of $n$ is negative if $n_{eff}<0$ and positive otherwise. An analysis of the real and imaginary parts of $n$ in the quark-gluon plasma, considering various values of $\eta/s$, reveals that the properties of the real part of $n$ are consistent with those of $n_{eff}$, except near the magnetic permeability pole where significant differences emerge. Additionally, the viscous correction to the real and imaginary parts of $n$ is negligible, except at specific frequencies, and is consistent with previously reported hard-thermal-loop results.

source_text: We study glass behavior in a mixture of elliptic and circular particles in two dimensions at low temperatures using an orientation-dependent Lennard-Jones potential. The ellipses have a mild aspect ratio ( $\sim 1.2)$ and tend to align at low temperatures, while the circular particles play the role of impurities disturbing the ellipse orientations at a concentration of $20\%$ . These impurities have a size smaller than that of the ellipses and attract them in the homeotropic alignment. As a result, the coordination number around each impurity is mostly five or four in glassy states. We realize double glass, where both the orientations and the positions are disordered but still hold mesoscopic order. suspicious_text: In the context of functional graphs, a specific measurement known as the six-length of a vertex is determined by a function's repetitive application. This measurement, denoted as $\mathfrak{s}_{f}(v)$ for a vertex $v$, essentially calculates the minimum number of times a function $f$ must be applied to $v$ such that the result is a vertex that has been previously visited during these applications. The process involves composing the function $f$ with itself multiple times, starting from the identity function, and tracking the sequence of vertices obtained. Interestingly, this six-length can be broken down into two components: the tail-length, which represents the initial sequence of unique vertices before a cycle is entered, and the cycle-length, which is the size of the cycle itself. These components provide insight into the structure of the graph by identifying how a vertex is connected within a cycle and how it enters that cycle.
source_text:
The six-length of a vertex in a functional graph is defined as follows: Let $f:V\rightarrow V$ be a function and let $id$ denote the identity function on $V$ . Let ${f}^{k}$ denote the $k$ -times composition of $f$ , that is ${f}^{0}=id$ and ${f}^{k}={f}^{k-1}\circ f$ for $k\geq 1$ . The six-length of $v\in V$ is defined as 
 
 $\mathfrak{s}_{f}(v)=\min\left\{k\in{\mathbb{N}}:{f}^{k}(v)\in\{{f}^{j}(v):0\leq j\leq k-1\}\right\}.$ 
 An example for the six-length in a functional graph is given in Fig.1(a) . Note that $\mathfrak{s}_{f}(v)$ can be decomposed into the tail-length $\mathfrak{t}_{f}(v)$ and the cycle-length $\mathfrak{c}_{f}(v)$ as indicated in Fig.1(b) . More formally, the tail-length is the unique integer that satisfies 
 
 $\displaystyle\mathfrak{t}_{f}(v)<\mathfrak{s}_{f}(v)\quad\text{ and }\quad{f}^{\mathfrak{s}_{f}(v)}(v)={f}^{\mathfrak{t}_{f}(v)}(v),$ 
 and the cycle-length is given by $\mathfrak{c}_{f}(v):=\mathfrak{s}_{f}(v)-\mathfrak{t}_{f}(v)$ .

The former section describes the formation of H ii regions whereas the latter section discusses their evolution and the formation of second-generation stars. Finally, in section 6, we give concluding remarks.

Although the study by Ceperley and Alder is limited to a T = 0 K computation, it indicates that ferromagnetism can emerge in electron gases with certain characteristics, and subsequent calculations for CaB6 yield a Fermi energy corresponding to a temperature comparable to the observed Curie temperature, prompting further theoretical investigations into the underlying mechanisms of this novel magnetic behavior.

A complete description of the set of solutions and its dependence on the parameters is at the moment out of our reach. The rate of the charge exchange reaction 
 
 ${\rm H}_{2}+{\rm H}^{+}\rightarrow{\rm H}_{2}^{+}+{\rm H},$  (1) 
 was recently revised by Savin et al. (2004a,b). We use this updated rate because these processes become important when the ionization fraction of the gas is large. Another important process is collisional dissociation of H \textsubscript{2} by electron impacts 
 
 ${\rm H}_{2}+e\rightarrow 2{\rm H}+e.$  (2) 
 The commonly used values are for H \textsubscript{2} molecules which are initially in the $v=0$ level (e.g. Shapiro & Kang 1987). Stibbe & Tennyson (1999) show that the LTE rate, which accounts for dissociation from higher vibrational levels, is about two orders of magnitude larger. Since we are interested in cooling of gas in early cosmological H ii regions, where the density is typically less than $\sim 1{\rm cm}^{-3}$ , we assume that the higher vibrational levels are not significantly populated and thus we use the $v=0$ rate in the present paper.

The density is plotted as a function of distance from the center, whereas the other three quantities are plotted as a function of enclosed gas mass. The dashed line in the density profile plot indicates a power law of $\propto r^{-2.4}$ . The horizontal dashed line in the temperature profile plot indicates the CMB temperature at $z=16$ . We also show the temperature profile of the primordial proto-star in Yoshida et al. (2006) for comparison (dot-dashed line). In the lower-left panel, we shift the HD fraction two decades upward to simplify comparison with H \textsubscript{2} fraction.

suspicious_text: This research utilizes an optical multimodal sensing methodology, whereby soft materials are exposed to laser light, generating a complex interference pattern known as a speckle pattern. The speckle pattern, highly sensitive to the scattering process, contains valuable information regarding material deformation. While speckle-based techniques have been applied in various sensing applications, including single physical signal sensing, deformation field measurement, and three-dimensional shape measurement, this study introduces a novel approach to multimodal sensing in soft materials, expanding the capabilities of speckle-based sensing methodologies.

suspicious_text: In our study, we assessed the characteristics of 127 linear galaxies, categorized as chain, double, and tadpole morphologies, along with other linear objects resembling edge-on spirals, reaching an I-band magnitude of 27 in the HST ACS Tadpole field. Additionally, we examined two clump clusters, potentially the face-on counterparts of the chain galaxies. These objects exhibit similar blue clumps, presumed massive star-forming regions, with masses as high as $10^{9}$ M \textsubscript{⊙} . Notably, the normal galaxies are distinguished by their centralized, red clumps, which are likely bulges, in contrast to the linear galaxies that exhibit few red clumps. Furthermore, the normal galaxies feature exponential brightness profiles, while the linears display flat profiles. The chain, double, and tadpole galaxies are predominant among edge-on galaxies within the field, with an apparent magnitude exceeding $\sim 24$ .

Notably, this mass scale is lower than the characteristic mass typically associated with galaxy formation, which relies on more efficient atomic cooling. As a result, feedback effects from the initial generation of stars are expected to significantly influence the conditions for subsequent galaxy formation. Recent theoretical studies suggest that these first stars were relatively massive, and their radiation would have had a substantial impact on the thermal and chemical evolution of the surrounding gas and intergalactic medium. source_text: The standard CDM model predicts that the first cosmological objects form very early in low-mass halos in which the primordial gas condenses via molecular hydrogen cooling (Couchman & Rees 1986; Tegmark et al. 1997; Yoshida et al. 2003). This mass scale is smaller than the often assumed characteristic mass for galaxy formation, for which more efficient atomic cooling is thought to be vital. Therefore, in the hierarchical model, feedback effects from the first generation of stars are expected to play a key role in setting the scene, i.e. the initial conditions , for (proto-)galaxy formation. Intriguingly, recent theoretical studies of the formation of the first stars (Abel, Bryan & Norman 2002; Omukai & Palla 2003; Yoshida et al. 2006) consistently indicate that these objects were rather massive. suspicious_text: While we can suggest modifications to the architecture, it is important to note that the causal relationships provided by the knowledge base do not accurately represent the actual operations within the black box. Consequently, we cannot confirm that the model's predicted output is based on the natural language explanation provided. Since there is no direct connection between the knowledge base and the black box, it becomes impossible to establish a link between the explanation and the output. Although the objective of avoiding human analyst involvement in explanation generation is achieved, as the model does generate a line of reasoning, the explanation itself is not accurate (or lacks precise provenance). This is because it explains what the black box should have learned, rather than what it actually learned.
source_text: However, we can propose some adjustments in this architecture: the causal links given by the KB do not directly reflect the operations that took place in the black box, and it is therefore impossible to affirm that the model predicted this output for the reasons given in the natural language explanation. Since nothing connects the KB and the black box, therefore it is impossible to link the explanation and the predicted output. The objective of not leaving explanation generation to human analysts is fulfilled, as the model formulates a line of reasoning, but the explanation given is not correct (or does not have accurate provenance), as it only explains what the black box should have learned, and not what it actually learned.

suspicious_text: High-performance models often face challenges in interpretability. Despite a growing body of research focused on explainable artificial intelligence (XAI) to clarify its objectives and expectations, no universal agreement exists on how to create or evaluate explanations. This paper explores why methods combining connectionist and symbolic approaches are the most effective for generating explanations for non-specialist users. We introduce a reasoning model, inspired by Doran et al. (2017), to elucidate the decision-making process of neural networks. This framework is utilized to address biases within the network's decision rationale. Additionally, we provide an illustrative example of its application, drawing on the image captioning technique from Burnse et al. (2018).

source_text: The nonlocal spin resistance is measured as a function of temperature in a Fe/GaAs spin-injection device. For nonannealed samples that show minority-spin injection, the spin resistance is observed up to room temperature and decays exponentially with temperature at a rate of 0.018 K \textsuperscript{-1} . Post-growth annealing at 440 K increases the spin signal at low temperatures, but the decay rate also increases to 0.030 K \textsuperscript{-1} . From measurements of the diffusion constant and the spin lifetime in the GaAs channel, we conclude that sample annealing modifies the temperature dependence of the spin transfer efficiency at injection and detection contacts. Surprisingly, the spin transfer efficiency increases in samples that exhibit minority-spin injection.

suspicious_text: The primary aim of this study is to devise a Finite Element methodology capable of addressing macroscopic mechanical systems while accounting for the effects of sub-scale heterogeneities. Our approach circumvents the necessity of explicitly resolving these heterogeneities within the Finite Element mesh. Instead, we derive analytical expressions that quantify the mechanical field perturbations induced by the presence of such heterogeneities, which are subsequently integrated into the enhanced Finite Element framework.
source_text: The objective of this work is to develop a Finite Element formulation for modelling the macroscopic mechanical problem that is enhanced to capture the influence of the underlying heterogeneities. In our approach, the Finite Element mesh is not required to explicitly resolve the heterogeneities. Closed-form expressions for the perturbation of the mechanical fields due to the presence of the heterogeneities are determined and these are then utilised to enhance the Finite Element formulation.

suspicious_text: In a manifold M, whether oriented or not, there exists a canonical principal Z2 bundle, known as the orientation bundle, denoted as ξM. The holonomy of the associated Z2-connection assigns -1 to loops that reverse orientation and +1 to those that do not change it. A closed manifold M, whether oriented or not, has a fundamental class residing in Hd(M,ZT), where ZT is a local system with fiber Z twisted by the orientation bundle. Its Pontryagin-dual is Hd(M,U(1)T), where U(1)T is a local system over M with fiber U(1) twisted by the orientation bundle.
source_text: Recall that a manifold $M$ , whether oriented or not, carries a canonical principal ${\mathbb{Z}}_{2}$ bundle $\xi_{M}$ called the orientation bundle of $M$ . The holonomy of the associated ${\mathbb{Z}}_{2}$ -connection assigns $-1$ to all closed loops which reverse orientation and assigns $+1$ to all closed loops which do not. A closed manifold $M$ , whether oriented or not, has the fundamental class living in $H_{d}(M,{\mathbb{Z}}^{T})$ , where ${\mathbb{Z}}^{T}$ is a local system with fiber ${\mathbb{Z}}$ twisted by the orientation bundle. Its Pontryagin-dual is $H^{d}(M,U(1)^{T})$ , where $U(1)^{T}$ is a local system over $M$ with fiber $U(1)$ twisted by the orientation bundle.

In the realm of condensed matter theory, a significant conundrum lies in the elucidation of symmetry-protected topological (SPT) phases of matter. These phases, characterized by a symmetry group G and an absence of long-range entanglement, constitute distinct equivalence classes, akin to the concepts of homotopy theory. The designation 'symmetry-protected' implies that these phases are differentiated based on their equivalence, rather than their intrinsic properties.

The classification of SPT phases for free fermionic systems, whether translationally invariant or not, has been substantially elucidated. However, the landscape becomes more intricate when dealing with interacting systems. In this discourse, we shall focus on the interacting scenario for the sake of simplicity, and for the avoidance of doubt, we will restrict our discussion to theories devoid of fermions. The SPT phases under consideration are resilient to disorder, as we do not assume translational invariance.

source_text: An important problem in condensed matter theory is to understand equivalence classes of gapped phases of matter with a symmetry group $G$ and no long-range entanglement. The equivalence is understood in the sense of homotopy theory. Such equivalence classes are called Symmetry Protected Topological (SPT) phases. The classification of free fermionic SPT phases with or without translational invariance is well understood by now [1] , but the situation with interacting systems is more complicated. We will be interested in the interacting case here and for simplicity will discuss only theories without fermions. We do not assume translational invariance, so the SPT phases we discuss are robust with respect to disorder.

This would allow to provide an explanation in natural language directly from the black box, emphasizing in the meantime the model’s reasoning errors and highlighting possible bias in the dataset or model. This is the option we propose pursuing as we believe it provides the most faithful explanation of how the model actually works.

suspicious_text: The impact of meta-galactic ionizing radiation on star formation in high-redshift galaxies is expected to be significant, with research suggesting that both global UV background radiation and local UV sources play a crucial role in regulating dwarf galaxy formation. Following global reionization, the formation of H2 molecules is substantially suppressed, limiting star formation to high-mass halos where gas cooling occurs through atomic processes. Moreover, even moderate-intensity photo-dissociating radiation can delay star formation in relic H ii regions for tens of millions of years, and the subsequent arrival of ionizing photons from global or local UV sources can further prolong this delay, highlighting the complex interplay between these factors in shaping the star formation process.

source_text: In the present paper, we study the formation and evolution of early cosmological H ii regions using three-dimensional cosmological radiation-hydrodynamics simulations. Unlike previous three-dimensional calculations of H ii regions that use either a static density field (Alvarez et al. 2006a) or do not follow the propagation of ionization fronts (O’Shea et al. 2005), we couple hydrodynamics with photo-ionization heating as well as radiative and chemical cooling self-consistently. We first locate a primordial star-forming cloud in a large cosmological simulation. We then carry out ray-tracing calculations of radiation transfer. We show that nearby low-density gas clumps are destroyed by a sweeping ionization front. The ionized gas first escapes out of the shallow potential wells of the dark halos with a velocity much larger than the virial velocity, but eventually falls back when the halos have grown large enough. We utilized viscous chromohydrodynamics, a framework derived from quantum chromodynamic kinetic theory and viscosity-modified distribution functions, to examine the polarization tensor and refractive index in viscous quark-gluon plasma (QGP). Although this approach may omit certain dynamical details inherent in the kinetic theory, the use of effective parameters in hydrodynamic formalisms can help mitigate discrepancies between kinetic theory and chromohydrodynamics. Despite its phenomenological nature, viscous chromohydrodynamics can still capture essential aspects of QGP physics, making it a valuable tool for investigating the viscous effects on QGP's color electromagnetic properties, which are challenging to study using microscopic kinetic theory descriptions.

suspicious_text: The paper is organized as follows: In Section II, we introduce the concept of randomized measurements and demonstrate its application in measuring the concurrence of pure multiparticle quantum states, as well as establishing a suitable lower bound for mixed states. In Section III, we address the statistical estimation of these quantities using finite samples of randomized measurements, with a particular focus on analyzing the statistical error through the evaluation of estimator variances. Sections IV and V respectively present numerical simulations of typical multiparticle entangled states and ensembles generated by random quantum circuits to illustrate the proposed protocols. Finally, we conclude our findings in Section VI and provide a brief outlook.
source_text: The paper is structured as follows: In Sec. II we introduce the paradigm of randomized measurements and show how it enables a measurement of the concurrence of pure multiparticle quantum states as well as of a suitable lower bound in the case of mixed states. Further on, in Sec. III we discuss the statistical estimation of the involved quantities based on finite samples of randomized measurements and, in particular, analyse the involved statistical error through evaluation of the variances of the respective estimators. In Sec. IV and V , respectively, we demonstrate the introduced protocols through numerical simulations of typical examples of multiparticle entangled states as well as of ensembles of states produced by random quantum circuits. Finally, we conclude our work in Sec. VI and give a brief outlook.

It is commonly assumed that prioritizing performance alone leads to increased model opacity, which is accurate in the sense that there exists a trade-off between model performance and transparency (Došilović et al., 2018). suspicious_text: In summary, since the pure state concurrence (7) and its associated lower bound (11) are straightforward functions of the second moments $\mathcal{R}^{(2)}_{A}$, they can be directly measured using Eq. (14) with $3^{N}$ distinct measurement configurations. However, this approach becomes increasingly impractical for systems involving tens of qubits due to the exponential growth in the number of required measurements ($3^{N}$). In such cases, it becomes more advantageous to employ randomized measurements to approximate the concurrence, albeit introducing a non-zero statistical error. The subsequent section will examine this statistical error and evaluate the scaling of both the number of measurement settings and the number of projective measurements per setting necessary to achieve a predefined level of precision.
source_text: In conclusion, as the pure state concurrence ( 7 ) and the corresponding lower bound ( 11 ) are simple functions of the second moments $\mathcal{R}^{(2)}_{A}$ , we can use Eq. ( 14 ) to measure them directly using $3^{N}$ measurement settings. Such a direct measurement, however, becomes impractical as soon as one reaches system sizes of several tens of qubits where, due to the exponential scaling $3^{N}$ , the required number measurements becomes too large. In the latter regime it can be favourable to estimate the concurrence approximately using randomized measurements at the expense of a non-zero statistical error. In the next section we will analyse this statistical error and discuss the scaling of the required number of measurement settings, as well as the required number of projective measurements per individual measurement setting, in order to reach an estimate with a predefined accuracy.

suspicious_text: Our radiative transfer calculations employ a two-stage approach, wherein photon arrival times are computed and assigned to surrounding gas particles for a given point radiation source. This involves defining a photon ray as a straight line connecting the source to a target particle and calculating local densities and smoothing lengths at multiple evaluation points along this path using a smoothed particle hydrodynamics (SPH) methodology. An ionization front is then advanced along the path, with the position of each subsequent evaluation point determined by the condition that the segment length between points is less than the local smoothing length, ensuring minimal density variation. The I-front crossing time between adjacent evaluation points is computed by integrating the jump condition for the I-front position, taking into account the photon emission rate and recombination effects, as described by equation (15).

$S_{3d}=\frac{1}{2}\int_{\partial M}a\delta b,$

suspicious_text: In a retrospective analysis, Adadi and Berrada (2018) introduced a categorization that distinguishes between methodologies. These methods are divided into two groups: those that analyze both the model's operational mechanics and its results, and those that solely focus on the outcomes. The former are termed model-based methods, which aim to elucidate the inner workings of the prediction process as a means of achieving transparency. Conversely, the latter are classified as post-hoc methods, where the explanation is generated independently of the model. Post-hoc explanations are derived from a secondary process that interprets the model's outputs without delving into the underlying decision-making mechanisms.
source_text: A distinction with more hindsight is made in Adadi and Berrada [2018] by separating methods that take into consideration both the process and the outcome of the model on the one hand, and those that focus only on the outcome, on the other hand. They use the term of model-based methods, which aims at understanding how the prediction process works as a transparency method, versus post-hoc methods. An explanation is considered as post-hoc if it is the produce of a separate method explaining the prediction produced by the black box while ignoring the decision process.

suspicious_text: During the initial glasma stage and the later stages of evolution near the critical temperature in the magnetic scenario of quark-gluon plasma (QGP), color-electric flux tubes with strong color-electric fields are present, suggesting that color electromagnetic properties significantly influence the evolution of hot and dense matter formed in heavy ion collisions. Furthermore, investigating these properties can provide insight into the QGP's response to external color currents, shedding light on its fundamental nature. Notably, research on the color electromagnetic properties of viscous QGP is limited, highlighting the need to explore the impact of viscosity on these properties and their subsequent effects on the evolution of matter in heavy ion collisions.
source_text: At the very early stage of the relativistic heavy ion collisions, named glasma stage (17) , and the late stage of the evolution process in the near $T_{c}$ region in the so-called magnetic scenario for the QGP (18) , there are color-electric flux tubes which contain strong color-electric fields in them. Therefore, the color electromagnetic properties may play an important role in the evolution of hot and dense matter produced in heavy ion collisions. In addition, color electromagnetic properties could reflect the response of QGP to external color current, so the study of them may be helpful for understanding the nature of QGP. However, to the best of our knowledge, the study on the color electromagnetic properties of viscous QGP is scare in literature. It makes sense to study how the viscosity affects them and how the viscous electromagnetic properties affect the evolution of produced matter in heavy ion collisions.

A random functional graph with a given degree sequence $\textbf{d}_{n}$ is constructed by randomly selecting a function $F$ from the set $\mathfrak{F}(\textbf{d}_{n})$, which consists of all functions $f$ from the set $[n]$ to itself such that the pre-image of each element $i$ in $[n]$ has a cardinality of $d_{n,i}$. This critical radius serves as a demarcation line between stable and unstable spinning exotic compact configurations with rotation parameter ${\bar{a}}$. Particularly, horizonless compact objects with rotation parameter ${\bar{a}}$ and reflecting surfaces that meet the condition $r_{\text{c}}<r^{\text{max}}_{\text{c}}({\bar{a}})$ are prone to instability due to scalar perturbation modes, whereas horizonless compact objects with reflecting surfaces that satisfy $r_{\text{c}}>r^{\text{max}}_{\text{c}}({\bar{a}})$ display stability.
source_text: It should be emphasized that the physical significance of the outermost (largest) radius $r^{\text{max}}_{\text{c}}({\bar{a}})\equiv\text{max}_{n}\{r_{\text{c}}({\bar{a}};n)\}$ stems from the fact that, for a given value of the dimensionless rotation parameter ${\bar{a}}$ , this unique critical radius marks the boundary between stable and unstable spinning exotic compact configurations. In particular, horizonless compact objects of rotation parameter ${\bar{a}}$ whose reflecting surfaces are characterized by the inequality $r_{\text{c}}<r^{\text{max}}_{\text{c}}({\bar{a}})$ are superradiantly unstable to scalar perturbation modes, whereas horizonless compact objects whose reflecting surfaces are characterized by the inequality $r_{\text{c}}>r^{\text{max}}_{\text{c}}({\bar{a}})$ are stable.

source_text: QNMs are single frequency modes dominating the time evolution of perturbations of systems which are subject to damping, either by internal dissipation or by radiating away energy. Due to the damping, the frequency of a QNM must be complex, its imaginary part being inversely proportional to the typical damping time. Recall that, in general relativity, damping occurs even without friction, since energy may be radiated away towards infinity by gravitational waves (1) . Thus, they could lead to the direct identification of the existence of the BH through gravitational wave observation, which might be realized in the near future. QNMs, which are believed to be characteristic sounds of perturbed black hole (BH) spacetimes have been investigated for a long time and their physical properties have been presented in various studies (see for example (3) ). It is worth noting that similar to the BH geometries, QNMs can also arise from other spacetimes including the wormholes (33) .

suspicious_text: In this context, the movement of a particle is influenced by a constant drift velocity, denoted as $\mu$, which is determined by the initial potential $U_{0}$ and the friction coefficient $\zeta$. The direction of the drift depends on the nature of the potential: attractive potentials cause the particle to move towards the origin, while repulsive potentials drive it away. The introduction of resetting, characterized by a rate $r$, alters the probability distribution of the particle's position by transferring probability from any given point $x$ to a specific reset point $x_{r}$. This resetting process is reflected in the additional terms of the equation, which are directly proportional to the resetting rate $r$, effectively modifying the standard Fokker-Planck equation to account for the loss and gain of probability due to resetting.
source_text: where $\delta(x-x_{r})$ is a Dirac delta function. Here $\mu\coloneqq U_{0}\zeta^{-1}$ is the constant drift velocity, $\zeta$ being the friction coefficient. Since $\zeta$ is always positive, the drift acts towards the origin ( $\mu>0$ ) when the potential is attractive and away from the origin ( $\mu<0$ ) when the potential is repulsive [see Fig. 1 (b),(c)]. Note that when $r=0$ , Eq. ( 1 ) boils down to the Fokker-Planck equation for the underlying process without resetting. Once resetting is introduced, i.e., for $r>0$ , there is a loss of probability from position $x$ and a subsequent gain of probability at position $x_{r}$ . The last two terms on the right hand side of Eq. ( 1 ) account for this additional probability flow, which is proportional to $r$ , the rate of resetting.

source_text: An exclusion from this rule is the appearance of internal stagnation points —fluid particles with zero velocity relative to the steady flow—and critical layers . By a critical layer we mean a connected part of the fluid domain consisting only of closed streamlines. The existence of such waves within linear theory was established in [13] , where the streamlines and particle paths of waves with constant vorticity were studied. For irrotational flows such behavior is not encountered, cf. [3] . Also earlier studies had indicated that internal stagnation should be possible (see, e.g., [16] ), but it was first with [26] that waves with critical layers were given a firm mathematical foundation (see also [9] ). A different approach in dealing with the free boundary allowed for including internal stagnation, a phenomenon which for technical reasons had not been possible to see with earlier methods for rotational waves (cf., e.g., [7] ).

source_text: We investigate the effects of self-interacting dark matter (SIDM) on the tidal stripping and evaporation of satellite galaxies in a Milky Way-like host. We use a suite of five zoom-in, dark-matter-only simulations, two with velocity-independent SIDM cross-sections, two with velocity-dependent SIDM cross-sections, and one cold dark matter (CDM) simulation for comparison. After carefully assigning stellar mass to satellites at infall, we find that stars are stripped at a higher rate in SIDM than in CDM. In contrast, the total bound dark matter mass-loss rate is minimally affected, with subhalo evaporation having negligible effects on satellites for viable SIDM models. Centrally located stars in SIDM haloes disperse out to larger radii as cores grow. Consequently, the half-light radius of satellites increases, stars become more vulnerable to tidal stripping, and the stellar mass function is suppressed. suspicious_text: The study of steady water-wave motion concerns two-dimensional surface waves that propagate as wave-trains, maintaining a consistent form and velocity. Mathematical theories confirm the existence of such steady waves, which can be categorized into infinite or finite depth configurations, as well as gravitational and capillary types. Additionally, these waves may exhibit vorticity or remain irrotational. Historically, research has predominantly focused on irrotational waves, characterized by the absence of vorticity. A significant subset within this category is the Stokes wave: periodic gravity waves featuring a single crest per period, symmetrically arranged around it. Of particular mathematical intrigue is the wave of maximal height, distinguished by its sharp crest with an interior angle of 120 degrees. For comprehensive insights into this theory, refer to [21], while [14] offers an overview of the broader water-wave problem.
source_text: The periodic steady water-wave problem describes wave-trains of two-dimensional surface water waves propagating with constant shape and velocity. The mathematical existence of such steady water waves is well-established, and one distinguishes between infinite and finite depth, gravitational and capillary waves, and flows with and without vorticity. Historically, the main focus has been on irrotational waves, i.e. on flows without vorticity. An important class of such waves is given by the Stokes waves : periodic gravity-waves with a single crest in each period around which the wave is symmetric. Of particular mathematical interest is the so-called wave of greatest height with a sharp crest of interior angle $120$ degrees. The paper [21] describes much of that theory; see also [14] for a general overview of the water-wave problem.

suspicious_text: When the parameter \( c \) is much less than 1, the system forms an orientationally disordered crystal, also referred to as a plastic solid, below a specific temperature \( T \). Upon further cooling, the system undergoes an orientational phase transition. In this study, small isotropic impurities are introduced, as defined in Eq. (9). Due to the small size ratio \( \sigma_{2}/\sigma_{1} \), both the positions and orientations of the particles can become highly disordered, leading to the formation of a double glass at low temperatures. However, when the size ratio is close to unity, increasing \( c \) results in orientational glass at low \( T \) (18).
source_text: If $c\ll 1$ , our system forms an orientationally disordered crystal (plastic solid) below a certain $T$ . It then undergoes an orientational phase transition with further lowering $T$ . In this paper, we add small isotropic impurities as specified in Eq.(9). Since the size ratio $\sigma_{2}/\sigma_{1}$ is rather small, the positions can be highly disordered as well as the orientations, resulting in double glass at low $T$ . If the size ratio is close to unity, we obtain orientational glass at low $T$ with increasing $c$ (18) .

 $R_{K}=\frac{h}{e^{2}}\,.$  (1) 
 This constant plays a fundamental role in the QHE: The Hall conductance of a two-dimensional incompressible electron gas (2DEG) exhibiting the QHE turns out to be an integral or rational multiple of $R_{K}^{-1}$ . Its significance for metrology is clearly an important aspect of the QHE. Apart from that, the QHE is a fascinating phenomenon, because its theoretical description is related to quite fundamental and abstract concepts in mathematics and theoretical physics, such as fractional- or braid statistics, tensor categories, knot theory, 2D conformal field theory (CFT), and 3D topological field theory (TFT); see Fig. 1 . source_text: Extending the trend of diminishing enhancement of stripping towards higher halo masses in the v-d models, there should be little difference between SIDM and CDM for haloes above $10^{10}\,\mathrm{M_{{\sun}}}$ . Indeed, by matching individual haloes to each other via identifying common particle IDs between simulations, we find no significant difference in stripping in the few haloes that follow similar orbits. For SIDM1, the opposite effect of enhanced stripping increasing towards higher masses exists. We therefore expect some difference to exist for haloes above $10^{10}\,\mathrm{M_{{\sun}}}$ , but the SIDM effect is sub-dominant to the more critical orbital parameters and infall time. By matching large subhaloes to each other in SIDM1 and CDM, we could not isolate a clear SIDM related effect. The implications of stellar stripping in these largest systems on the stellar halo are discussed in Section 7.3 .

source_text: Quantum chromodynamics (QCD) predicts that deconfined phase transition will take place at high temperature and/or high density, as a result the nuclear matter will undergo a transition to quark-gluon plasma (QGP). One main goal for Relativistic Heavy Ion Collider (RHIC) and Large Hadronic Collider (LHC) is to seek this new state of matter. There are two striking findings at RHIC. One is that the deconfined hot medium behaviors as a nearly perfect fluid with a small viscosity (1) . Several groups have applied viscous hydrodynamics to simulate the evolution of the produced matter in heavy ion collisions. The simulations successfully fit the observables at RHIC, such as the elliptic flow, the particle spectra, etc (5) . The other is the strong jet quenching, which is believed to be a potential signal for the QGP formation (14) . It should be stressed that the first LHC results also strongly support similar conclusions as seen at RHIC (15) .

To analyze systems at low densities, the virial expansion offers a convenient framework. In this approach, various thermodynamic quantities are expressed as power series in terms of the fugacity parameter \( z = e^{\beta \mu} \), where \( T = 1/\beta \) represents the temperature and \( \mu \) is the chemical potential. The particle density \( n \) and pressure \( P \) can be written as:

\[
n = \frac{1}{\lambdabar^{3}} \left( b_{1}z + 2b_{2}z^{2} + 3b_{3}z^{3} + \cdots \right),
\]

\[
P = \frac{T}{\lambdabar^{3}} \left( b_{1}z + b_{2}z^{2} + b_{3}z^{3} + \cdots \right).
\]

This formulation provides a systematic way to express thermodynamic properties in terms of fugacity expansions, as shown in equations (1).

source_text: A few years ago the authors of [7] proved the existence of water waves with a general vorticity distribution (for a different approach, cf. [20] ). This opened the way, not only for a new branch of research, but also for the existence of new types of waves. In many aspects, the features of rotational waves resemble those of irrotational waves: results on symmetry [4] , regularity [6] , and waves of greatest height [23] have now been extended from irrotational waves to waves with a general vorticity distribution. In most of those cases the differences have manifested themselves as technical difficulties, not different properties per se .

suspicious_text: The significance of interpretability in AI models is widely acknowledged. Nevertheless, there is no universal agreement on the best methods to assess interpretation techniques. A common distinction is made between transparency-based methods, which focus on explaining the model's internal processes, and post-hoc methods, which analyze the model after it has made a prediction. As defined by the Oxford dictionary, post-hoc reasoning involves analyzing an event after it has occurred, often incorrectly assuming a logical connection between the event and the outcome. This contrasts with methods that seek transparency, which aim to provide a clear, direct explanation of the model itself.

suspicious_text: In the SIDM1 model, differences emerge in the stellar density and velocity dispersion profiles, primarily caused by perturbations to satellite orbits induced by a core in the host halo. These perturbations are clearly visible in Fig. 3. The most massive satellites in SIDM1 exhibit significant cores, leading to enhanced stripping compared to CDM for the same orbital parameters. Additionally, these satellites generate tidal streams with higher velocity dispersion perpendicular to the orbital plane, as shown in Errani et al. (2015) for cored satellites. However, the global characteristics of the stellar halo are predominantly shaped by the specific orbits of the most massive satellites. Without detailed knowledge of these orbits, it is challenging to isolate effects directly tied to SIDM. On smaller mass scales, SIDM1 does not produce cores as large as those in the v-d models, resulting in less pronounced features compared to the vdSIDMb model, as illustrated in Fig. 18.

source_text: In conclusion, as the pure state concurrence ( 7 ) and the corresponding lower bound ( 11 ) are simple functions of the second moments $\mathcal{R}^{(2)}_{A}$ , we can use Eq. ( 14 ) to measure them directly using $3^{N}$ measurement settings. Such a direct measurement, however, becomes impractical as soon as one reaches system sizes of several tens of qubits where, due to the exponential scaling $3^{N}$ , the required number measurements becomes too large. In the latter regime it can be favourable to estimate the concurrence approximately using randomized measurements at the expense of a non-zero statistical error. In the next section we will analyse this statistical error and discuss the scaling of the required number of measurement settings, as well as the required number of projective measurements per individual measurement setting, in order to reach an estimate with a predefined accuracy.

source_text: For $d=3$ no non-trivial bosonic SPT phase with ${\mathbb{Z}}_{2}^{T}$ symmetry is possible, because $w_{1}w_{2}=w_{3}=w_{1}^{3}=0$ for all closed 3-manifolds, or equivalently, because any closed 3-manifold is a boundary of some compact 4-manifold. This also agrees with the group cohomology classification [2] .

source_text: The rate of the charge exchange reaction ${\rm H}_{2}+{\rm H}^{+}\rightarrow{\rm H}_{2}^{+}+{\rm H},$ was recently revised by Savin et al. (2004a,b). We use this updated rate because these processes become important when the ionization fraction of the gas is large. Another important process is collisional dissociation of H \textsubscript{2} by electron impacts The commonly used values are for H \textsubscript{2} molecules which are initially in the $v=0$ level (e.g. Shapiro & Kang 1987). Stibbe & Tennyson (1999) show that the LTE rate, which accounts for dissociation from higher vibrational levels, is about two orders of magnitude larger. Since we are interested in cooling of gas in early cosmological H ii regions, where the density is typically less than $\sim 1{\rm cm}^{-3}$ , we assume that the higher vibrational levels are not significantly populated and thus we use the $v=0$ rate in the present paper.

source_text: We derive two prerequisites that are necessary to create a truly reasoning AI: i) The KB must inherently emerge from the black box model in order to conceptually (symbolically) reflect what the model learned. ii) The symbolic part must constraint the connectionist part to improve the prediction performance of the model.

We base our investigation on a variety of theoretical models that predict the properties of both quark matter and hadronic matter. To refine our understanding, we estimate the surface tension at the quark-hadron interface, $\Sigma$, by employing the multiple reflection expansion method and equivparticle model.

source_text: We investigate systematically the quark-hadron mixed phase in dense stellar matter, and its influence on compact star structures. The properties of quark matter and hadronic matter are fixed based on various model predictions. Beside adopting constant values, the surface tension $\Sigma$ for the quark-hadron interface is estimated with the multiple reflection expansion method and equivparticle model. To fix the structures of quark-hadron pasta phases, a continuous dimensionality of the structure is adopted as proposed by Ravenhall, Pethick, and Wilson. The corresponding properties of hybrid stars are then obtained and confronted with pulsar observations. It is found that the correlation between radius and tidal deformability in traditional neutron stars preserves in hybrid stars. For those permitted by pulsar observations, in almost all cases the quark phase persists inside the most massive compact stars. To achieve an accurate assessment of common-mode noise, it is essential that the baseline levels for each channel are uniform. However, the initial baseline levels for the individual channels may exhibit some disparity. To address this discrepancy, we utilize coarse 4-bit digital-to-analog converters (DACs) incorporated in the resistor networks $R_{\rm 1}$ and $R_{\rm 1}^{\prime}$ , as well as fine 5-bit DACs comprising a resistor and a variable current source situated at the rear ends of the rapid and slow shapers. source_text: We find that a large volume of a few kiloparsec diameter is ionized by a single massive star, within which a smaller He iii region is embedded. The ionized hot gas is evacuated at a velocity of $\sim 30$ km/sec. After the central star dies off, the gas recombines, and cools first by atomic line cooling, then by H \textsubscript{2} cooling, and finally by HD line cooling down to a few tens Kelvin. The fractionation of HD/H \textsubscript{2} occurs when the gas temperature becomes as low as 100 K, and the HD fraction reaches $10^{-5}$ . Run-away collapse is triggered when the cloud mass exceeds $\sim 40M_{\odot}$ at the temperature minimum of $\sim 40-50$ K. It is substantially smaller than the corresponding mass scale for the first generation stars, $\sim 300M_{\odot}$ (Yoshida et al. 2006), indicating that second-generation stars formed under these conditions likely have smaller masses than the first stars. Recall that $F$ denotes a function drawn uniformly at random from the set $\mathfrak{F}(\textbf{d}_{n})$ defined in ( 1 ). Note that $J_{n,k}(v)$ corresponds to the set of all possible non-self-intersecting $k$ -paths starting at $v$ . Thus, we have 
 
 $\displaystyle\mathbb{P}(\mathfrak{s}_{n}(v)>k)=\sum_{J\in J_{n,k}(v)}\mathbb{P}\left(\left(F(v),\ldots,F^{(k)}(v)\right)=J\right).$ 
 The probability on the right hand side can be derived by counting the functions in $\mathfrak{F}(\textbf{d}_{n})$ that lead to the path $J$ . Since $J$ determines the images of exactly $k$ elements to be $i_{1},\ldots,i_{k}$ , there are 
 
 $\displaystyle\frac{(n-k)!}{\prod_{\ell\notin\{i_{1},\ldots,i_{k}\}}d_{\ell}!\prod_{j=1}^{k}(d_{i_{j}}-1)!}$ 
 possible ways to choose the remaining images. The assertion follows after dividing by the total number $n!/\prod_{\ell=1}^{n}d_{\ell}!$ of elements in $\mathfrak{F}(\textbf{d}_{n})$ . ∎

The six-length of a vertex in a functional graph is defined as follows: Let $f:V\rightarrow V$ be a function and let $id$ denote the identity function on $V$ . Let ${f}^{k}$ denote the $k$ -times composition of $f$ , that is ${f}^{0}=id$ and ${f}^{k}={f}^{k-1}\circ f$ for $k\geq 1$ . The six-length of $v\in V$ is defined as 
 
 $\mathfrak{s}_{f}(v)=\min\left\{k\in{\mathbb{N}}:{f}^{k}(v)\in\{{f}^{j}(v):0\leq j\leq k-1\}\right\}.$ 
 An example for the six-length in a functional graph is given in Fig.1(a) . Note that $\mathfrak{s}_{f}(v)$ can be decomposed into the tail-length $\mathfrak{t}_{f}(v)$ and the cycle-length $\mathfrak{c}_{f}(v)$ as indicated in Fig.1(b) . More formally, the tail-length is the unique integer that satisfies 
 
 $\displaystyle\mathfrak{t}_{f}(v)<\mathfrak{s}_{f}(v)\quad\text{ and }\quad{f}^{\mathfrak{s}_{f}(v)}(v)={f}^{\mathfrak{t}_{f}(v)}(v),$ 
 and the cycle-length is given by $\mathfrak{c}_{f}(v):=\mathfrak{s}_{f}(v)-\mathfrak{t}_{f}(v)$ .

source_text: Models combining connectionism and symbolism are not widely represented in the state of the art of XAI. These paradigms are rarely combined when providing explanations. The use of a symbolic basis with a neural network can provide explanations close to the functioning of human reasoning while maintaining the state-of-the-art performance at the same time. We build upon Doranet al.[2017] and extend Burnset al.[2018] to further characterize what a neural-symbolic explainable model could output. We propose a model endowed with a non-external KB, i.e., directly built on the learning data of a neural network, that allows to influence its learning and to correct bias thoroughly, while giving a fair explanation from its predictions. As the user or expert external knowledge does not interfere the predictions in the explanation process, it constitutes a truly explainable model that is faithful to communicate the reasoning behind its output decisions. Let $r\geq 3$ and $d=rp+1,p\geq 1$ . Then by Proposition 4.6 , we have $g(E_{0})=0$ and $b=1$ . According to Theorem 4.2 , we have $\alpha=d/\gcd(r,d)=d$ and $b^{\prime}=r/\gcd(r,d)=r$ , so $\alpha=b^{\prime}p+1;$ since $0<\beta<\alpha$ is chosen so that $b^{\prime}\beta\equiv-1\,\text{\rm mod}\,\alpha$ , we have $\beta=p$ . In addition, 
 
 $\alpha/\beta=(rp+1)/p,$ 
 so considering the continued fraction decomposition, we have 
 
 $\lambda=p,\qquad n_{1}=r+1,\qquad n_{2}=n_{3}=\cdots=n_{\lambda}=2.$

suspicious_text: This study examines the impact of stochastic resetting on the first-passage properties of diffusion in a spatially-dependent environment with a constant bias. Using an analytically solvable model, where a particle diffuses in a linear potential with a spatially-varying diffusion coefficient and undergoes stochastic resetting at a constant rate, we derive an exact expression for the survival probability and first-passage time distribution. Our analysis reveals a series of dynamical transitions controlled by a single parameter that captures the interplay between drift and diffusion, resulting in distinct behaviors depending on the potential's strength and direction. Notably, resetting accelerates or hinders first-passage to the origin depending on the parameter's value, with a critical transition occurring at a specific threshold. This research provides a foundation for future theoretical and experimental investigations into the effects of stochastic resetting on inhomogeneous diffusion
source_text: We explore the effect of stochastic resetting on the first-passage properties of space-dependent diffusion in presence of a constant bias. In our analytically tractable model system, a particle diffusing in a linear potential $U(x)\propto\mu|x|$ with a spatially varying diffusion coefficient $D(x)=D_{0}|x|$ undergoes stochastic resetting, i.e., returns to its initial position $x_{0}$ at random intervals of time, with a constant rate $r$ . Considering an absorbing boundary placed at $x_{a}<x_{0}$ , we first derive an exact expression of the survival probability of the diffusing particle in the Laplace space and then explore its first-passage to the origin as a limiting case of that general result. In the limit $x_{a}\to 0$ , we derive an exact analytic expression for the first-passage time distribution of the underlying process. Once resetting is introduced, the system is observed to exhibit a series of dynamical transitions in terms of a sole parameter, $\nu\coloneqq(1+\mu D_{0}^{-1})$

suspicious_text: To improve the traditional Discriminative Correlation Filters (DCF), several methods have been proposed to expand the search area. However, these methods introduce unwanted noise due to the excessive background data. The model can then learn from the surrounding environment more than the target object, leading to potential errors in the detection process. To tackle this issue, this study presents an innovative solution called Aberrance Repressed Correlation Filter (ARCF). By imposing a constraint on the rate of change of response maps during the detection phase, the ARCF tracker effectively mitigates aberrances, making it more reliable and accurate for object tracking. Experiments were conducted on various UAV datasets such as UAV123, UAVDT, and DTB70, using 243 challenging image sequences with over 90 thousand frames to assess the ARCF tracker's performance. suspicious_text: The study of quasinormal modes (QNMs) in curved spacetime has consistently garnered significant interest due to the inherent complexity of solving the associated wave equations analytically. To address this challenge, researchers have devised numerous computational methods. Among these, artificial neural networks (ANNs) have emerged as a powerful tool for solving differential equations arising in various physical contexts. The feedforward neural network (FNNM), as the foundational architecture of ANNs, processes information in a unidirectional manner, progressing from input nodes through hidden layers (if present) to the output nodes without forming any cycles or feedback loops. This architecture, inspired by biological neural systems found in animal brains, has demonstrated versatility across diverse applications, including those in general relativity and cosmology. The utility of FNNM in solving differential equations can be attributed to several key advantages.

suspicious_text: The SIDM framework addresses the "too big to fail" issue, as identified by Boylan-Kolchin et al. (2011), which highlights the discrepancy between high-density subhaloes in Milky Way simulations and the lack of observed counterparts. These subhaloes, if they existed as modeled, would be massive enough to form stars, yet no corresponding visible structures have been detected. Specifically, their circular velocity profiles are inconsistent with the kinematics of known satellite galaxies. This problem persists for both the most massive haloes at $z=0$, first noted in Read et al. (2006), and those at infall and pre-reionization stages. For certain cross-sections, SIDM resolves this issue by reducing the circular velocity profiles of all haloes, as demonstrated by Vogelsberger et al. (2012).

source_text: The rate of the charge exchange reaction ${\rm H}_{2}+{\rm H}^{+}\rightarrow{\rm H}_{2}^{+}+{\rm H},$ was recently revised by Savin et al. (2004a,b). We use this updated rate because these processes become important when the ionization fraction of the gas is large. Another important process is collisional dissociation of H \textsubscript{2} by electron impacts The commonly used values are for H \textsubscript{2} molecules which are initially in the $v=0$ level (e.g. Shapiro & Kang 1987). Stibbe & Tennyson (1999) show that the LTE rate, which accounts for dissociation from higher vibrational levels, is about two orders of magnitude larger. Since we are interested in cooling of gas in early cosmological H ii regions, where the density is typically less than $\sim 1{\rm cm}^{-3}$ , we assume that the higher vibrational levels are not significantly populated and thus we use the $v=0$ rate in the present paper.

source_text: In summary, we have investigated the role of the bulk-brane energy exchange on the evolution of a brane universe. Due to the energy flow between the bulk and the brane, the standard energy conservation is broken. Parameterizing the energy exchange term by the scale factor and Hubble parameter, we have derived the effective cosmological equations in the limit of low energy density on the brane. Compared to the usual Friedmann equation, we have got an additional modified term due to the brane-bulk energy exchange. source_text: We are only interested in the finite type cluster algebras. Such cluster algebras have a finite set of distinct generators (cluster variables), that is grouped in a finite number of clusters (sets) of equal size and that relate to each other by exchange relations where one of the cluster variables is replaced by (mutates to) another cluster variable outside the cluster. These exchange relations can be codified within each cluster by associating them with oriented quivers. From the quiver associated to a cluster we can define $\mathcal{X}$ -coordinates related to each cluster variable in that cluster.

The results are compared with 20 state-of-the-art trackers with both hand-crafted based trackers and deep-based trackers, i.e., KCF [13] , DSST [8] , SAMF [18] , MUSTER [14] , BACF [15] , SRDCF [7] , STAPLE_CA [21] , MCCT-H [27] , STRCF [16] , ECO-HC (with gray-scale) [5] , ECO [5] , C-COT [9] , HCF [19] , ADNet [29] , CFNet [25] , CREST [23] , MCPF [30] , SINT [24] , SiamFC [1] , and HDT [22] . All evaluation criteria are according to the original protocol defined in three benchmarks respectively [20] .

Specifically, as the ADCK frequency is increased, resulting in a shorter $T_{\rm ADC}$, we observe a reduction in the ENC value. This choice may potentially improve the overall data quality by reducing the impact of external noise on the HOLD signal.
source_text: We plot measurements of the equivalent noise charge (ENC) for the slow shapers of the TT02 in Figure 11 . We find that the ENC value depends on the amount of input charge, which may be related to the ADC time period $T_{\rm ADC}$ because shortening it by increasing the ADCK frequency gives lower ENC values, where the gradient of the RAMP signal is adjusted to keep the same ADC value with different ADCK frequencies. This might indicate that the $T_{\rm ADC}$ -dependence is caused by an accumulation of external noise in the HOLD signal during the $T_{\rm ADC}$ period. To minimize this feature, we adopt a 60-MHz ADCK when obtaining energy spectra.

suspicious_text: The SIDM framework addresses the "too big to fail" issue, as identified by Boylan-Kolchin et al. (2011), which highlights the discrepancy between high-density subhaloes in Milky Way simulations and the lack of observed counterparts. These subhaloes, if they existed as modeled, would be massive enough to form stars, yet no corresponding visible structures have been detected. Specifically, their circular velocity profiles are inconsistent with the kinematics of known satellite galaxies. This problem persists for both the most massive haloes at $z=0$, first noted in Read et al. (2006), and those at infall and pre-reionization stages. For certain cross-sections, SIDM resolves this issue by reducing the circular velocity profiles of all haloes, as demonstrated by Vogelsberger et al. (2012).
source_text: SIDM also provides a solution to the so-called too big to fail problem Boylan-Kolchin et al. (2011) , which refers to a population of high-density subhaloes in Milky Way-like simulations with no observed analogues. If such haloes did exist as simulated, they would be too big to fail to form stars, and as such should have visible counterparts. More specifically, these haloes have a circular velocity profile which is too large to match the kinematics of known satellites. The problem exists for both the largest haloes at $z=0$ , initially pointed out in Read et al. (2006) , and the largest haloes at infall and before reionization. For certain cross-sections, SIDM offers a solution to this issue by reducing the circular velocity profile of all haloes Vogelsberger et al. (2012) .

suspicious_text: The Adiabatic Quantum Computation (AQC) process commences with the easily preparable ground state of an initial Hamiltonian, which is then gradually transformed into the target Hamiltonian through a time-dependent Hamiltonian. This time-dependent Hamiltonian, defined over a time interval, enables a continuous interpolation between the initial and final Hamiltonians, allowing the system to evolve towards the desired solution. The interpolation is achieved through a linear or non-linear schedule, governed by continuous functions that satisfy specific boundary conditions, ensuring a smooth transition between the initial and final states. The time evolution of the system is described by the Schrödinger equation, with the initial condition set as the ground state of the initial Hamiltonian, and the goal is to obtain the ground state of the target Hamiltonian with high probability.

However, in many cases, only a limited number of microscopic interaction characteristics are significant. Systems that share these same characteristics exhibit identical macroscopic behavior, indicating a degree of universality. When the density and temperature are such that the thermal de Broglie wavelength $\lambda$ is much greater than the interaction range $R$, the specifics of the interaction potential become largely inconsequential, and the thermodynamics are governed by the two-body scattering length $a$, with corrections of order $\lambda/R$. To address this issue, inspired by the work of Burnset al.[2018], we propose that employing loss functions with more tangible and interpretable meanings could enhance the ability to provide clear explanations, as opposed to relying on less intuitive metrics like cross-entropy. In their study, Burnset al.[2018] developed two innovative loss functions: the "Appearance Confusion Loss" and the "Confident Loss," aimed at mitigating gender bias in image captioning. The Appearance Confusion Loss operates under the principle that, for an image lacking explicit gender cues, the likelihood of predicting "man" or "woman" should be balanced. As the quark matter (phase I) emerges and transitions into a droplet phase, the dimensionality decreases from 3 to 1 with increasing density. Later, the phases switch, and the dimensionality increases from 1 to 3. In cases where the dimensionality lies between 1 and 3, the radius varies smoothly, while sudden variations are observed during the transition to 1 or 3.

The variation of quark-hadron pasta phases resembles the evolution of intermediate structures found in quantum molecular dynamics (QMD) simulations and fully three-dimensional calculations. suspicious_text: Glass exhibits significant orientational and positional variations at mesoscopic scales. These heterogeneous patterns have been observed in multiple model systems (24). Initially, a mesoscopic heterogeneity emerges in flip motions, which correlates with impurity clustering. Subsequently, changes in positional configurations induce non-flip rotations of ellipses. These rotations are responsible for the long-term decay of rotational correlation functions $G_{\ell}(t)$ for even $\ell$ (10). Furthermore, this dynamic heterogeneity in non-flip rotations is associated with dynamic heterogeneity in displacements or bond breakage (27).
source_text: Discriminative correlation filter based framework has been broadly applied to visual tracking since it was first introduced by Bolme et al . [2] who proposed a method called minimum output sum of squared error (MOSSE) filter. Kernel trick was introduced to DCF framework by Henriques et al . [13] to achieve better performance. Introduction of scale estimation has further improved the framework [18] . Context and background information are also exploited to have more negative samples so that learned correlation filters can have more discriminative power [21] . Besides hand-crafted features used in [18] , the application of deep features is also investigated for more precise and comprehensive object appearance representation [19] . Some trackers combine hand-crafted features with deep ones to better describe the tracked objects from multiple aspects [16] . DCF based trackers have achieved state-of-the-art performance in multiple datasets specified for UAV object tracking [10] .

suspicious_text: In a manifold M, whether oriented or not, there exists a canonical principal Z2 bundle, known as the orientation bundle, denoted as ξM. The holonomy of the associated Z2-connection assigns -1 to loops that reverse orientation and +1 to those that do not change it. A closed manifold M, whether oriented or not, has a fundamental class residing in Hd(M,ZT), where ZT is a local system with fiber Z twisted by the orientation bundle. Its Pontryagin-dual is Hd(M,U(1)T), where U(1)T is a local system over M with fiber U(1) twisted by the orientation bundle.

suspicious_text: A graph $H$ is considered an $n$-extension of a functional graph $G=(V^{\prime},E^{\prime})$, where $V^{\prime}$ is a subset of $[n]$, if it is constructed through a specific iterative process. The process begins with the initial vertex and edge sets $V_{0}=V^{\prime}$ and $E_{0}=E^{\prime}$. At each step $i$, the smallest element $w$ not in $V_{i}$ is selected from $[n]$, and with probability $1/(|E_{i}|+1)$, $w$ is added to the graph as an isolated vertex with a self-loop, updating $V_{i}$ and $E_{i}$ accordingly. Otherwise, an edge $xy$ is chosen uniformly at random from $E_{i}$, and $w$ is connected to $x$ and $y$ while removing the edge $xy$, thereby updating $V_{i}$ and $E_{i}$. This process continues until all elements of $[n]$ are included in $V_{i}$, at which point the resulting graph $H=(V_{i},E_{i})$ is defined as the $n$-extension of $G$.

suspicious_text: The distance between the final state of $|\psi(\Delta t)\rangle$ and the initial state $|\phi(\Delta t)\rangle$, up to a time-dependent phase factor, is denoted as $d(\Delta t,\beta(t))$ and is defined as the norm of the difference between these two states. By setting $\beta(t)=0$, we can relate this distance back to the initial state $|\phi_{0}\rangle$, and we find that the distance between the initial state and any allowable state in the Hilbert space is bounded above by 2. The time at which this bound is achieved is denoted as $\Delta t_{\forall}$, and from this, we derive the inequality $2\hbar\leq\int^{\Delta t_{\forall}}_{0}{\left\|H(\tau)|\phi_{0}\rangle\right\|}d\tau$, which represents a fundamental limit on the evolution of the system.

source_text: The standard CDM model predicts that the first cosmological objects form very early in low-mass halos in which the primordial gas condenses via molecular hydrogen cooling (Couchman & Rees 1986; Tegmark et al. 1997; Yoshida et al. 2003). This mass scale is smaller than the often assumed characteristic mass for galaxy formation, for which more efficient atomic cooling is thought to be vital. Therefore, in the hierarchical model, feedback effects from the first generation of stars are expected to play a key role in setting the scene, i.e. the initial conditions , for (proto-)galaxy formation. Intriguingly, recent theoretical studies of the formation of the first stars (Abel, Bryan & Norman 2002; Omukai & Palla 2003; Yoshida et al. 2006) consistently indicate that these objects were rather massive. suspicious_text: In a proposed framework, bosonic topological phase transitions in a space-time dimension of d are categorized by the dth cohomology group of $BG_{0}$, with U(1) coefficients [2]. Here, $G_{0}$, a finite symmetry group, acts only internally and trivially on space-time, and $BG_{0}$ represents its classifying space. $BG_{0}$ serves to classify principal $G_{0}$ bundles, with the isomorphism classes of such bundles on any space X correlating with the homotopy classes of maps from X to $BG_{0}$. This cohomology of $BG_{0}$ is also referred to as the group cohomology of $G_{0}$.
source_text: It has been proposed that bosonic SPT phases in space-time dimension $d$ are classified by the degree- $d$ cohomology group of $BG_{0}$ with $U(1)$ coefficients [2] . Here $G_{0}$ is a finite symmetry group \footnote{ 1 A version suitable for compact Lie group symmetries has also been proposed in [2] , but we will limit ourselves to finite groups here.} which is purely internal, i.e. acts trivially on space-time, and $BG_{0}$ is its classifying space. The space $BG_{0}$ classifies $G_{0}$ bundles in the sense that isomorphism classes of principal $G_{0}$ bundles on any space $X$ are in one-to-one correspondence with homotopy classes of maps from $X$ to $BG_{0}$ . Cohomology of $BG_{0}$ is also known as group cohomology of $G_{0}$ .

suspicious_text: In a counterintuitive scenario, one might inquire if bosonic topological phase transitions (SPT phases) exist that are nontrivial from the perspective of group cohomology but trivial from the cobordism standpoint. Initially, this seems improbable as a nontrivial element in $H^{d}(BG,U(1))$ typically corresponds to a nontrivial topological action $S_{M}^{\alpha}(A)$. However, such a situation may arise due to the more restrictive nature of the topology of smooth orientable manifolds compared to general topological spaces. In these instances, there might be nontrivial elements in $H^{d}(BG,U(1))$ that vanish when pulled back to any closed oriented d-manifold. As demonstrated in [9], this can occur only for d>6 (with the example provided in [9] being for G=G0=Z3×Z3 and d=7), implying that for d≤6, the cobordism classification of SPT phases is stricter than the group cohomology classification, making such phenomena largely academic.

Each of these methods possesses distinct advantages and limitations. While they offer accurate approximate solutions, they necessitate the discretization of the problem domain. source_text: Recall that a manifold $M$ , whether oriented or not, carries a canonical principal ${\mathbb{Z}}_{2}$ bundle $\xi_{M}$ called the orientation bundle of $M$ . The holonomy of the associated ${\mathbb{Z}}_{2}$ -connection assigns $-1$ to all closed loops which reverse orientation and assigns $+1$ to all closed loops which do not. A closed manifold $M$ , whether oriented or not, has the fundamental class living in $H_{d}(M,{\mathbb{Z}}^{T})$ , where ${\mathbb{Z}}^{T}$ is a local system with fiber ${\mathbb{Z}}$ twisted by the orientation bundle. Its Pontryagin-dual is $H^{d}(M,U(1)^{T})$ , where $U(1)^{T}$ is a local system over $M$ with fiber $U(1)$ twisted by the orientation bundle.

source_text: As demonstrated in Section 4 , SIDM causes an increase in the mass-loss rate of stellar material but does not change significantly the mass-loss rate of the total dark matter mass. In turn, the subhalo mass function in terms of dark matter is not affected, but the stellar mass function should be suppressed. Due to limitations of not tagging the same total number of haloes in SIDM as in CDM, however, we cannot directly show the suppression.

suspicious_text: The thermodynamic properties of a system can typically be determined from microscopic interactions only under specific conditions. However, in many cases, only a limited number of microscopic interaction characteristics are significant. Systems that share these same characteristics exhibit identical macroscopic behavior, indicating a degree of universality. A prime example is a gas composed of particles with short-range interactions. When the density and temperature are such that the thermal de Broglie wavelength $\lambda$ is much greater than the interaction range $R$, the specifics of the interaction potential become largely inconsequential, and the thermodynamics are governed by the two-body scattering length $a$, with corrections of order $\lambda/R$. This insight has been instrumental in the study of both fermionic and bosonic gases since the 1950s, particularly in N.N. Bogoliubov's [1947] work on calculating the properties of dilute gases using an expansion in powers of $na^{3}$,

source_text: We would like to emphasize that, up to gauge invariant terms, the effective action for the edge current, ( 36 ), is uniquely determined by the requirement of electric charge conservation. The only possible generalization is to consider several independent edge channels of charged quasi-particles. In contrast to the bulk contribution to the total action ( 33 ), the edge action is not topological, i.e., it depends on the space-time metric of the edge. Therefore, in general, each edge channel may couple to a different space-time metric (i.e., exhibit a different propagation speed). We will discuss this point in more detail in the next section.

These special choices of $D(x)$ and $U(x)$ ensure that the particle, with an initial position $x_{0}>0$ , can never cross the origin. Therefore, the system is essentially confined at the positive values of $x$ . Note that when $U_{0}>0$ , the potential is attractive, whereas it is repulsive for $U_{0}<0$ . A possible choice of generators for $\Omega_{SO,d}^{4}(pt,U(1))$ is $w_{1}^{4}$ and $w_{2}^{2}$ . Thus we expect three nontrivial bosonic SPT phases in $d=4$ , in agreement with [4] . Note that group cohomology classification sees only one of these three phases. We can figure out which action corresponds to this special SPT phase by noting that the group cohomology approach is based on classifying actions which depend only on the gauge field on $M$ , which in this case is the first Stiefel-Whitney class $w_{1}\in H^{1}(M,{\mathbb{Z}}_{2})$ . The rate of the charge exchange reaction 
 
 ${\rm H}_{2}+{\rm H}^{+}\rightarrow{\rm H}_{2}^{+}+{\rm H},$  (1) 
 was recently revised by Savin et al. (2004a,b). We use this updated rate because these processes become important when the ionization fraction of the gas is large. Another important process is collisional dissociation of H \textsubscript{2} by electron impacts 
 
 ${\rm H}_{2}+e\rightarrow 2{\rm H}+e.$  (2) 
 The commonly used values are for H \textsubscript{2} molecules which are initially in the $v=0$ level (e.g. Shapiro & Kang 1987). Stibbe & Tennyson (1999) show that the LTE rate, which accounts for dissociation from higher vibrational levels, is about two orders of magnitude larger. Since we are interested in cooling of gas in early cosmological H ii regions, where the density is typically less than $\sim 1{\rm cm}^{-3}$ , we assume that the higher vibrational levels are not significantly populated and thus we use the $v=0$ rate in the present paper.

source_text: We are only interested in the finite type cluster algebras. Such cluster algebras have a finite set of distinct generators (cluster variables), that is grouped in a finite number of clusters (sets) of equal size and that relate to each other by exchange relations where one of the cluster variables is replaced by (mutates to) another cluster variable outside the cluster. These exchange relations can be codified within each cluster by associating them with oriented quivers. From the quiver associated to a cluster we can define $\mathcal{X}$ -coordinates related to each cluster variable in that cluster.

To reiterate, the critical reflecting radius $r^{\text{max}}_{\text{c}}({\bar{a}})$ holds a key role in the physical context. This radius represents the boundary for a stably spinning object with a specific dimensionless angular momentum parameter ${\bar{a}}$. When the reflecting surface of an object with this parameter value is situated beyond $r^{\text{max}}_{\text{c}}({\bar{a}})$, the object remains stable. On the other hand, if the reflecting surface falls within the region $r_{\text{c}}<r^{\text{max}}_{\text{c}}({\bar{a}})$, the object becomes unstable to scalar perturbation modes, leading to superradiant instabilities in the system consisting of exotic compact objects, massless scalar fields, and the object's own spin.

suspicious_text: In the detection process, the conventional DCF structure produces a response map, with the object presumed to be situated at the location of the highest value. The quality of the data within the response map is significant, as it provides an indication of the similarity between the model of the object's appearance learned in prior frames and the actual object detected in the current frame. It is common for inconsistencies to occur in situations such as occlusion, out-of-plane rotation, and other complex scenarios. Unfortunately, the conventional DCF framework does not capitalize on this information, and when such inconsistencies arise, no further action can be taken, resulting in the loss of the tracked object.

In this situation, we encounter impurity clustering or aggregation at low $T$ , which often results in small crystalline domains of impurities (22) . In our model, this tendency is considerably suppressed by the above-mentioned impurity-ellipse attractive interaction. Nevertheless, the impurity distribution is still mesoscopically heterogeneous, leading to a mesoscopic heterogeneity in the rotational motions. We shall see that some fraction of the ellipses still rotate under weak constraints even at low $T$ . Furthermore, if anisotropic particles have the elliptic symmetry (the spheroidal one in three dimensions), they can undergo flip (turnover) motions with $\pm\pi$ angle changes (13) . These flip motions can occur thermally for mild aspect ratios, while they are sterically hindered by the surrounding particles for large aspect ratios. Thus, we expect a wide range of the rotational activity for mild aspect ratios.

The rate of the charge exchange reaction 
 
 ${\rm H}_{2}+{\rm H}^{+}\rightarrow{\rm H}_{2}^{+}+{\rm H},$  (1) 
 was recently revised by Savin et al. (2004a,b). We use this updated rate because these processes become important when the ionization fraction of the gas is large. Another important process is collisional dissociation of H \textsubscript{2} by electron impacts 
 
 ${\rm H}_{2}+e\rightarrow 2{\rm H}+e.$  (2) 
 The commonly used values are for H \textsubscript{2} molecules which are initially in the $v=0$ level (e.g. Shapiro & Kang 1987). Stibbe & Tennyson (1999) show that the LTE rate, which accounts for dissociation from higher vibrational levels, is about two orders of magnitude larger. Since we are interested in cooling of gas in early cosmological H ii regions, where the density is typically less than $\sim 1{\rm cm}^{-3}$ , we assume that the higher vibrational levels are not significantly populated and thus we use the $v=0$ rate in the present paper.

source_text: An exclusion from this rule is the appearance of internal stagnation points —fluid particles with zero velocity relative to the steady flow—and critical layers . By a critical layer we mean a connected part of the fluid domain consisting only of closed streamlines. The existence of such waves within linear theory was established in [13] , where the streamlines and particle paths of waves with constant vorticity were studied. For irrotational flows such behavior is not encountered, cf. [3] . Also earlier studies had indicated that internal stagnation should be possible (see, e.g., [16] ), but it was first with [26] that waves with critical layers were given a firm mathematical foundation (see also [9] ). A different approach in dealing with the free boundary allowed for including internal stagnation, a phenomenon which for technical reasons had not been possible to see with earlier methods for rotational waves (cf., e.g., [7] ).
