source_text: The paper is structured as follows. The methodology of the proposed strategy is described in Section 2 . Construction of the perturbation approximation functions for Finite Element Analysis is derived in Section 3 . The implementation into the Hybrid-Trefftz stress element formulation containing an arbitrary number of inclusions is presented in Section 4 . Section 5 comprises examples demonstrating the model’s performance. Finally we present the conclusions as well as a discussion on future research directions. An appendix is included that highlights some important, but rather technical, aspects of the proposed technique in order to keep the paper self-contained.

source_text: The efficient injection of spin-polarized electrons from a ferromagnetic source into a semiconducting channel is a fundamental ingredient of spin-based electronic device concepts. The injected spin polarization can be detected by analyzing the degree of circular polarization of photons that are emitted in the semiconductor after recombination of the injected electrons with resident holes Alvarado and Renaud (1992) . In all-electrical devices, the concept of nonlocal spin detection Johnson and Silsbee (1985) has been used to convert the injected spin polarization into a nonlocal voltage $\Delta U_{\rm nl}$ that is measured at a ferromagnetic detection contact to which the electron spins diffuse. This voltage depends not only on how efficient spins are injected and detected, but also on the loss of spin polarization during the diffusive spin transport in the semiconductor. For electrical spin-injection into GaAs, a rapid decay of $\Delta U_{\rm nl}$ with temperature has been reported Lou

source_text: To produce glassy states, we slowly quench the mixture from liquid. In this situation, we encounter impurity clustering or aggregation at low $T$ , which often results in small crystalline domains of impurities (22) . In our model, this tendency is considerably suppressed by the above-mentioned impurity-ellipse attractive interaction. Nevertheless, the impurity distribution is still mesoscopically heterogeneous, leading to a mesoscopic heterogeneity in the rotational motions. We shall see that some fraction of the ellipses still rotate under weak constraints even at low $T$ . Furthermore, if anisotropic particles have the elliptic symmetry (the spheroidal one in three dimensions), they can undergo flip (turnover) motions with $\pm\pi$ angle changes (13) . These flip motions can occur thermally for mild aspect ratios, while they are sterically hindered by the surrounding particles for large aspect ratios. Thus, we expect a wide range of the rotational activity for mild aspect ratios.

In the nonannealed sample, $\Delta\rho_{\rm nl}$ exhibits an exponential decay with increasing temperature up to 200 K, characterized by a decay rate of approximately 0.018 K\textsuperscript{-1}, consistent across $U_{\rm c}$ values of -200 and 100 mV. However, at $U_{\rm c}=0$ mV, the behavior is more complex, which is attributed to the bias-dependent variation of $\eta_{i}$ as $\Delta U_{\rm nl}$ approaches 0 at small positive $U_{\rm c}$. In contrast, annealing the sample enhances $\Delta\rho_{\rm nl}$ at low temperatures but also leads to a more rapid decrease with temperature, with a decay rate of 0.030 K\textsuperscript{-1}, resulting in a smaller signal at higher temperatures compared to the nonannealed sample, as observed at $U_{\rm c}=0$ mV and similarly at $U_{\rm c}=100$ mV.
source_text: Figure 3 summarizes the $T$ -dependence of $\Delta\rho_{\rm nl}$ for both nonannealed and annealed samples. For the nonannealed sample, $\Delta\rho_{\rm nl}$ decays exponentially with increasing $T$ up to 200 K. The decay rate is about $0.018$ K \textsuperscript{-1} (black line) for both $U_{\rm c}=-200$ and 100 mV. For $U_{\rm c}=0$ mV, a more complicated behavior with $T$ is observed, which we attribute to the crossing of $\Delta U_{\rm nl}=0$ at small positive $U_{\rm c}$ , i.e. to the bias dependence of $\eta_{i}$ . Annealing the sample increases $\Delta\rho_{\rm nl}$ at low $T$ , but at the same time, $\Delta\rho_{\rm nl}$ decreases much faster with $T$ , namely, at a rate of 0.030 K \textsuperscript{-1} (red dashed line). This faster decrease results in a signal that at higher $T$ becomes smaller than that of the nonannealed sample. The data for the annealed sample were obtained at $U_{\rm c}=0$ , but similar behavior is observed for $U_{\rm c}=100$ mV (not shown).

suspicious_text: In dense molecular gas clouds, self-shielding effects must be considered, and can be accounted for using an effective shielding factor that depends on the column density of molecular hydrogen. This factor is calculated based on the local molecular hydrogen column density, which is determined by integrating the molecular hydrogen number density along multiple lines of sight surrounding each gas particle. The calculation takes into account the velocity structure of the gas, with particles having large relative velocities discarded to account for Doppler shifting, and uses a thermal velocity appropriate for cold, dense gas. The resulting column density estimates are then used to compute the shielding factor, which becomes significant at high column densities, although the gas can remain optically thin to Lyman-Werner photons even at high densities if there are large velocity gradients or disordered motion.

source_text: The organization of this paper is as follows. In Sec.II, we will explain our simulation model and method. In Sec.IIIA, we will present simulation results on the heterogeneities in the orientations and the positions. In Sec.IIIB, the time-correlation functions will be examined. In Sec.IIIC, the angular and translational mean-square displacements will be calculated. In Sec.IIID, we will introduce the flip number for each ellipse in a time interval and study its heterogeneity. In Sec.IIIE, time-development of a configurational change with large displacements and/or large angle changes will be illustrated.

Two such examples are depicted in Figure 9. It appears that the integrated and clump properties of these clusters are comparable to those of chains, doubles, and tadpoles. However, the average surface brightnesses of the clump clusters are approximately one magnitude arcsec \textsuperscript{-2} dimmer. The lowest contours in Figure 9 correspond to $1\sigma$ for the left-hand clump cluster, just as in the contours of Figure 2, and $0.5\sigma$ for the right-hand cluster.

source_text: Optical multimodal sensing methodology. The proposed sensing approach is based on optical scattering from soft materials when the material is irradiated with laser light [Fig. 1 ]. The optical scattering produces a complex interference pattern, referred to as a speckle pattern Goodman (2000) , on an observation plane. The speckle pattern is highly sensitive to the scattering process in a material; thus, it can contain various information regarding the deformation of a soft material. Although speckle-based techniques have been used in sensing a single physical signal Murray et al. (2019) , deformation field measurement Fricke-Begemann (2003) , three-dimensional shape measurement Schaffer et al. (2010) , and in spectrometers Redding et al. (2013) , this study presents the first demonstration of multimodal sensing in soft materials.

suspicious_text: The primary aim of this study is to devise a Finite Element methodology capable of addressing macroscopic mechanical systems while accounting for the effects of sub-scale heterogeneities. Our approach circumvents the necessity of explicitly resolving these heterogeneities within the Finite Element mesh. Instead, we derive analytical expressions that quantify the mechanical field perturbations induced by the presence of such heterogeneities, which are subsequently integrated into the enhanced Finite Element framework.

suspicious_text: To validate the accuracy of our radiation hydrodynamics calculations, we utilize a spherical cloud model, which provides a straightforward yet informative scenario for evaluation. This test case involves a Population III star with a mass of 200 solar masses embedded within a collapsing dark halo of $10^{6}M_{\odot}$ at a redshift of 20. The initial gas density profile follows a power-law distribution, with a specified number of particles distributed accordingly to replicate the desired density profile. The radiation source is characterized by a thermal spectrum with an effective temperature of $10^{5}$ K, and the simulation is limited to hydrogen to simplify the calculation, with further details available in the referenced study.
source_text: We test the overall accuracy of our radiation hydrodynamics calculation using a spherical cloud problem. Expansion of an I-front in a spherical gas cloud offers a simple, yet illustrative case for this purpose. We adopt a spherical gas cloud problem studied by KYSU: a massive Population III star with $M_{*}=200M_{\odot}$ is embedded at the center of a $10^{6}M_{\odot}$ dark halo that is collapsing at $z=20$ . We set the initial gas density profile to follow a power law $n=3000(r/{\rm pc})^{-2}{\rm cm}^{-3}$ . We distribute 0.2 million particles in the sphere according to this density profile. The source is chosen to have a thermal spectrum with effective temperature $T_{\rm eff}=10^{5}$ K. For this test problem we include only hydrogen. (See KYSU for further details.)

source_text: We do not trigger a supernova explosion in the simulations in the present paper for several reasons. First, the fate of Population III stars with mass $\sim 20-100M_{\odot}$ is rather uncertain in terms of their mechanical feedback efficiency (Umeda, Nomoto & Nakamura 2000; Heger & Woosley 2002), while very massive stars with $\sim 140-260M_{\odot}$ likely cause complete destruction of the surrounding structure (Bromm, Yoshida & Hernquist 2003). Kitayama & Yoshida (2005) show that the halo destruction efficiency of supernova explosions is sensitively dependent on the explosion energy, initial density of the explosion site and even on reshift, because of efficient Compton cooling in the early Universe. Therefore, it is necessary to use outputs of simulations of H ii regions, such as those presented in the present paper, for the study of supernova feedback effect. We will defer a consideration of these effects to future work.

suspicious_text: At elevated temperatures and densities, the theoretical framework of quantum chromodynamics forecasts a deconfined phase transition, wherein nuclear matter undergoes a transformative shift to a quark-gluon plasma state. A primary objective of the Relativistic Heavy Ion Collider and the Large Hadron Collider is to experimentally verify the existence of this novel state of matter. Notably, two significant discoveries have emerged from RHIC experiments: firstly, the deconfined, high-temperature medium exhibits characteristics akin to a nearly ideal fluid, possessing minimal viscosity, a phenomenon that has been effectively modeled by various research groups utilizing viscous hydrodynamics to replicate the evolution of matter produced in heavy ion collisions, accurately reproducing observable phenomena such as elliptic flow and particle spectra. Secondly, the pronounced suppression of jet formation, or jet quenching, is thought to be a promising indicator of quark-gluon plasma formation. M
source_text: Quantum chromodynamics (QCD) predicts that deconfined phase transition will take place at high temperature and/or high density, as a result the nuclear matter will undergo a transition to quark-gluon plasma (QGP). One main goal for Relativistic Heavy Ion Collider (RHIC) and Large Hadronic Collider (LHC) is to seek this new state of matter. There are two striking findings at RHIC. One is that the deconfined hot medium behaviors as a nearly perfect fluid with a small viscosity (1) . Several groups have applied viscous hydrodynamics to simulate the evolution of the produced matter in heavy ion collisions. The simulations successfully fit the observables at RHIC, such as the elliptic flow, the particle spectra, etc (5) . The other is the strong jet quenching, which is believed to be a potential signal for the QGP formation (14) . It should be stressed that the first LHC results also strongly support similar conclusions as seen at RHIC (15) .

suspicious_text: In dense molecular gas clouds, self-shielding effects must be considered, and can be accounted for using an effective shielding factor that depends on the column density of molecular hydrogen. This factor is calculated based on the local molecular hydrogen column density, which is determined by integrating the molecular hydrogen number density along multiple lines of sight surrounding each gas particle. The calculation takes into account the velocity structure of the gas, with particles having large relative velocities discarded to account for Doppler shifting, and uses a thermal velocity appropriate for cold, dense gas. The resulting column density estimates are then used to compute the shielding factor, which becomes significant at high column densities, although the gas can remain optically thin to Lyman-Werner photons even at high densities if there are large velocity gradients or disordered motion.

This process occurs through two key steps: the formation of H^- via the reaction of hydrogen and electrons, followed by the interaction of H^- with hydrogen to produce H2 and release an electron. However, at very high redshifts (z>200), alternative formation pathways, including the H2+ channel and reactions involving HeH+ ions, become dominant due to the destruction of H^- by cosmic microwave background photons. Notably, significant variability exists among experimental and theoretical estimates of the reaction rate for the second step, which can substantially impact the resulting H2 abundance and gas cooling timescale. suspicious_text: In the initial discourse, it is noted that SPT phases in d dimensions are linked to ’t Hooft anomalies in (d-1) dimensions. To illustrate, time-reversal-invariant SPT phases in dimension d are equivalent to ’t Hooft anomalies for time-reversal in dimension (d-1). Implementing time-reversal symmetry involves establishing the theory on a non-oriented manifold. Consequently, the presence of an ’t Hooft anomaly for time-reversal implies that a theory possesses a time-reversal symmetry in flat spacetime, but fails to be consistently defined on a non-oriented manifold. This particular scenario can be viewed as a specific instance of global gravitational anomaly.

Representative objects are shown in Figure 2, with V-band grayscale images and contours. The contours are in linear increments; the lowest contour level is 1 $\sigma$ of the pixel noise, which corresponds to a surface brightness of 25.5 mag arcsec \textsuperscript{-2} .

suspicious_text: In this segment, we outline the essential mathematical prerequisites as per Atiyah [12]. Given a topological space X, we define an oriented degree-d bordism as a closed, oriented d-dimensional manifold M coupled with a continuous function f:M->X. Two such bordisms are considered equivalent if a (d+1)-dimensional compact oriented manifold N with boundary equal to M1 ∪ ∓M2 exists, along with a map g:N->X that restricts to f1 and f2 on the two parts of N's boundary. The collection of these equivalence classes forms an abelian group, denoted ΩSO,d(X), referred to as the oriented bordism group. The operation within this group stems from the disjoint union operation on d-manifolds, and the inverse of (M,f) is represented by (∓M,f), where ∓M is the reversal of M's orientation. The Pontryagin dual of ΩSO,d(X) is termed the degree-d oriented cobordism group of X, denoted ΩdSO(X). A map exists from ΩSO,d(X) to Hd(X,Z), which sends (M,f) to f_*(M), where [M] ∈ Hd(M,Z) is M's fundamental homology
source_text: This section explains the necessary mathematical background following Atiyah [12] . Let $X$ be a topological space. A degree- $d$ oriented bordism to $X$ is a closed oriented $d$ -manifold $M$ together with a continuous map $f:M\rightarrow X$ . There is an equivalence relation on bordisms: $(M_{1},f_{1})\sim(M_{2},f_{2})$ if there exists a $d+1$ -dimensional compact oriented manifold $N$ with boundary $M_{1}\sqcup\bar{M}_{2}$ and a map $g:N\rightarrow X$ which reduces to $f_{1}$ and $f_{2}$ on the two components of $\partial N$ . The set of equivalence classes of oriented bordisms to $X$ forms an abelian group called the oriented bordism group denoted $\Omega_{SO,d}(X)$ . The group operation arises from the disjoint union operation on $d$ -manifolds, and the negative of $(M,f)$ can be represented by $(\bar{M},f)$ , where $\bar{M}$ is the orientation reversal of $M$ . This section will delve into the examination of the latter. I am indebted to Alexei Kitaev for his insight that such geometric terms may indeed be permissible.
source_text: In general the effective action may contain local geometric terms. They can be of two kinds: the terms which depend only on the topology of $M$ and the terms which also depend on the geometry of $M$ . In this section we discuss the latter. \footnote{ 3 I am grateful to Alexei Kitaev for pointing out that such geometric terms are allowed.}

source_text: For abelian IHFs with $N$ conserved currents, ( 71 ), the family of physical theories has been classified mathematically, [5] . For each fluid, it is possible to enumerate all quasi-particle excitations. For a given Hall conductivity, $\sigma$ , and a certain number, $N$ , of currents, the quasi-particles are labeled by vertices of a lattice, $\Gamma^{*}$ , of dimension $N$ , dual to an odd integral lattice , $\Gamma$ , of (multi-)electron excitations. This again implies that the dimensionless Hall conductivity $\sigma$ is a rational number . Steps towards a generalization of this approach to non-abelian IHFs have been undertaken in [11] .

suspicious_text: In the SIDM1 model, differences emerge in the stellar density and velocity dispersion profiles, primarily caused by perturbations to satellite orbits induced by a core in the host halo. These perturbations are clearly visible in Fig. 3. The most massive satellites in SIDM1 exhibit significant cores, leading to enhanced stripping compared to CDM for the same orbital parameters. Additionally, these satellites generate tidal streams with higher velocity dispersion perpendicular to the orbital plane, as shown in Errani et al. (2015) for cored satellites. However, the global characteristics of the stellar halo are predominantly shaped by the specific orbits of the most massive satellites. Without detailed knowledge of these orbits, it is challenging to isolate effects directly tied to SIDM. On smaller mass scales, SIDM1 does not produce cores as large as those in the v-d models, resulting in less pronounced features compared to the vdSIDMb model, as illustrated in Fig. 18.
source_text: In the case of SIDM1, some differences do arise in the stellar density profile and velocity dispersion profile, but primarily result from perturbations of satellite orbits due to a core in the host halo. These perturbations are visually apparent in Fig. 3 . The largest satellites in SIDM1 do have significant cores and consequently have enhanced stripping relative to CDM for a fixed orbit. They also produce tidal streams with a higher velocity dispersion perpendicular to the orbital plane, as demonstrated in Errani et al. (2015) for satellites with cores. However, the global level characteristics of the stellar halo considered are dominated by the particular orbits of the handful of most massive satellites. We are unable to isolate effects directly indicative of SIDM without detailed knowledge of the orbits. On smaller mass scales, SIDM1 does not produce large enough cores as in the v-d models to create significant features like those shown in Fig. 18 for the vdSIDMb model.

source_text: To evaluate the long-term stability of the proposed sensing approach, we recorded the time transition of the estimation errors for 30 days, as shown in Fig. 4 (d). In this experiment, we used the training dataset acquired on the first day and set the network parameters of the model shown in Fig. 2 (d); then, we measured the estimation errors. Although the speckle pattern measurement is generally sensitive to environmental changes, particularly temperature change, our results reveal that the error increases only by 1.7 $\%$ over 30 days and does not significantly change for 5 days, suggesting the robustness of our measurement method. This is attributed to the fact that the network architecture is trained for temperature changes.

In the above example of an isobarically cooling gas, one of the main formation path is the reaction ${\rm D}^{+}+{\rm H}_{2}\rightarrow{\rm H}^{+}+{\rm HD}$ , for which there is no counterpart for H \textsubscript{2} . The H \textsubscript{2} fraction in the diffuse neutral gas in the early universe is $\sim 10^{-5}-10^{-6}$ (Stancil et al. 1998; Galli & Palla 1998), whereas the universal H \textsubscript{2} fraction in the collapsing halo is as large as a few times $10^{-3}$ (Susa et al. 1998; Oh & Haiman 2002). Hence, the large H \textsubscript{2} fraction promotes the formation of HD molecules at low temperatures. suspicious_text: The foundational equations of the kinetic theory for quark-gluon plasma are comprised of equations (10) through (12), (15), and (16). Utilizing the ideal, equilibrium distribution functions of QGP constituents within the linear approximation of the QCD transport equation yields expressions for gluon self-energy, specifically $\Pi_{L}(\omega,k)$ and $\Pi_{T}(\omega,k)$, which are consistent with results obtained through the hard thermal loop approximation in finite temperature field theory. These expressions, in conjunction with the HTL photon self-energy and equations (7) through (9), have been employed by Juan Liu et al. to investigate the refractive index of light in QGP, revealing a negative refractive index within certain frequency ranges.
source_text:
Eqs.( 10 ),( 11 ),( 12 ), ( 15 ) and ( 16 ) make up the fundamental equations of the kinetic theory for the quark-gluon plasma. In the linear approximation of QCD transport equation, by using the ideal, equilibrium distribution functions of constituents of the QGP, one can obtain the gluon self-energy (41) 
 
 $\Pi_{L}(\omega,k)=m_{D}^{2}(1-\frac{\omega^{2}}{k^{2}})[1-\frac{\omega}{2k}\log[\frac{\omega+k}{\omega-k}]],$  (17) 
 and 
 
 $\Pi_{T}(\omega,k)=\frac{1}{2}m_{D}^{2}[\frac{\omega^{2}}{k^{2}}+(1-\frac{\omega^{2}}{k^{2}})\frac{\omega}{2k}\log[\frac{\omega+k}{\omega-k}]],$  (18) 
 where $m_{D}$ is the Debye mass. ( 17 ) and ( 18 ) are consistent with those obtained in the hard thermal loop (HTL) approximation in diagrammatic methods at finite temperature field theory (43) . By combining with the HTL photon self-energy and Eqs.( 7 ), ( 8 ) and ( 9 ), Juan Liu et al. have studied the refraction index of light in the QGP and found that it becomes negative in some frequency range.

An algorithm to reconstruct strip ScECAL has been developed [8] . In the algorithm the total energy deposited on a strip, $E_{\mathrm{strip}}$ is split into virtual square cells where the strip is divided by its width along its length; a 45 $\times$ 5 mm \textsuperscript{2} strip is split into nine 5 $\times$ 5 mm \textsuperscript{2} cells. The energy is split conservatively according to the weights estimated by using the energy deposited on the strips in immediately neighboring layers, having intersection with the strip being considered, when seen from the interaction point of ILD. The energy deposited on the virtual cell $k$ is estimated as the following: 
 
 $E_{\mathrm{virtual}}^{k}=E_{\mathrm{strip}}\cdot\Sigma_{i}E_{\mathrm{neighbor}}^{\{i,k\}}/\Sigma_{i}E_{\mathrm{neighbor}}^{i},$  (1) 
 where, $k$ is the index of the virtual cell within the strip and $i$ is the index of neighbouring intersecting strips, and $E_{\mathrm{neighbor}}^{\{i,k\}}$ is the energy deposited in the strip

At these densities, Quantum Chromodynamics (QCD) can be addressed using perturbative methods, as demonstrated by Fraga et al. (2014). The resulting equation of state (EOS) is anticipated to align with the perturbative QCD (pQCD) model discussed in Section III.2.

In contrast, the Nambu-Jona-Lasinio (NJL) model serves as a low-energy model for QCD, with gluons being integrated out while maintaining only local quark interactions. The NJL model's coupling constants are fixed by reproducing the masses of $\pi$, $K$, $\eta^{\prime}$, and the $\pi$ decay constant, as established by Hatsuda and Kunihiro (1994).

suspicious_text: Truly explainable models should inherently incorporate reasoning to avoid relying on human users for explanation generation. In the model proposed by Doran et al. [2017], the black box, or connectionist component, produces the final output, while the knowledge base (KB) is externally provided. This design enables the system to generate its own explanations in natural language, bridging the high-level features identified by the model and the final output. It also underscores the logical pathway the model should follow: since the KB is user-provided and assumed correct, any reasoning error in the natural language explanation would indicate a flaw in the black box's processing between high-level features and the final output. Furthermore, as noted by Doran et al. [2017], integrating reasoning within the model prevents potential distortions in explanations that might arise from using external sources to justify the model's decision-making process.

suspicious_text: This section outlines a computational approach that integrates radiative transfer with hydrodynamics and chemical reactions, leveraging a highly efficient parallel processing framework. By incorporating the GADGET2 code, a sophisticated smoothed particle hydrodynamics tool, the entire simulation - including radiative transfer, gravitational forces, and fluid dynamics - can be scaled up to run on high-performance computing architectures. Notably, the hydrodynamics formulation employed here conserves key physical quantities, such as energy and momentum, by accurately tracking changes in the smoothing lengths of gas particles over time. To validate this methodology, a straightforward yet critical test is presented, involving the simulation of a small gas sphere undergoing photo-evaporation, which demonstrates the code's capability to model complex astrophysical phenomena.
source_text: In this section, we describe our numerical scheme for radiative transfer coupled with hydrodynamics and chemistry. The entire procedure is fully parallelized and coupled with the parallel smoothed particle hydrodynamics (SPH) code GADGET2 (Springel 2005), so that the radiative transfer calculation, as well as the gravity and hydrodynamics, can be performed on massively parallel architectures. We note that the version of SPH we adopt is a fully conservative formulation (Springel & Hernquist 2002), where the equations of motion properly account for evolution in the smoothing lengths of the gas particles. At the end of this section, we present a simple, yet very important, test case of photo-evaporation of a small gas sphere using our code.

In the literature, the angular mean-square displacement has been calculated to study the rotational diffusion (14) . In two dimensions, it is defined by 
 
 $M_{\theta}(t)=\langle{|\Delta\theta|^{2}}\rangle=\frac{1}{N_{1}}\sum_{i\in 1}\langle{|\Delta\theta_{i}(t_{0},t_{0}+t)|^{2}}\rangle.$  (17) 
 We also introduce the usual positional mean-square displacement for the ellipses by 
 
 $M(t)=\langle{|\Delta{\mbox{\boldmath$r$}}|^{2}}\rangle=\frac{1}{N_{1}}\sum_{i\in 1}\langle{|\Delta{\mbox{\boldmath$r$}}_{i}(t_{0},t_{0}+t)|^{2}}\rangle,$  (18) 
 where $\Delta{\mbox{\boldmath$r$}}_{i}(t_{0},t_{0}+t)={\mbox{\boldmath$r$}}_{i}(t_{0}+t)-{\mbox{\boldmath$r$}}_{i}(t_{0})$ . At very short times, these quantities exhibit the ballistic behavior $(\propto t^{2}$ ). At long times, they grow linearly in time as 
 
 $\displaystyle M_{\theta}(t)\cong 2D_{R}t,$  (19) 
 
 $\displaystyle M(t)\cong 4Dt,$  (20) 
 where $D_{R}$ and $D$ are the rotational and translational diffusion constants, respectively.

We adopt the approach outlined in Vogelsberger et al. (2012) to model our haloes using Hernquist profiles (Hernquist 1990), where the density distribution is given by 

\[
\rho(r) = \frac{Ma}{2\pi r} \frac{1}{(r + a)^3} \quad (6)
\]

and the velocity dispersion, \(\sigma_{\text{vel}}^2(r, a, M)\), is derived directly from Hernquist (1990). Here, \(M\) represents the halo mass, and \(a\) is the scale radius. Based on our CDM simulation and virial radius definition, the halo concentration \(c \equiv r_{\text{vir}} / a\) is found to scale as 

\[
c = 80.1 \times \left( \frac{M}{\mathrm{M_{\odot}}} \right)^{-1/11}
\]

at \(z = 0\). The halo mass \(M\) is expressed in terms of the virial mass \(m_{\text{vir}}\) as 

\[
M = m_{\text{vir}} \frac{(1 + c)^2}{c^2} \quad (7)
\]

with the scale radius \(a\) determined by \(a = r_{\text{vir}} / c\).

source_text: In summary, we have investigated the role of the bulk-brane energy exchange on the evolution of a brane universe. Due to the energy flow between the bulk and the brane, the standard energy conservation is broken. Parameterizing the energy exchange term by the scale factor and Hubble parameter, we have derived the effective cosmological equations in the limit of low energy density on the brane. Compared to the usual Friedmann equation, we have got an additional modified term due to the brane-bulk energy exchange. We found that the modified cosmological picture on the brane accommodates the late time acceleration and the equation of state for the effective dark energy experiences a transition behavior from above $-1$ to below $-1$ , while the dark energy on the brane still obeys the causality condition. Section IIIC involves the computation of angular and translational mean-square displacements. We caution that due to the nature of our particle tagging, we cannot definitely declare the magnitude of the signal, only the trends. source_text: Braid statistics is an interesting phenomenon only encountered in two-dimensional systems. In dimensions larger than two, quantum statistics is always described by representations of the permutation group; see, e.g., [37] and refs. given there. The theoretical possibility of braid statistics in 2D systems appears to be realized in IHFs at certain fractional plateaux.

source_text: Much attention has been paid to various types of glass transitions, where the structural relaxations become extremely slow with lowering the temperature $T$ (1) . In experiments, colloidal particles can be spherical, but real molecules are mostly nonspherical. The translational and rotational diffusion constants have thus been measured in molecular systems near the glass transition (3) . Using generalized mode-coupling theories, some authors (4) have studied the coupled translation-rotation dynamics to predict translational glass and orientational glass. Theoretically, for double glass (6) , the translational and orientational degrees of freedom can be simultaneously arrested at the same temperature. In real systems, the molecular rotations sensitively depend on many parameters including the molecular shapes, the density and the concentration (for mixtures).

source_text: In the following we will investigate the entanglement properties of other, so-called restricted, classes of quantum circuits, i.e., circuits produced by gate sets that are in general not universal. A famous example of such a restricted class of quantum circuits is that consisting of so-called Clifford transformations which are generated by the set $\mathcal{I}_{\text{Clif}}=\{C_{X},H,P=\exp{[-i\sigma_{z}\pi/4]}\}$ . The latter are, by virtue of the Gottesman-Knill theorem, always classically simulatable when initiated in the state $\lvert 0\rangle^{\otimes N}$ and readout in the computational basis (1) . Another class of quantum circuits that is known to be efficiently simulatable are so-called nearest-neighbour matchgate circuits (see Fig. 7 (a)). Matchgates are two-qubit gates that consist of two single qubit gates with equal determinant that act on the even and odd parity subspace of the two qubits, respectively (69) . The moments (3) have been identified as suitable tools for analyzing multi-particle correlations (21). Specifically, combining moments of varying orders enhances detectability by expanding the range of states that can be identified (25). Additionally, evaluating moments across different subsets $A$ of qudits often provides deeper insights into the system's state (see Refs. (31)). For example, the purity formula (1) can be represented as a summation of second-order reduced moments over all qudit subsets, expressed as  

$\displaystyle\mathrm{tr}(\varrho^{2})=\frac{1}{d^{N}}\sum_{A\subset\{1,\ldots,N\}}(d^{2}-1)^{|A|}\mathcal{R}_{A}^{(2)}(\varrho).$  (4)  
where $\mathcal{R}_{\emptyset}^{(2)}\equiv 1$, with $\emptyset$ denoting the empty set.

The moments ( 3 ) have been previously shown to be good candidates for the characterization of multiparticle correlations (21) . In particular, it was shown that the combination of moments of different order leads to an improved sensitivity in the sense that a larger class of states can be detected (25) . Furthermore, it is often useful to combine moments evaluated on different subsets $A$ of qudits in order to obtain more information about the underlying state (see Refs. (31) ). For instance, the purity formula ( 1 ) can be expressed as a sum over second order reduced moments of all subsets of qudits, yielding 
 
 $\displaystyle\mathrm{tr}(\varrho^{2})=\frac{1}{d^{N}}\sum_{A\subset\{1,\ldots,N\}}(d^{2}-1)^{|A|}\mathcal{R}_{A}^{(2)}(\varrho).$  (4) 
 where $\mathcal{R}_{\emptyset}^{(2)}\equiv 1$ , with $\emptyset$ denoting the empty set.

source_text: An important problem in condensed matter theory is to understand equivalence classes of gapped phases of matter with a symmetry group $G$ and no long-range entanglement. The equivalence is understood in the sense of homotopy theory. Such equivalence classes are called Symmetry Protected Topological (SPT) phases. The classification of free fermionic SPT phases with or without translational invariance is well understood by now [1] , but the situation with interacting systems is more complicated. We will be interested in the interacting case here and for simplicity will discuss only theories without fermions. We do not assume translational invariance, so the SPT phases we discuss are robust with respect to disorder.

source_text: To explain the results of Section 4 without subhalo evaporation, we turn to the fundamental physics of tidal stripping. External to subhaloes, the strength of the gravitational tidal field controls how much mass is stripped from satellites. This plays a role in determining the tidal radius, $r_{\rm{t}}$ , which defines an approximate distance beyond which mass is no longer bound to a subhalo. Naturally, a smaller tidal radius results in more stripping. Internal to subhaloes, which stellar particles are stripped is controlled by dynamics at the centre of the halo. To compare CDM against SIDM, the size of the halo core, $r_{\rm{core}}$ , is critical since it correlates with the strength of dynamical differences due to self-interactions. SIDM reduces halo central density and increases halo central velocity dispersion, reducing the binding energy of particles within the core. Consequently, an increase in stellar mass-loss manifests in two ways.

suspicious_text: This section examines the thermal and chemical development of a gravitationally re-collapsing gas cloud originating from a previously ionized gas within a relic H ii region, focusing on the region surrounding the first star and excluding potential distant gas cooling and star formation. A comparative simulation is also conducted without H ii region calculation to facilitate a direct evaluation of the effects of radiative feedback, which will be discussed in subsequent sections.
source_text: In this section, we study the thermal and chemical evolution of a gas cloud which is re-collapsing gravitationally from a once-ionized gas in the relic H ii region. To do so, we deliberately restrict ourselves to the vicinity of the first star; i.e., we ignore (possible) gas cooling and star-formation occurring in places remote from the H ii region. We also run a simulation without H ii region calculation, in order to make a direct comparison with runs with various radiative feedback effects described in the following section.

The mean pericentre of satellite orbits is well correlated with the strength of disruptive tidal forces exerted on a satellite, even though it ignores the complexity of a full orbit. We therefore divide our sample into bins of $0-30\,\mathrm{kpc}$ , $30-70\,\mathrm{kpc}$ , and $>70\,\mathrm{kpc}$ mean pericentre crossing. We choose $30\,\rm{kpc}$ as the upper limit of the first bin since it corresponds approximately to the radius at which a Milky Way-like disc causes divergent tidal stripping in cored versus cuspy subhaloes as studied in Peñarrubia
et al. (2010) . Both cored and cuspy satellites experience more disruption with a disc, but cored satellites with low pericentres experience a much greater increase in disruption than cuspy ones. Since our simulations do not include a disc in the host, the effects of stripping in both CDM and SIDM cases in the $d_{\rm{peri}}<30\,\mathrm{kpc}$ bin will be underestimated relative to the other bins.

This scholarly discourse aims to elucidate analytically the sequence of critical radii $\{r_{\text{c}}({\bar{a}};n)\}^{n=\infty}_{n=1}$, which serve as distinctive features of horizonless, rotating exotic compact objects that accommodate stable, spatially uniform static scalar field configurations. Notably, we will present a strikingly succinct analytical equation for the maximum radii $\{r^{\text{max}}_{\text{c}}({\bar{a}})\}$ of these exotic objects, delineating the borderline between stable and unstable horizonless, rotating configurations.

The presented formula provides a rational basis for understanding the physico-mathematical intricacies associated with the existence and stability of these unique, horizonless, spinning objects. By elucidating the mathematical underpinnings of their spatial structure, we further our comprehension of the boundaries that govern their stability and instability in the realm of exotic compact objects.

source_text:
We use a suite of five simulations, each of which takes its initial conditions from the Aquarius Project Aq-A-3 halo of Springel
et al. (2008) . Two simulations are run with a velocity-independent cross-section (the v-i models), two with a velocity-dependent cross-section (the v-d models), and one is run with pure CDM as the baseline for comparison. Using the same initial conditions allows us to isolate the effects of each SIDM model. All of the simulations are the same as those initially used in and described in Vogelsberger et al. (2012) . A summary of the simulations is found in Table 1 . In the v-i cases, $\sigma_{T}^{\mathrm{max}}/m_{\chi}$ is the collisional cross-section per unit mass. In the v-d cases, it is the cross-section achieved when the relative particle velocity is $v_{\mathrm{max,\sigma}}$ , which is the velocity at which the quantity $\sigma v$ is maximized.

source_text: The evolution of an isobarically cooling gas serves as a simple, yet illustrative model of how a photo-ionized or collisionally ionized gas evolves in collapsed halos. Figure 2.3 shows the temperature evolution for a parcel of gas cooling from $T=5\times 10^{4}$ K. We assume that the gas is initially fully ionized and then solve the full rate equations to study the thermal and chemical evolution. In Figure 2.3 , we show the evolutionary tracks for two cases, with and without HD chemistry and cooling. The overall evolution except in the low temperature region appears quite similar to that in, e.g., Oh & Haiman (2002). We see a clear difference in the evolution at $T<200$ K, however. Previous calculations neglect the formation of and cooling by HD, and hence the gas temperature is limited to $T\sim 100$ K, at which point cooling by H \textsubscript{2} becomes inefficient (see Fig. 1). suspicious_text: This study initiates an examination of the refractive index of gluons within a viscous quark-gluon plasma (QGP) using viscous chromohydrodynamics, given that gluons are the quantum chromodynamic equivalents of photons. The investigation of gluon refraction in QGP is pertinent due to its potential to elucidate the properties of QGP, particularly in the context of jet quenching, a phenomenon extensively studied over the past three decades in relation to heavy ion collisions and parton propagation in hot media. Notably, viscosity influences the distribution functions of QGP constituents, which in turn affects gluon self-energy and consequently the electric permittivity and magnetic permeability, ultimately impacting the refractive index.

More importantly, we want to point out and emphasise here that although we may be able to reduce the time complexity to ${\cal O}(1)$ , as with the choice of ( 45 )or ( 46 ), we need to consider also the energy resource required for the computation. The choice of ( 45 ) can, in fact, only be had at the cost of an increase in the energy required: 
 
 $\displaystyle\max_{0\leq t\leq T}\langle\phi_{0}|{\cal H}_{G}(t)|\phi_{0}\rangle$ $\displaystyle\to$ $\displaystyle{\cal O}(\sqrt{M}).$  (48) 
 That is, a reduction in time complexity (from ${\cal O}(\sqrt{M})$ to ${\cal O}(1)$ ) incurs and is balanced by an increase in the cost in energy resource (from ${\cal O}(1)$ to ${\cal O}(\sqrt{M})$ ). That is, in general the computational complexity of the AQC ( 40 ) is of the order ${\cal O}(\sqrt{M})$ , taking into account both the energy and time resources.

source_text: Overall performance evaluation: Figure 3 demonstrates the overall performance of ARCF-H and ARCF-HC with other state-of-the-art hand-crafted feature-based trackers on UAV123@10fps, DTB70 and UAVDT datasets. The proposed ARCF-HC tracker has outperformed all other trackers based on hand-crafted features on all three datasets. More specifically, on UAV123@10fps dataset, ARCF-HC (0.666) has an advantage of 0.6% and 3.9% over the second and third best tracker ECO-HC (0.660), STRCF (0.627) respectively in precision, as well as an advantage of 0.1% and 1.6% over the second (ECO-HC, 0.472) and third best tracker (STRCF, 0.457) respectively in AUC. On DTB70 dataset, ARCF-HC (0.694, 0.472) also achieved the best performance, followed by ECO-HC (0.648, 0.457) and STRCF (0.649, 0.437). On UAVDT, ARCF-HC tracker (0.720, 0.458) is closely followed by ARCF-H (0.705) and BACF (0.432) in precision and AUC respectively. The electric permittivity and magnetic permeability are typically characterized by complex-valued functions of frequency and wavevector, denoted as $\varepsilon(\omega,k)$ and $\mu_{M}(\omega,k)$, which can be decomposed into real and imaginary components, $\varepsilon_{r}(\omega,k)$, $\varepsilon_{i}(\omega,k)$, $\mu_{r}(\omega,k)$, and $\mu_{i}(\omega,k)$. Consequently, the refractive index $n$ also exhibits complex behavior. Research has shown that the condition for negative refraction in dissipative media is not as straightforward as requiring both $\varepsilon_{r}$ and $\mu_{r}$ to be negative simultaneously. Instead, a more nuanced criterion has been established, which states that the effective refractive index, $n_{eff} = \varepsilon_{r}|\mu_{M}| + \mu_{r}|\varepsilon|$, must be less than zero. suspicious_text: For the purpose of deriving the necessary conditions for the computation time $T_{AQC}$, we can assume an initial ground state energy of zero, similar to the time-independent Hamiltonian. This yields two key inequalities that relate $T_{AQC}$ to the energy expectation and spread of the initial state with respect to the final Hamiltonian. Specifically, the inequalities provide lower bounds on $T_{AQC}$ in terms of the integral of the annealing schedule $g(\tau)$, as well as the expectation energy $E_P$ and energy spread $\Delta_P E$ of the initial state. These bounds can be expressed in terms of characteristic times ${\cal T}_{AQC\forall}$ and ${\cal T}_{AQC\perp}$, which depend on the same parameters. If the computation time is less than ${\cal T}_{AQC\forall}$, there may exist states that are dynamically allowed but inaccessible from the initial state, while evolution times less than ${\cal T}_{AQC\perp}$ preclude the system from reaching any state orthogonal to the initial state.

suspicious_text: The Newtonian equations of motion were numerically integrated employing the leapfrog algorithm within a periodic boundary framework. Temperature ($T$) was gradually reduced from an initial value of 1 to a final value of 0.1, following a cooling protocol with a rate of $dT/dt = 0.9 \times 10^{-5}$. Upon reaching the target temperature, the system was allowed to equilibrate for $2 \times 10^{5}$ time units under the influence of a Nosé-Hoover thermostat, as described in reference (33). Subsequently, the thermostat was deactivated, ensuring that the remainder of the simulation proceeded in the $NVE$ ensemble. In this ensemble, the average translational kinetic energy per particle was maintained at $k_{B}T$, where $k_{B}$ represents the Boltzmann constant.
source_text: We integrated the Newton equations using the leap-frog method under the periodic boundary condition. We lowered $T$ from 1 to 0.1 at a cooling rate of $dT/dt=0.9\times 10^{-5}$ . We then changed $T$ to a final temperature and waited for $2\times 10^{5}$ , where a Nosé-Hoover thermostat (33) was imposed. But after this initial preparation, we switched off the thermostat, so our simulations have been performed in the $NVE$ ensemble, where the average translational kinetic energy was kept at $k_{B}T$ per particle.

The total electric current, $\mathcal{J}^{\mu}$ , is conserved, 
 
 $\partial_{\mu}\mathcal{J}^{\mu}=0\,.$  (39) 
 By Poincaré’s lemma, it can therefore be derived from a vector potential, which we denote by $B$ , 
 
 $\mathcal{J}^{\mu}=\sqrt{\sigma}\,\epsilon^{\mu\nu\lambda}\,\partial_{\nu}B_{\lambda}\,.$  (40) 
 The potential $B$ in ( 40 ) gives rise to the gauge symmetry $B_{\mu}\mapsto B_{\mu}+\partial_{\mu}\beta$ : $\mathcal{J}^{\mu}$ does not change under a gauge transformation of $B$ . The action

suspicious_text: In a more comprehensive cosmological framework, the presence of external radiation from other stars and galaxies formed prior to the first star in the simulation must be considered, as it can significantly impact the intergalactic medium. The intergalactic medium is largely transparent to far-UV radiation that can dissociate molecules, allowing for the rapid establishment of an early UV background radiation as stars and galaxies form. However, the net effect of this feedback on relic H ii regions remains uncertain, with some studies suggesting a positive feedback and others indicating a negative feedback relative to primordial neutral regions. The strength of the background Lyman-Werner radiation appears to be a crucial factor in determining the outcome, with a critical value existing below which H2 cooling in relic H ii regions is substantially suppressed. Nevertheless, existing simulations may underestimate this critical value due to the omission of gas self-shielding effects.

suspicious_text: High-performance models often face challenges in interpretability. Despite a growing body of research focused on explainable artificial intelligence (XAI) to clarify its objectives and expectations, no universal agreement exists on how to create or evaluate explanations. This paper explores why methods combining connectionist and symbolic approaches are the most effective for generating explanations for non-specialist users. We introduce a reasoning model, inspired by Doran et al. (2017), to elucidate the decision-making process of neural networks. This framework is utilized to address biases within the network's decision rationale. Additionally, we provide an illustrative example of its application, drawing on the image captioning technique from Burnse et al. (2018).
source_text: Many high-performance models suffer from a lack of interpretability. There has been an increasing influx of work on explainable artificial intelligence (XAI) in order to disentangle what is meant and expected by XAI. Nevertheless, there is no general consensus on how to produce and judge explanations. In this paper, we discuss why techniques integrating connectionist and symbolic paradigms are the most efficient solutions to produce explanations for non-technical users and we propose a reasoning model, based on definitions by Doranet al.[2017] , to explain a neural network’s decision. We use this explanation in order to correct bias in the network’s decision rationale. We accompany this model with an example of its potential use, based on the image captioning method in Burnset al.[2018] .

source_text: The evolution of an isobarically cooling gas serves as a simple, yet illustrative model of how a photo-ionized or collisionally ionized gas evolves in collapsed halos. Figure 2.3 shows the temperature evolution for a parcel of gas cooling from $T=5\times 10^{4}$ K. We assume that the gas is initially fully ionized and then solve the full rate equations to study the thermal and chemical evolution. In Figure 2.3 , we show the evolutionary tracks for two cases, with and without HD chemistry and cooling. The overall evolution except in the low temperature region appears quite similar to that in, e.g., Oh & Haiman (2002). We see a clear difference in the evolution at $T<200$ K, however. Previous calculations neglect the formation of and cooling by HD, and hence the gas temperature is limited to $T\sim 100$ K, at which point cooling by H \textsubscript{2} becomes inefficient (see Fig. 1). suspicious_text: In SIDM1, haloes with a mean pericentre exceeding $70\,\mathrm{kpc}$ exhibit negligible stripping, a characteristic shared with CDM models. Conversely, for haloes with a mean pericentre below $30\,\mathrm{kpc}$, self-interactions fail to induce additional stripping, a departure from velocity-dependent models where significant enhancement occurs. This discrepancy arises because SIDM1 generates a pronounced core in the host, thereby weakening the tidal forces responsible for stripping. In contrast, velocity-dependent models produce only minor core formation, leaving the tidal effects comparatively stronger.
source_text: For haloes in SIDM1 with mean pericentre above $70\,\mathrm{kpc}$ , there is neither appreciable stripping in SIDM nor in CDM. For haloes with mean pericentre below $30\,\mathrm{kpc}$ , we see an interesting effect where self-interactions do not cause any additional enhancement in stripping. This is dramatically different than the velocity-dependent models where a strong enhancement is apparent. A large core forms in the host in SIDM1, reducing the local tidal field that causes stripping, whereas only a small core forms in the host of the v-d models.

source_text: A mutation of a cluster variable in a cluster, or vertex $k$ in the corresponding quiver transforms it to a new quiver according to the following operations: • for every pair of arrows $i\rightarrow k$ and $k\rightarrow j$ , add a new arrow $i\rightarrow j$ , • reverse all arrows that target k or depart from k, • proceed with all two-cycle cancelation.

Under circumstances where there is no chromatic aberration, the potential energy remains constant at U0. If atoms deviate at an angle φ from the z-axis, the density profile shifts a distance δx approximately equal to U0φtf or Uxtf along the x-axis. This displacement should ideally be minute relative to the fundamental spot size w, which takes into account spherical aberration. We can leverage the previously computed, precise spot sizes and focal times for each θ0 to approximate the maximum allowable root mean square transverse velocity from this reasoning, Vrms(f) ≈ w/tf. This corresponds to an angular divergence φf = Vrms(f)/U0 ≈ w/(tfU0). It's anticipated that allowing Vrms to be Vrms(f) will at most double the spot size.
source_text: Finally, the atomic beam angular divergence has a crucial impact on the focus, independent of the chromatic aberration. For a beam with no chromatic aberration, $U=U_{0}$ , if atoms move at an angle $\varphi$ with respect to the $z$ -axis, the density profile displaces a distance $\delta x\sim U_{0}\varphi t_{f}\simeq U_{x}t_{f}$ along the $x$ axis. This displacement should ideally be much smaller than the fundamental spot size $w$ that accounts for spherical aberration. We can use the previously generated, exact spot sizes and focal times for each $\theta_{0}$ to estimate the maximum allowed $rms$ transverse velocity from this argument, $V_{rms}^{(f)}\approx w/t_{f}$ . This corresponds to an angular divergence $\varphi_{f}=V_{rms}^{(f)}/U_{0}\approx w/(t_{f}U_{0})$ . We expect that allowing $V_{rms}$ to be $V_{rms}^{(f)}$ will at most double the spot size.

source_text: Our basic assumption is that a gapped state of matter with short-range interactions can be put on a curved space-time of arbitrary topology, and that at long distances the partition function can be computed using field theory. This is likely to apply to a wide range of gapped systems, not just SPT phases. At short distances a system is usually defined on a regular lattice, with short-range interactions. However, if we allow for disorder, then dislocations in the lattice are possible, and more general triangulations also become possible. If the system admits a Euclidean lattice formulation, this applies both to space and time directions.

suspicious_text: We commence our analysis with a low-resolution simulation of a 1.4 Mpc box, identifying a prominent halo at a redshift of 20 and generating zoomed initial conditions with enhanced mass resolution around this object. The highest resolution region boasts a gas particle mass of 1.82M⊙ and a dark matter particle mass of 11.8M⊙. Utilizing the GADGET2 solver, we evolve the system from z=100 to the point at which the first collapsed gas cloud is detected, incorporating relevant atomic and molecular processes. A cold, dense cloud with a mass exceeding 300M⊙ is pinpointed as a potential site for star formation, nestled within a dark matter halo of 5×10^5M⊙. We then embed a 100M⊙ PopIII star at the cloud's densest point and initiate radiation, with the stellar mass and source spectrum informed by recent research on proto-stellar evolution and blackbody effective temperatures.
source_text: The TT02APD32 ASIC (henceforth TT02) is designed to read out 32 APD signal channels. Its main function is to condition the signal with a good signal-to-noise ratio and feed them into a Wilkinson-type analog-to-digital converter (ADC). The TT02 is fabricated based on the XFAB 0.35 $\mu$ m CMOS technology with 4-metal and MIM (Metal-Insulator-Metal) options, and assembled in a ceramic package of 160 pins (QC-160360-WZ, KYOCERA). The electrical architecture of the TT02 utilizes a property of the Open IP project, which has amassed technologies and the development of several radiation detectors (e.g., [5] ). In particular, since the large APD capacitance of $\sim$ 100 pF leads to a corresponding large capacitive noise, the TT02 implements an ingenious noise reduction scheme (see Section 2.5 ).

This is accomplished by adhering to the $\beta$-stability condition, which necessitates the equalization of chemical potentials for up, down, and strange quarks, as well as electrons and muons (i.e., $\mu_{u}+\mu_{e}=\mu_{d}=\mu_{s}$ , $\mu_{e}=\mu_{\mu}$). source_text: To evaluate the long-term stability of the proposed sensing approach, we recorded the time transition of the estimation errors for 30 days, as shown in Fig. 4 (d). Although the speckle pattern measurement is generally sensitive to environmental changes, particularly temperature change, our results reveal that the error increases only by 1.7 $\%$ over 30 days and does not significantly change for 5 days, suggesting the robustness of our measurement method. This is attributed to the fact that the network architecture is trained for temperature changes.

suspicious_text: This study does not simulate supernova explosions due to uncertainties surrounding the mechanical feedback efficiency of Population III stars with masses between 20-100M and the destructive potential of more massive stars on their surroundings. The efficacy of supernova explosions in destroying halos is also highly dependent on various factors, including explosion energy, initial density, and redshift, which are influenced by efficient Compton cooling in the early universe. As a result, the current research focuses on simulating H ii regions, with the investigation of supernova feedback effects to be pursued in future studies.
source_text: We do not trigger a supernova explosion in the simulations in the present paper for several reasons. First, the fate of Population III stars with mass $\sim 20-100M_{\odot}$ is rather uncertain in terms of their mechanical feedback efficiency (Umeda, Nomoto & Nakamura 2000; Heger & Woosley 2002), while very massive stars with $\sim 140-260M_{\odot}$ likely cause complete destruction of the surrounding structure (Bromm, Yoshida & Hernquist 2003). Kitayama & Yoshida (2005) show that the halo destruction efficiency of supernova explosions is sensitively dependent on the explosion energy, initial density of the explosion site and even on reshift, because of efficient Compton cooling in the early Universe. Therefore, it is necessary to use outputs of simulations of H ii regions, such as those presented in the present paper, for the study of supernova feedback effect. We will defer a consideration of these effects to future work.

source_text: We choose our infall mass bins as $10^{8.0}\,\mathrm{M_{\sun}}<M_{\rm{infall}}<10^{8.5}\,\mathrm{M_{\sun}}$ , $10^{8.5}\,\mathrm{M_{\sun}}<M_{\rm{infall}}<10^{9.1}\,\mathrm{M_{\sun}}$ , and $10^{9.1}\,\mathrm{M_{\sun}}<M_{\rm{infall}}<10^{10}\,\mathrm{M_{\sun}}$ to highlight interesting ranges of the halo mass dependence for each SIDM model, and to have enough satellites per bin to see trends of the average effect. Specifically, the mass $M_{\rm{infall}}<10^{9.1}\,\mathrm{M_{\sun}}$ is chosen to highlight a transition in tidal effects on the v-d models from significant to insignificant. On halo mass scales above $10^{10}\,\mathrm{M_{{\sun}}}$ , there are only $\sim 10$ subhaloes that ever enter the host. In these cases, the specific orbits of the satellites drive the differences in evolution more than SIDM, and cannot be averaged out. Additionally, dynamical friction drags massive haloes more rapidly to low pericentre orbits, leaving few examples with large orbits.

suspicious_text: The destiny of out-flowing gas is a crucial factor in determining the prompt triggering of star formation following the initial star-formation epoch, as it significantly influences the star-formation rate in more massive systems that form at later stages. The efficient re-incorporation of halo gas is essential for effective gas cooling, and our findings indicate that the recollapse of ionized gas occurs through a complex process. This complexity is attributed to the hierarchical assembly of dark matter halos, which can lead to changes in the mass hierarchy of halos over time. Our simulation illustrates this phenomenon, where the primary halo hosting the first star-forming cloud exhibits slow growth, while smaller halos grow more rapidly, and gas cooling and condensation occur at the center of a nearby halo, approximately 0.5 kpc away, at a later epoch.

Here, $a$ and $w$ are defined in terms of the electromagnetic potential, vector potential, and a scalar potential, and are abelian in nature, meaning they only influence the phases of the respective spinor components. suspicious_text: To further investigate the properties of the silicone material, we conducted an experiment involving indentation, examining its behavior under both increasing and decreasing pressure. Using a cylindrical indenter made of stainless steel with a contact diameter of 3 mm, we measured the force exerted on the material as its depth of indentation changed, with the results plotted in Fig. 1. Notably, the difference in the material's response when subjected to increasing versus decreasing force, known as hysteresis, was found to be minimal. By fitting the data to a fourth-order polynomial curve, which closely matched the experimental results with a root mean square error of 0.0016, we were able to accurately estimate the forces applied at specific indentation depths, as discussed in the main text, providing valuable insights into the material's mechanical characteristics.
source_text: We also performed an indentation experiment for the silicone material in both loading and unloading regimes. In this test, a stainless cylindrical indenter with the contact area diameter of 3 mm was used. The result is shown in Fig. 1 , where the force applied to the silicone material is displayed as a function of the indentation depth. Note that the hysteresis between the loading and unloading regimes in the experimental result was not significant. We fitted the experimental data with a fourth-order polynomial curve. The root mean square error was 0.0016. We estimated the applied forces corresponding to the measured indentation depth (shown in the main text) by using the fitting curve.

suspicious_text: The process of fractionation of deuterium hydride (HD) and hydrogen (H2) occurs due to several factors, including chemical reactions such as the formation of HD through the reaction between deuterium ions and hydrogen molecules. In the early universe, the abundance of H2 in diffuse neutral gas was relatively low, ranging from 10^-5 to 10^-6, whereas in collapsing halos, the H2 fraction was significantly higher, reaching levels of several times 10^-3. This disparity in H2 abundance facilitates the formation of HD molecules at low temperatures, as they are energetically favored due to their higher binding energy compared to H2 molecules. The equilibrium abundance ratio of HD to H2 is given by an equation that depends on the ratio of deuterium to hydrogen and the temperature, with HD production being favored at temperatures below 465 K.

suspicious_text: Various solutions to the cusp/core and "too big to fail" problems have been proposed that do not involve altering dark matter assumptions. For instance, supernova-driven outflows have been suggested as a mechanism to eliminate cusps, as outlined by Navarro et al. (1996) and Pontzen et al. (2015). However, resolving the "too big to fail" issue simultaneously would require an implausibly high number of supernovae, as noted by Garrison-Kimmel et al. (2013). Nonetheless, early supernovae might generate sufficiently large cores to address the problem, as proposed by Amorisco et al. (2014). Zolotov et al. (2012) and Brooks & Zolotov (2014) also support this idea, adding that tidal stripping could further reduce the central density of satellites, helping to explain observations of the Milky Way. Additionally, late and bursty star formation has been suggested to alleviate both cusp/core and "too big to fail" issues, as demonstrated by Oñorbe et al. For an AQC algorithm, we start with an initial state $|\phi_{0}\rangle$ that is a uniform superposition of all the states in the given search set,

 $\displaystyle H_{I}$ $\displaystyle=$ $\displaystyle{\bf 1}-|\phi_{0}\rangle\langle\phi_{0}|.$  (38) 
 The target Hamiltonian $H_{P}$ is then designed to have the solution state $|m\rangle$ as the ground state, 
 
 $\displaystyle H_{P}$ $\displaystyle=$ $\displaystyle{\bf 1}-|m\rangle\langle m|.$  (39) 
 The AQC is performed in the usual manner with a time-dependent Hamiltonian ${\cal H}_{G}(t)$ in the time interval $t\in[0,T]$ according to ( 4 ), 
 
 $\displaystyle{\cal H}_{G}(t)$ $\displaystyle=$ $\displaystyle f(t/T)H_{I}+g(t/T)H_{P}.$  (40)

source_text: We investigate the effects of self-interacting dark matter (SIDM) on the tidal stripping and evaporation of satellite galaxies in a Milky Way-like host. We use a suite of five zoom-in, dark-matter-only simulations, two with velocity-independent SIDM cross-sections, two with velocity-dependent SIDM cross-sections, and one cold dark matter (CDM) simulation for comparison. After carefully assigning stellar mass to satellites at infall, we find that stars are stripped at a higher rate in SIDM than in CDM. In contrast, the total bound dark matter mass-loss rate is minimally affected, with subhalo evaporation having negligible effects on satellites for viable SIDM models. Centrally located stars in SIDM haloes disperse out to larger radii as cores grow. Consequently, the half-light radius of satellites increases, stars become more vulnerable to tidal stripping, and the stellar mass function is suppressed. source_text: Rather than using the diffraction theory simply for numerical propagation of the matter waves, we extend its usefulness by deriving approximate analytical expressions for the SW field lens parameters. The Fresnel-Kirchhoff integral, incorporating the lowest-order spherical aberration, gives a relatively simple form for the atomic wave function near the focal plane and SW intensity extrema. Asymptotically for large atom-field pulse areas, this integral leads to a universal atomic density profile, revealing the behavior of the focal distance, peak atomic density, focal spot size [half-width at half-maximum (HWHM) of the atom density at the focus], depth of focus, and background density as a function of the pulse area. The analytical asymptotic results can then be compared to either numerical asymptotic approximations of the Fresnel integral or to the exact atomic distributions and lens parameters, most easily evaluated using a Fourier approach.

suspicious_text: SIDM presents a potential solution to the "too big to fail" problem and the cusp/core issues observed in satellite and field galaxies within the Local Group, as demonstrated by Vogelsberger et al. (2012). Particle scattering reduces the central density of dark matter halos, forming lower-density cores. Current constraints on velocity-independent SIDM suggest an interaction cross-section range of $0.1\leq\sigma/m_{x}\leq 1\,\mathrm{cm^{2}/g}$, as reported by Rocha et al. (2013). However, velocity-dependent SIDM models, where the cross-section scales approximately as $\sigma\propto 1/v^{\alpha}$ for some $\alpha$, remain largely unconstrained. This study investigates new signatures of SIDM in satellite galaxies and their interactions with host halos to aid in identifying or constraining SIDM.

suspicious_text: Within the context of viscous chromohydrodynamics, a theoretical framework is applied to derive the gluon self-energy in a quark-gluon plasma characterized by shear viscosity. This derivation enables the calculation of viscous electric permittivity and magnetic permeability, which are subsequently used to examine the refractive index. Numerical results show that the refractive index exhibits negative values over a specific frequency range, with the onset of this range coinciding with the pole of the electric permittivity and the termination point determined by the magnetic permeability pole. Furthermore, an increase in the ratio of shear viscosity to entropy density, denoted as $\eta/s$, is found to broaden the frequency range over which negative refraction occurs.
source_text: Under the framework of the viscous chromohydrodynamics, the gluon self-energy is derived for the quark-gluon plasma with shear viscosity. The viscous electric permittivity and magnetic permeability are evaluated from the gluon self-energy, through which the refraction index is investigated. The numerical analysis indicates that the refractive index becomes negative in some frequency range. The start point for that frequency range is around the electric permittivity pole, and the magnetic permeability pole determines the end point. As the increase of $\eta/s$ , the frequency range for the negative refraction becomes wider.

suspicious_text: Theoretical models based on quantum chromodynamics anticipate a phase transition to a quark-gluon plasma state at elevated temperatures and densities, prompting substantial research efforts at the Relativistic Heavy Ion Collider and Large Hadron Collider to verify this phenomenon. Notably, experiments at RHIC have yielded two key observations: firstly, the deconfined medium exhibits characteristics of a near-perfect fluid with low viscosity, which has been effectively modeled using viscous hydrodynamics to replicate observed outcomes such as elliptic flow and particle spectra; secondly, the significant suppression of jet formation, considered a potential indicator of quark-gluon plasma creation, has been observed. Furthermore, preliminary results from LHC experiments corroborate the findings from RHIC, providing additional evidence to support these conclusions.

A distinction is commonly made between methods that elucidate the model's inner workings, referred to as transparency methods, and post-hoc methods. As defined by the Oxford dictionary, post-hoc reasoning involves "an occurrence or action following an event, particularly with the mistaken belief that the event logically caused the occurrence in question." This contrasts with the pursuit of transparency, which seeks a direct and clear explanation of the model.

O’Shea et al (2005) also claim an overall positive feedback effect, but its strength is still uncertain because their calculation does not include directly the effects of photo-evaporation. Oh & Haiman (2003) argue that the residual entropy of relic HII regions implies that gas collapses into low-mass halos with much lower central densities, making H \textsubscript{2} much more vulnerable to UV photo-dissociation and resulting in negative feedback. source_text: We plot measurements of the equivalent noise charge (ENC) for the slow shapers of the TT02 in Figure 11 . We find that the ENC value depends on the amount of input charge, which may be related to the ADC time period $T_{\rm ADC}$ because shortening it by increasing the ADCK frequency gives lower ENC values, where the gradient of the RAMP signal is adjusted to keep the same ADC value with different ADCK frequencies. This might indicate that the $T_{\rm ADC}$ -dependence is caused by an accumulation of external noise in the HOLD signal during the $T_{\rm ADC}$ period. To minimize this feature, we adopt a 60-MHz ADCK when obtaining energy spectra.

source_text: Here, we adopt a current-mirror resistor circuit ( $R_{\rm 1}$ , $R_{\rm 2}$ , $R_{\rm 1}^{\prime}$ and $R_{\rm 2}^{\prime}$ ) to obtain a specified large resistance as illustrated in Figure 3 . The voltage difference between the IN/OUT nodes is converted into a current by the poly resistor sandwiched by two nMOS transistors. The circuit is configured to attenuate the current with the two stages of an asymmetric current mirror. As a result, the output current is substantially reduced to attain an effective resistance of a few M $\Omega$ . Another benefit of this resistor circuit is its high-Z input impedance. As a consequence, the amplifier located just before the resistor circuit does not feed any current. In addition, by feeding an adjustable constant current into the resistor, a coarse pedestal adjustment can be done.

source_text: In this paper, within the framework of the viscous chromohydrodynamics, the gluon self-energy has been evaluated in the QGP associated with shear viscosity, through which the electric permittivity and magnetic permeability have been derived. Based on the viscous $\varepsilon(\omega,k)$ and $\mu_{M}(\omega,k)$ , we have investigated the Depine-Lakhtakia index $n_{eff}$ and the refraction index $n$ in the viscous QGP. For comparison, we have also presented the corresponding HTL reslults. $n_{eff}<0$ implies the negative real part of $n$ , which signifies the negative refraction in the medium. The numerical analysis shows that i) the refraction index becomes negative in some frequency range; ii) the start point of that frequency range is around the pole of electric permittivity $\varepsilon(\omega,k)$ , and the magnetic permeability pole determines the end point; iii) with the increase of the $\eta/s$ , the frequency range for the negative refraction becomes broader. source_text: Recall that a manifold $M$ , whether oriented or not, carries a canonical principal ${\mathbb{Z}}_{2}$ bundle $\xi_{M}$ called the orientation bundle of $M$ . The holonomy of the associated ${\mathbb{Z}}_{2}$ -connection assigns $-1$ to all closed loops which reverse orientation and assigns $+1$ to all closed loops which do not. A closed manifold $M$ , whether oriented or not, has the fundamental class living in $H_{d}(M,{\mathbb{Z}}^{T})$ , where ${\mathbb{Z}}^{T}$ is a local system with fiber ${\mathbb{Z}}$ twisted by the orientation bundle. Its Pontryagin-dual is $H^{d}(M,U(1)^{T})$ , where $U(1)^{T}$ is a local system over $M$ with fiber $U(1)$ twisted by the orientation bundle.

suspicious_text: The refractive index is typically characterized by the relationship $n^{2}=\varepsilon(\omega,k)\mu_{M}(\omega,k)$, which is invariant under a simultaneous sign change of the electric permittivity and magnetic permeability. However, Veselago's proposition suggests that a concurrent change in the signs of $\varepsilon$ and $\mu_{M}$ from positive to negative values effectively switches the refractive index between two branches, namely $n=\sqrt{\varepsilon(\omega,k)\mu_{M}(\omega,k)}$ and $n=-\sqrt{\varepsilon(\omega,k)\mu_{M}(\omega,k)}$, thereby transitioning from a conventional to a negative refractive index. This phenomenon is rooted in the opposing directions of electromagnetic phase velocity and energy flow, with the condition for negative refraction being met when both the electric permittivity and magnetic permeability are simultaneously negative for a medium with real values of these parameters.
source_text: The refraction index is generally defined by the electric permittivity and magnetic permeability as $n^{2}=\varepsilon(\omega,k)\mu_{M}(\omega,k)$ which is a square definition and not sensitive to the simultaneous change of signs of $\varepsilon$ and $\mu_{M}$ . But it is proposed by Veselago that the simultaneous change of positive $\varepsilon$ and $\mu_{M}$ to negative $-\varepsilon$ and $-\mu_{M}$ corresponds to the transformation of the refraction index from one branch $n=\sqrt{\varepsilon(\omega,k)\mu_{M}(\omega,k)}$ to the other $n=-\sqrt{\varepsilon(\omega,k)\mu_{M}(\omega,k)}$ , ie, the turn from the general refraction index to the negative one. The physical nature of the negative refraction is that the electromagnetic phase velocity propagates opposite to the energy flow. For the detailed discussion on the physics of negative refraction, please refer to the Refs. (20) . suspicious_text: Finally, our investigation seeks to identify the telltale signs of SIDM imprinted on the stellar halo and assess the observable consequences of altered stellar and total mass-loss rates. To achieve this, we conduct simulations employing four distinct SIDM models to analyze their disruptive effects on satellite galaxies. By simulating these scenarios with dark matter only, we focus solely on the SIDM core formation process. While a comprehensive analysis would ideally incorporate baryonic matter alongside SIDM, such a task is computationally intensive and highly dependent on the implementation of baryonic feedback mechanisms, as noted by Vogelsberger et al. (2014). Therefore, a full suite of hydrodynamic simulations capable of resolving Milky Way-like substructures is beyond the current scope of this research.
source_text: Finally, we search for signatures of SIDM imprinted on the stellar halo, and observable implications of any changes in stellar and total mass-loss rates. With these goals in mind, we run simulations of four different models of SIDM to search for their impact on the disruption of satellites. By running our simulations with DM only, we isolate the effects of SIDM core formation. A complete picture would require including baryons in addition to SIDM, but such simulations are computationally more expensive and sensitive to the strength of the baryonic feedback implemented Vogelsberger et al. (2014) . As such, a suite of full hydrodynamic simulations with SIDM that resolve Milky Way-like substructure is beyond the scope of this paper.

In a theory describing a physical IHF, there must exist bulk states with the quantum numbers and properties of one-electron states. Suppose that the state $|(q^{*}\!,\lambda^{*}),z\rangle$ is obtained by adding a single electron at the point $z\in\Omega$ to the groundstate of the IHF. Then we have the following constraints: The charge of this state, see ( 45 ), must be

 Furthermore, the spin, see ( 50 ), must be half-integer, i.e., 
 
 $s_{q^{*}\!,\lambda^{*}}=\frac{(q^{*})^{2}}{2}+\Delta_{\lambda^{*}}=\frac{1}{2\sigma}+\Delta_{\lambda^{*}}=l+\frac{1}{2}$  (53) 
 with $l\in\mathbbm{Z}$ . From Vafa’s theorem we know that $\Delta_{\lambda^{*}}$ is rational. It thus follows that the Hall conductivity , $\sigma$ , is rational ,

suspicious_text: The illustrated pedestal distribution in Figure 10 (left) reveals a successful pedestal adjustment, falling within a range of ±2 ADC values. The unadjusted pedestal distribution presents a significant variation, reaching up to 300 channels (approximately 200 mV) due to a substantial offset voltage. This offset voltage is primarily attributed to the feedback transistors $R_{1}$, as depicted in Figure 2. Manufacturing inconsistencies result in disparate threshold voltages ($V_{\rm th}$) among the transistors surrounding the poly resistor, as well as imbalanced pairs within the current-mirror transistors. Furthermore, the variability in the gate-source voltage ($V_{\rm GS}$) of the CSA input transistor adds to the offset voltage through $R_{\rm pz}$. These factors collectively generate a large offset voltage. A potential solution to alleviate this imbalance could be the implementation of a common-centroid layout for the differential pair and current mirror transistors.

source_text: The paper is structured as follows. The methodology of the proposed strategy is described in Section 2 . Construction of the perturbation approximation functions for Finite Element Analysis is derived in Section 3 . The implementation into the Hybrid-Trefftz stress element formulation containing an arbitrary number of inclusions is presented in Section 4 . Section 5 comprises examples demonstrating the model’s performance. Finally we present the conclusions as well as a discussion on future research directions. An appendix is included that highlights some important, but rather technical, aspects of the proposed technique in order to keep the paper self-contained.
