suspicious_text: The cold dark matter (CDM) framework is a foundational model that effectively explains the large-scale structure of the Universe. By assuming that gravitational interactions govern the behavior of dark matter particles, the theoretical CDM model successfully accounts for halo mass functions, correlation functions, and the morphologies of large-scale structures such as galaxies, galaxy clusters, and filaments (for a detailed review, see Frenk & White 2012). However, on smaller scales, discrepancies between CDM predictions and observational data persist, particularly concerning the properties of substructure and dwarf galaxies. For example, while simulations predict dark matter haloes with steep central density cusps (Navarro et al. 1997), observations of low surface brightness galaxies (de Blok & McGaugh 1997), low-mass spiral galaxies (Gentile et al. 2004), and Milky Way dwarf spheroidal galaxies (Walker & Peñarrubia 2011) reveal lower central densities and more diffuse, or cored, inne
source_text: It has been proposed that bosonic SPT phases in space-time dimension $d$ are classified by the degree- $d$ cohomology group of $BG_{0}$ with $U(1)$ coefficients [2] . Here $G_{0}$ is a finite symmetry group \footnote{ 1 A version suitable for compact Lie group symmetries has also been proposed in [2] , but we will limit ourselves to finite groups here.} which is purely internal, i.e. acts trivially on space-time, and $BG_{0}$ is its classifying space. The space $BG_{0}$ classifies $G_{0}$ bundles in the sense that isomorphism classes of principal $G_{0}$ bundles on any space $X$ are in one-to-one correspondence with homotopy classes of maps from $X$ to $BG_{0}$ . Cohomology of $BG_{0}$ is also known as group cohomology of $G_{0}$ .

source_text: Let $V^{\prime}\subset[n]$ and let $G=(V^{\prime},E^{\prime})$ be a functional graph. An $n$ -extension of $G$ is a graph $H$ with vertex set $[n]$ which is generated according to the following procedure: (1) Start with $V_{0}=V^{\prime}$ and $E_{0}=E^{\prime}$ and $i=0$ . (2) Let $w$ be the smallest element in $[n]\setminus V_{i}$ . Let $X_{w}=1$ with probability $1/(|E_{i}|+1)$ and let $X_{w}=0$ otherwise. Then do the following: (a) If $X_{w}=1$ , add $w$ to the graph as an isolated vertex with a single loop, that is $V_{i+1}=V_{i}\cup\{w\}$ and $E_{i+1}=E_{i}\cup\{ww\}$ . (b) If $X_{w}=0$ , choose an edge $xy\in E_{i}$ uniformly at random. Set $V_{i+1}=V_{i}\cup\{w\}$ and $E_{i+1}=(E_{i}\setminus\{xy\})\cup\{xv,vw\}$ . (3) If $V_{i}=[n]$ set $H=(V_{i},E_{i})$ . Otherwise, increase $i$ by one and return to step 2.

source_text: We have studied the formation and structure of early cosmological H ii regions using three-dimensional radiation hydrodynamics simulations. We have also examined in detail the formation of second-generation stars, by following cooling and condensation of primordial gas in relic H ii regions. With the three-dimensional simulations, we can eliminate a number of uncertain assumptions regarding the formation of early H ii regions. First, the simulations allow us to directly calculate the ionizing photon escape fraction from cosmological minihalos. The corresponding EOS at highest densities is then expected to be reproduced by the perturbation model (pQCD) explained in Sec. III.2 . The NJL model, on the other hand, is a low-energy model for QCD, where the gluons are integrated out while retaining only local quark interactions. The corresponding coupling constants of NJL model are then fixed by reproducing the masses of $\pi$ , $K$ , $\eta^{\prime}$ and the $\pi$ decay constant Hatsuda and Kunihiro (1994) . The equivparticle model carries similar traits of quasiparticle model Wenet al.(2012) , where the results of pQCD at highest densities can be reproduced with the parameter $C$ in Eq. ( 13 ) depending explicitly on $\alpha_{\mathrm{s}}$ Xiaet al.(2014) . suspicious_text: The exchange of energy $T$ between the bulk and the brane leads to a violation of conventional energy conservation. Our analysis will focus on a universe comprising two dark components, namely dark matter and dark energy, with a total energy density given by $\rho=\rho_{m}+\rho_{de}$. The interaction between the bulk and the brane will disrupt the adiabatic equation, affecting either the dark matter or the dark energy component. We will examine both scenarios in turn, assuming $\lambda=0$ and $k=0$ for the purposes of our discussion.
source_text:
SIDM may offer a resolution to the too big to fail problem and cusp/core issues of satellite and field galaxies in the Local Group Vogelsberger et al. (2012) . Scattering between particles drives mass away from dark matter halo central density cusps, creating lower density cores. Currently, constraints on velocity-independent SIDM places the interesting range of the interaction cross-section at $0.1\leq\sigma/m_{x}\leq 1\,\mathrm{cm^{2}/g}$ Rocha
et al. (2013) . However, the class of velocity-dependent SIDM models, in which the cross-section scales approximately as $\sigma\propto 1/v^{\alpha}$ for some $\alpha$ , remain largely unconstrained. In this paper, we search for new signatures of SIDM on satellite galaxies and their interactions with a host halo to ultimately aid in identifying or constraining SIDM.

Furthermore, the density of generalized dark radiation can be derived from these relations, yielding a form that depends on $a$ and the constants $D_1$, $T_0$, and $n$, ultimately influencing the structure of the Friedmann equation that describes the universe's expansion.

source_text: Thin lens focusing becomes especially effective for a large field area $\theta$ . When $\sqrt{\theta}\gg 1$ for the monovelocity case, using the Fresnel-Kirchhoff diffraction integral, we can consider only the first anharmonic term of the phase shift created by the SW light field when expanding around the potential wells at $x^{\prime}=2\pi m$ . This procedure, labeled Method 2, leads to a manageable expression ( II B ) for the atomic density distribution which can be used to evaluate the focal parameters by numerical integration.

Exact quantum expressions for the Fourier components of the density (that include all spherical aberration) are used to study the focusing numerically. The following lens parameters and density profiles are calculated as functions of the pulsed field area $\theta$ : the position of the focal plane, peak atomic density, atomic density pattern at the focus, focal spot size, depth of focus, and background density. The lens parameters are compared to asymptotic, analytical results derived from a scalar diffraction theory for which spherical aberration is small but non-negligible ( $\theta\gg 1$ ). suspicious_text: Our research has utilized three-dimensional radiation hydrodynamics simulations to investigate the formation and structure of early cosmological H ii regions, as well as the development of second-generation stars through the cooling and condensation of primordial gas in relic H ii regions. By leveraging these simulations, we have been able to mitigate uncertainties associated with the formation of early H ii regions, including the direct calculation of ionizing photon escape fractions from cosmological minihalos and the self-consistent incorporation of gas clumping effects. Notably, our simulations yield photon escape fractions approaching unity for cases involving massive central stars, with the accuracy of our radiation transfer scheme validated against prior one-dimensional calculations, thereby confirming the suitability of our methodology for analyzing early H ii regions.
source_text: We have studied the formation and structure of early cosmological H ii regions using three-dimensional radiation hydrodynamics simulations. We have also examined in detail the formation of second-generation stars, by following cooling and condensation of primordial gas in relic H ii regions. With the three-dimensional simulations, we can eliminate a number of uncertain assumptions regarding the formation of early H ii regions. First, the simulations allow us to directly calculate the ionizing photon escape fraction from cosmological minihalos. With enough mass resolution, our calculation self-consistently includes the effect of gas clumping. We obtain photon escape fractions of order unity, for the cases with rather massive ( $M_{*}>60M_{\odot}$ ) central stars. While our radiation tranfer scheme is based on a few approximations, the accuracy of the method is tested against the results of previous one-dimensional calculations (KYSU; Whalen et al. 2004). suspicious_text: -based classification systems (FRBCS) are a type of fuzzy inference system that combines the principles of fuzzy logic with machine learning techniques to perform classification tasks. In these systems, fuzzy rules are used to model the relationships between input variables and output classes, allowing for the representation of uncertainty and imprecision inherent in many real-world problems. FRBCS has been widely applied in various domains, such as pattern recognition, medical diagnosis, and financial analysis, due to its ability to handle vague and ambiguous information effectively. The development of FRBCS often involves the use of techniques like fuzzy c-means clustering, genetic algorithms, and particle swarm optimization to optimize the fuzzy rule base and improve classification accuracy.

For a beam with no chromatic aberration, $U=U_{0}$ , if atoms move at an angle $\varphi$ with respect to the $z$ -axis, the density profile displaces a distance $\delta x\sim U_{0}\varphi t_{f}\simeq U_{x}t_{f}$ along the $x$ axis. This displacement should ideally be much smaller than the fundamental spot size $w$ that accounts for spherical aberration. We can use the previously generated, exact spot sizes and focal times for each $\theta_{0}$ to estimate the maximum allowed $rms$ transverse velocity from this argument, $V_{rms}^{(f)}\approx w/t_{f}$ . This corresponds to an angular divergence $\varphi_{f}=V_{rms}^{(f)}/U_{0}\approx w/(t_{f}U_{0})$ . We expect that allowing $V_{rms}$ to be $V_{rms}^{(f)}$ will at most double the spot size.

A detailed examination of the proofs is provided in Sections 3 and 4, with the former focusing on cases where the second moment of the indegree sequence is substantially large, while the latter addresses the remaining scenarios, excluding a few negligible exceptions. source_text: Our main results are stated in Section 2 after some basic definitions. In particular we give the limiting distribution of the six-length for functional graphs with given indegree sequence, and also asymptotics for the moments of the distribution, as well as the joint distribution of the tail- and six-lengths. Proofs for the case that the second moment of the indegree sequence is “large” are given in Section 3 , and for the remaining case (except for some almost trivial cases) in Section 4 . See also Konyagin, Luca, Mans, Mathieson, Sha and Shparlinski [5] for a study of polynomials over finite fields considering similar aspects, such as largest component and tree size of the associated functional digraphs. Similar to [6] , they observe, in [5] , that the in-degree sequence of these random digraphs is distributed rather differently from that of uniformly random functional digraphs.

By harnessing the sensitivity of these patterns to various stimuli, it is possible to extract a wealth of information that would be difficult to obtain using traditional single-mode sensors, such as those designed to measure pressure or temperature. As mentioned above, the speckle patterns are highly sensitive to various external stimuli on the soft material, i.e., they contain the information of various external stimuli. This method has demonstrated the ability to extract key characteristics of many-body quantum states, such as entanglement structures (25), subsystem purities (30), fidelities with target states or other quantum systems (18), and interference signatures of indistinguishable particles (23). Additionally, the measurement resources required for a statistically robust verification of these properties have been analyzed, highlighting potential advantages, particularly in the noisy intermediate-scale regime (41).

suspicious_text: Interpretability is widely recognized as a critical aspect of AI models, yet there is no统一 standard for assessing interpretation techniques. A common distinction is made between methods that explain the model's internal processes, referred to as transparency methods, and those that provide explanations after the fact, known as post-hoc methods. According to the Oxford dictionary, post-hoc reasoning is defined as "occurring or done after the event, especially with reference to the fallacious assumption that the occurrence in question has a logical relationship with the event it follows." This contrasts with the pursuit of transparency, which focuses on directly clarifying the model's operations.

source_text: In this contribution, we present an introduction to the physical principles underlying the quantum Hall effect. The field theoretic approach to the integral and fractional effect is sketched, with some emphasis on the mechanism of electromagnetic gauge anomaly cancellation by chiral degrees of freedom living on the edge of the sample. Applications of this formalism to the design and theoretical interpretation of interference experiments are outlined.

This critical radius serves as a demarcation line between stable and unstable spinning exotic compact configurations with rotation parameter ${\bar{a}}$. Particularly, horizonless compact objects with rotation parameter ${\bar{a}}$ and reflecting surfaces that meet the condition $r_{\text{c}}<r^{\text{max}}_{\text{c}}({\bar{a}})$ are prone to instability due to scalar perturbation modes, whereas horizonless compact objects with reflecting surfaces that satisfy $r_{\text{c}}>r^{\text{max}}_{\text{c}}({\bar{a}})$ display stability.

suspicious_text: In the interaction, atoms experience an additional, spatially-modulated phase shift, denoted as $\theta^{2}\omega_{q}\tau\cos\left(2qx\right)/24$ by Henkel and colleagues [37], referred to as the WKB correction. This phase shift originates from the harmonic motion of the atoms during the interaction, viewed through a semiclassical lens. From a quantum mechanical standpoint, these amplitude and phase modifications are a direct result of the kinetic energy acquired during the interaction. The wave function, $\psi\left(x,t\right)=\exp[-i2\left|\chi\right|^{2}t\cos\left(qx\right)/\Delta],$ evolving in the SW field, yields the correction that multiplies $\psi\left(x,\tau/2\right),$ as expressed in equations (17) and (18). This correction ensures the rigorously accurate result. In the context of far-field diffraction, the momentum state wave function may deviate substantially from the spectrum of Eq. (12) if $\theta^{2}\omega_{q}\tau/24\gtrsim 1$, even if condition (14) is met.

(2013), the core radii of SIDM satellite galaxies can be approximated by identifying the radius at which particle interactions occur roughly once over the Hubble time—corresponding to approximately 10 billion years since halo formation. This approach effectively predicts core sizes for SIDM1 simulations. However, Rocha et al. utilized a Burkert profile (Burkert, 1995) to model haloes, employing the Burkert scale radius, \( r_{\rm{b}} \), as the core size metric for SIDM1. They noted that this alignment between the Burkert profile and SIDM1 results is likely fortuitous and may not hold for other models. In contrast, we define core sizes as the radius where the density profile of an SIDM satellite deviates from that of a CDM satellite under otherwise similar conditions. This definition allows for a precise examination of where binding energy discrepancies arise due to SIDM effects.
source_text:
As discussed in Rocha
et al. (2013) , the radius of the cores in SIDM satellites can be estimated by the radius at which particles are expected to scatter once per Hubble time, or approximately once per $10$ Gyr halo lifetime since haloes first form after a few Gyr. They find this predicts the magnitude of core sizes well for the equivalent of our SIDM1 simulation. However, Rocha
et al. (2013) fit haloes with a Burkert profile Burkert (1995) and use the Burkert scale radius, $r_{\rm{b}}$ , to define the core size for SIDM1, commenting that it is likely a lucky coincidence that this works for SIDM1, and would not work as well for other models. We instead choose to define core sizes as the radius at which the density profile of an SIDM satellite diverges from that of a CDM satellite with otherwise equivalent conditions. This definition then accurately probes where binding energies will differ due to SIDM.

source_text: In this paper we extend the results from [26] and combine them with those from [12] . The novelty is two-fold: we construct i) waves with arbitrarily many critical layers, and ii) families of waves bifurcating from kernels with two different Fourier modes. The latter type of wave displays a more complicated surface profile than those of Stokes waves (cf. Figure 2 , p. 2 ). This qualitatively different behavior arises not as a result of involved rotational flow, but the vorticity functions are linear maps. Thus, the paper at hand is a natural extension of [26] , which dealt with flows of constant vorticity. We settle here for the mere existence of such waves. A qualitative investigation of the small-amplitude waves corresponding to the first Fourier mode will be given in a separate study [11] .

suspicious_text: The TT02 ASIC, referred to hereafter as TT02, is engineered to handle 32 APD signal channels. Its primary function is to enhance the signal-to-noise ratio and transmit the conditioned signals to a Wilkinson-type analog-to-digital converter (ADC). The TT02 is manufactured using XFAB 0.35 micron CMOS technology, featuring 4-metal and MIM (Metal-Insulator-Metal) options, and housed in a 160-pin ceramic package (QC-160360-WZ, KYOCERA). The TT02's electrical design leverages a technology from the Open IP project, which has accumulated various radiation detector technologies, such as [5]. Notably, due to the significant APD capacitance of approximately 100 pF resulting in substantial capacitive noise, the TT02 incorporates a clever noise reduction mechanism, as detailed in Section 2.5.
source_text: On the basis of these two examples we propose that in general $S^{\alpha}_{M}$ is cobordism-invariant. More precisely, In the case when the symmetry group $G_{0}$ is internal (does not involve time-reversal), the gauge field $A$ can be thought of as a map ${\mathcal{A}}:M\rightarrow BG_{0}$ . Thus the pair $(M,{\mathcal{A}})$ can be thought of as an oriented bordism to $BG_{0}$ . We propose that the effective action depends only on the equivalence class of this bordism. The partition function $\exp(2\pi iS^{\alpha}_{M}(A))$ then can be thought of as a map from $\Omega_{d,SO}(BG_{0})$ to $U(1)$ . This map is multiplicative under the disjoint union of bordisms and therefore is a homomorphism of groups. Identifying actions which differ only by the value of continuous parameters is equivalent to taking the quotient of ${\rm Hom}(\Omega_{d,SO}(BG_{0}),U(1))$ by the image of ${\rm Hom}(\Omega_{d,SO}(BG_{0}),{\mathbb{R}})$ .

suspicious_text: The impact of meta-galactic ionizing radiation on star formation in high-redshift galaxies is expected to be significant, with research suggesting that both global UV background radiation and local UV sources play a crucial role in regulating dwarf galaxy formation. Following global reionization, the formation of H2 molecules is substantially suppressed, limiting star formation to high-mass halos where gas cooling occurs through atomic processes. Moreover, even moderate-intensity photo-dissociating radiation can delay star formation in relic H ii regions for tens of millions of years, and the subsequent arrival of ionizing photons from global or local UV sources can further prolong this delay, highlighting the complex interplay between these factors in shaping the star formation process.

To tackle this, a strategic approach is employed, involving the systematic removal of edges connected to nodes with a degree of 1, progressively simplifying the graph until it meets a specific criterion, denoted as (A+). Notably, as the graph undergoes this transformation, its six-length property, as described, converges towards a standard Rayleigh distribution, as asserted by Proposition 3.1. Ultimately, by leveraging concentration inequalities, it becomes possible to derive a limiting theorem that applies to the original, unmodified graph.
source_text: In this section we discuss how to extend Theorem 2.1 to degree sequences with small coalescence, that is sequences with $\sigma^{2}(\textbf{d}_{n})=\mathrm{O}(n^{-1/3}\log n)$ and $n\sigma^{2}(\textbf{d}_{n})\rightarrow\infty$ . The key idea is to contract edges incident to vertices with degree $1$ until we obtain a reduced graph that satisfies ( A+ ). The six-length of this reduced graph converges to a standard Rayleigh distribution by Proposition3.1 . Finally, a concentration argument will allow us to deduce a limit theorem for the original graph.

source_text: Quantum chromodynamics (QCD) predicts that deconfined phase transition will take place at high temperature and/or high density, as a result the nuclear matter will undergo a transition to quark-gluon plasma (QGP). One main goal for Relativistic Heavy Ion Collider (RHIC) and Large Hadronic Collider (LHC) is to seek this new state of matter. There are two striking findings at RHIC. One is that the deconfined hot medium behaviors as a nearly perfect fluid with a small viscosity (1) . Several groups have applied viscous hydrodynamics to simulate the evolution of the produced matter in heavy ion collisions. The simulations successfully fit the observables at RHIC, such as the elliptic flow, the particle spectra, etc (5) . The other is the strong jet quenching, which is believed to be a potential signal for the QGP formation (14) . It should be stressed that the first LHC results also strongly support similar conclusions as seen at RHIC (15) .

suspicious_text: Our radiative transfer calculations employ a two-stage approach, wherein photon arrival times are computed and assigned to surrounding gas particles for a given point radiation source. This involves defining a photon ray as a straight line connecting the source to a target particle and calculating local densities and smoothing lengths at multiple evaluation points along this path using a smoothed particle hydrodynamics (SPH) methodology. An ionization front is then advanced along the path, with the position of each subsequent evaluation point determined by the condition that the segment length between points is less than the local smoothing length, ensuring minimal density variation. The I-front crossing time between adjacent evaluation points is computed by integrating the jump condition for the I-front position, taking into account the photon emission rate and recombination effects, as described by equation (15).

source_text: Since the PZC circuit does not include a time constant, the shaping amplifier is configured as a second-order low-pass filter, and the entire transfer function, including the PZC circuit, is equivalent to an ordinary CR–RC filter. The peaking time of the slow shaper is determined by a set of $R_{\rm 1}$ , $R_{\rm 2}$ , $C_{\rm 1}$ , and $C_{\rm 2}$ . To obtain critical damping in order to reduce undershooting of the output signal, the peaking time $\tau_{\rm peak,slow}$ = $C_{\rm 1}R_{\rm 1}$ = 4 $C_{\rm 2}R_{\rm 2}$ must be satisfied for the slow shaper. Similarly for the fast shaper, we impose $\tau_{\rm peak,fast}$ = $C_{\rm 1}^{\prime}R_{\rm 1}^{\prime}$ = 4 $C^{\prime}_{\rm 2}R_{\rm 2}^{\prime}$ on the peaking time. At the output stage of the slow shaper, the pulse height is acquired on a capacitor $C_{\rm H}$ at the timing of the HOLD signal, called the sample and hold (S/H).

Representative objects are shown in Figure 2, with V-band grayscale images and contours. The contours are in linear increments; the lowest contour level is 1 $\sigma$ of the pixel noise, which corresponds to a surface brightness of 25.5 mag arcsec \textsuperscript{-2} .

suspicious_text: Molecular dynamics simulations have been conducted on glass-forming fluids comprising anisotropic particles. These fluids can consist of a single component with intricate internal structures, such as methanol (10), ortho-terphenyl methanol (OTP) (11), and asymmetric dumbbell fluids (13). Additionally, various two-component glass-formers exist, with the simplest being a mixture of two symmetric dumbbell species (15). Recently, we investigated a mixture of spheroidal and spherical particles using an orientation-dependent potential to explore orientational glass (18).
source_text: Molecular dynamics simulations have also been performed on glass-forming fluids composed of anisotropic particles. They can be one-component fluids with a complex internal structure. Examples are methanol (10) , ortho-terphenyl methanol(OTP) (11) , and fluids of asymmetric dumbbells (13) . There are various kinds of two-component glass-formers. The simplest example is a mixture of two species of symmetric dumbbells (15) . Recently, we studied a mixture of spheroidal and spherical particles to examine the orientational glass using an orientation-dependent potential (18) .

suspicious_text: We presented numerical analyses of the introduced protocols by examining the multiparticle concurrence of the specified quantum states and ensembles of output states generated by various random quantum circuits, including matchgate and IQP circuits. Additionally, we explored the impact of noise, specifically single- and two-qubit gate errors, on the multiparticle entanglement of these states, thereby demonstrating the practical utility of the derived results in the noisy intermediate-scale quantum computing regime. Importantly, our findings reveal that the required measurement resources exhibit a significant dependence on the specific class of quantum states being analyzed, which underscores the necessity of conducting detailed statistical analyses to validate our approach.
source_text: We demonstrated the introduced protocols by numerically analysing the multiparticle concurrence of the aforementioned class of quantum states, as well as for ensembles of output states of different classes of random quantum circuits, such as matchgate and IQP circuits. Finally, we investigated the influence of noise in terms of single- and two-qubit gate errors on the multiparticle entanglement of the states under consideration and thereby showed that the obtained results on the required measurement resources prove useful in the noisy intermediate-scale regime. However, we also show that the required measurement resources strongly depend on the underlying class of quantum states under consideration showing that a detailed statistical analyses was justified.

To meet the requirements of the International Large Detector at the International Linear Collider, which typically demands a lateral segmentation of 5 × 5 mm^2 for the electromagnetic calorimeter, a significant number of channels - on the order of 10^8 - would be necessary. However, this number can be substantially reduced to 10^7 by utilizing scintillator strips with a lateral dimension of 45 × 5 mm^2, arranged in orthogonal layers.
source_text: In the future linear collider experiments the particle flow approach (PFA) is one of the most potential method to have high performance jet physics. For the PFA, a granular sampling electromagnetic calorimeter (ECAL) is mandatory [1] . With 5 $\times$ 5 mm \textsuperscript{2} lateral segmentation typically required to the ECAL in the International Large Detector (ILD) [2] for the International Linear Collider (ILC), the number of channels becomes $10^{8}$ , while it can be reduced to $10^{7}$ with scintillator strips of 45 $\times$ 5 mm \textsuperscript{2} lateral shape aligned orthogonally in successive layers.

suspicious_text: The article discusses the development and evaluation of a mixed-signal ASIC specifically designed for avalanche photodiodes (APDs) to detect hard X-ray emissions across a wide energy spectrum on the International Space Station. Utilizing Ce:GAGG scintillators, each paired with an APD, and low-noise front-end electronics, the system is capable of detecting energies as low as 20 keV. The ASIC is capable of reading 32-channel APD signals using 0.35 $\mu$ m CMOS technology and has an analog amplifier at the input stage to suppress capacitive noise from large APD detector capacitance. The ASIC boasts a performance of 2099 $e^{-}$ + 1.5 $e^{-}$ /pF at root mean square (RMS) with a wide 300 fC dynamic range. The combination of a reverse-type APD with a Ce:GAGG scintillator offers an energy resolution of 6.7% (FWHM) at 662 keV and a minimum detectable energy of 20 keV at 20 \textsuperscript{∘} C. suspicious_text: The V-D models exhibit negligible influence due to their minimal interaction cross-sections in haloes with mass scales exceeding $10^{10}\,\mathrm{M_{\sun}}$. The host halo develops a very small core, causing minor disruptions to satellite orbits and the spherically averaged dark matter dynamics in the outer regions. As detailed in Sections 5 and 6, satellites with $M_{\rm{infall}}>10^{10}\,\mathrm{M_{\sun}}$ exhibit a low $r_{\rm{core}}/r_{\rm{t}}$ ratio, resulting in minimal enhancement of stellar stripping and no subhalo evaporation. However, approximately $90\%$ of the stellar mass accreted by the host originates from these satellites. Consequently, the stellar halo is predominantly shaped by a few large satellites that remain largely unaffected by SIDM, within a host halo potential that undergoes minimal alteration, leading to no significant global modifications to the stellar halo.

source_text: In this paper, we can not determine shear viscosity coefficient itself in viscous chromohydrodynamics, but regard it as an input parameter to study the viscous effect on the refraction index of the QGP. A small value of the ratio for shear viscosity to entropy density $\eta/s\leq 0.2$ has been deduced from comparison of casual viscous hydrodynamic simulation results with the RHIC data (13) , which is less than three times of the famous bound result $\eta/s=\frac{1}{4\pi}$ of the strongly couple conformal field theory determined by the AdS/CFT correspondence (49) . Numerical results of the refraction index are presented with those explicit values of $\eta/s$ . In addition, in numerical analysis, such scales $k=0.2\omega_{p}$ and $T=\omega_{p}$ are used to study the $\omega$ -dependent behavior of the refraction index.

suspicious_text: The process of fractionation in deuterium hydride and hydrogen molecules occurs due to a multitude of factors. A key mechanism underlying this phenomenon, as illustrated in the scenario of an isobarically cooling gas, involves the reaction between deuterium ions and hydrogen molecules, yielding hydrogen ions and deuterium hydride, a process without an analogous counterpart for hydrogen molecules. Notably, the proportion of hydrogen molecules in the diffuse neutral gas present in the early universe is remarkably low, ranging from approximately $10^{-5}$ to $10^{-6}$, as reported by Stancil et al. and Galli & Palla in 1998. In stark contrast, the universal proportion of hydrogen molecules within collapsing halos is substantially higher, reaching levels of several times $10^{-3}$, according to research conducted by Susa et al. in 1998 and Oh & Haiman in 2002. source_text: Fractionation of [HD/H \textsubscript{2} ] occurs for various reasons. In the above example of an isobarically cooling gas, one of the main formation path is the reaction ${\rm D}^{+}+{\rm H}_{2}\rightarrow{\rm H}^{+}+{\rm HD}$ , for which there is no counterpart for H \textsubscript{2} . The H \textsubscript{2} fraction in the diffuse neutral gas in the early universe is $\sim 10^{-5}-10^{-6}$ (Stancil et al. 1998; Galli & Palla 1998), whereas the universal H \textsubscript{2} fraction in the collapsing halo is as large as a few times $10^{-3}$ (Susa et al. 1998; Oh & Haiman 2002). Hence, the large H \textsubscript{2} fraction promotes the formation of HD molecules at low temperatures. In other words, HD is energetically favored because the binding energy of HD molecules is higher than that of H \textsubscript{2} molecules. source_text: In this section, we first present some basic facts about normal (not necessarily smooth) surfaces that will be used in the sequel. Although none of them is new, it is hard to find a good reference. Then we focus on surfaces associated to line arrangements.

suspicious_text: It is suggested that Symmetry Protected Topological Phases (SPTs) with a finite symmetry group G can be classified by the cobordism groups of the classifying space of G. This approach offers an understanding for the recent emergence of bosonic SPT phases that do not align with the group cohomology classification. The relationship between the cobordism classification of SPT phases and gauge and gravitational anomalies in various dimensions is elaborated upon.

suspicious_text: The topological actions associated with these SPT phases can be written as integrals of polynomial functions of the Stiefel-Whitney classes (SWCs). These SWCs, $w_j \in H^j(M, \mathbb{Z}_2)$, exist for all integers $j$ within the range $0 < j \leq d$, where $d$ is the dimension of the manifold $M$. The lower SWCs have a straightforward geometrical interpretation. For instance, $w_1$ represents an orientation connection on $M$, while $w_2$ denotes an obstruction to establishing a spin structure on $M$. The integral of the top SWC, $w_d(M)$, equals the Euler characteristic modulo 2. There are dependencies among the product of SWCs that rely on $d$. Thom's theorem indicates that the unoriented bordism class of $M$ is determined by the SW numbers of $M$ (integrals of polynomial functions of SWCs over $M$). Considering the relationships among SWCs results in the stated result for the unoriented cobordism groups of a point.
source_text: One can write down explicitly the topological actions corresponding to all these SPT phases as integrals of polynomials of the Stiefel-Whitney classes. These are special cohomology classes $w_{j}\in H^{j}(M,{\mathbb{Z}}_{2})$ which exist for all $j$ in the range $0<j\leq d=\dim M$ . The lowest ones have a transparent geometric meaning. For example, $w_{1}$ is a connection on the orientation bundle of $M$ , while $w_{2}$ is the obstruction to having a spin structure on $M$ . The integral of the top class $w_{d}(M)$ is the Euler characteristic modulo $2$ . There are relations between products of Stiefel-Whitney classes whose form depends on $d$ . Thom’s theorem says that the unoriented bordism class of $M$ is determined by the Stiefel-Whitney numbers of $M$ (i.e. integrals of polynomials of Stiefel-Whitney classes over $M$ ). Taking into account the relations between Stiefel-Whitney classes leads to the above result for the unoriented cobordism groups of a point.

suspicious_text: The effects of velocity-dependent cross-sections remain uncertain, and velocity-independent models remain plausible. To address this, we propose a novel approach to observe these effects. Specifically, we examine how the presence of a core impacts the tidal stripping of stars in satellite galaxies of a Milky Way-like host. Both the reduced central dark matter density and the elevated central velocity dispersion occur within the stellar regions, influencing the dynamics of tidal stripping and streams (e.g., Bovy 2014). While Peñarrubia et al. (2010) showed that cored satellites lose mass faster than cusped ones, they manually assigned cores and did not consider SIDM. Additionally, they focused on tidal interactions with the host galaxy's disc, whereas our study primarily investigates tidal forces originating from the host halo.
source_text:
Since velocity-dependent cross-sections are as of yet not well constrained, and velocity-independent models are still viable, we seek to highlight a new approach where their effects could be observed. First, we investigate whether the presence of a core influences how stars in satellites of a Milky Way-like host are tidally stripped. The reduced central density of dark matter in satellites and increased central velocity dispersion both occur where stars reside. These two effects should have an impact on the stripping and distribution of stars based on the dynamics of tidal stripping and tidal steams (see Bovy (2014) for instance). Peñarrubia
et al. (2010) have already demonstrated that cored satellites lose mass more quickly than cusped ones. They, however, assign cores manually and do not consider SIDM. They also emphasize tidal interactions of satellites with the disc of their host galaxy, whereas we predominantly study tidal forces arising from the host halo only.

To achieve an accurate assessment of common-mode noise, it is essential that the baseline levels for each channel are uniform. However, the initial baseline levels for the individual channels may exhibit some disparity. To address this discrepancy, we utilize coarse 4-bit digital-to-analog converters (DACs) incorporated in the resistor networks $R_{\rm 1}$ and $R_{\rm 1}^{\prime}$ , as well as fine 5-bit DACs comprising a resistor and a variable current source situated at the rear ends of the rapid and slow shapers. This configuration, as depicted in Figure 2, is instrumental in readjusting the baseline levels to ensure uniformity across all channels.
source_text: The TT02 can detect common-mode noise by obtaining a median of the 32-channel ADC values. To estimate the common-mode noise properly, the pedestal levels for every channel should be the same while the original pedestal levels for each channel have some variation. To adjust the pedestal levels, we employ coarse 4-bit current DACs implemented in the resistor circuits $R_{\rm 1}$ and $R_{\rm 1}^{\prime}$ , and fine 5-bit voltage DACs consisting of a resistor and a variable current source located at the back-end of the fast and slow shapers, as illustrated in Figure 2 .

We then compute the time derivative of the norm squared of the difference between the two states, given by equation (14), and substitute equation (13) to obtain equation (15). Furthermore, we rewrite the left-hand side of equation (14) as equation (16) and combine it with equation (15) to arrive at inequality (17), which provides an upper bound for the time derivative of the norm of the difference between the two states in terms of the norm of the difference between the Hamiltonian and a time-dependent parameter, $\beta(t)$, acting on the initial state $|\phi_{0}\rangle$.
source_text: However, we can propose some adjustments in this architecture: the causal links given by the KB do not directly reflect the operations that took place in the black box, and it is therefore impossible to affirm that the model predicted this output for the reasons given in the natural language explanation. Since nothing connects the KB and the black box, therefore it is impossible to link the explanation and the predicted output. The objective of not leaving explanation generation to human analysts is fulfilled, as the model formulates a line of reasoning, but the explanation given is not correct (or does not have accurate provenance), as it only explains what the black box should have learned, and not what it actually learned.

source_text: More generally, if some elements of the symmetry group $G$ are time-reversing, we are given a homomorphism $\rho:G\rightarrow{\mathbb{Z}}_{2}$ . Its kernel $G_{0}$ is the group of internal symmetries. We propose that in space-time dimension $d$ bosonic SPT phases with symmetry $G$ and vanishing thermal Hall response are classified by the degree- $d$ oriented cobordism group of $BG$ with twisted coefficients, with the twist determined by $\rho$ (see below for a detailed explanation). This twisted cobordism group will be denoted $\Omega^{d}_{SO}(BG,U(1)^{\rho})$ . In this case one does not need to quotient by the cobordism group with real coefficients because the twisted bordism group is pure torsion.

This section $\sigma$ induces an isomorphism between the restriction of the structure sheaf $\mathcal{O}$ to $Y_U$ and the restriction of $L$ to $Y_U$, denoted as $\mathcal{O}|_{Y_{U}} \stackrel{{\scriptstyle\sim}}{{\longrightarrow}} L|_{Y_{U}}$. By functoriality, this isomorphism further induces another isomorphism, $N_{Y_{U}/U}(\sigma): \mathcal{O}_{U} \stackrel{{\scriptstyle\sim}}{{\longrightarrow}} (N_{Y/T}L)|_{U}$. The latter isomorphism extends to a section $s: \mathcal{O}_{T} \to N_{Y/T}L$, which is non-vanishing outside of $Z$ and is defined by the morphism $\det\pi_{\ast}s: \det\pi_{\ast}\mathcal{O}_{Y} \to \det\pi_{\ast}L$.

Exploiting the sparsity of $\hat{\mathbf{X}}_{k}$, it is found that each element of $\mathbf{\hat{y}}$, specifically $\mathbf{\hat{y}}(n),n=1,2,...,N,$ depends solely on $\hat{\mathbf{x}}_{k}(n)$ and $\hat{\mathbf{g}}_{k}(n)$, where $\hat{\mathbf{x}}_{k}(n)$ is a vector containing elements from each of the $D$ dimensions and $\hat{\mathbf{g}}_{k}(n)$ is the complex conjugate of a vector containing elements from each of the $D$ dimensions.
source_text: Unfortunately, unlike subproblem $\mathbf{w}_{k}^{*}$ , solving subproblem $\mathbf{\hat{g}}_{k}^{*}$ containing $\hat{\mathbf{X}}_{k}\hat{\mathbf{g}}_{k}$ can be highly time consuming and the calculation needs to be carried out in every ADMM iteration. Therefore, the sparsity of $\hat{\mathbf{X}}_{k}$ is exploited. suspicious_text: In the detection process, the conventional DCF structure produces a response map, with the object presumed to be situated at the location of the highest value. The quality of the data within the response map is significant, as it provides an indication of the similarity between the model of the object's appearance learned in prior frames and the actual object detected in the current frame. It is common for inconsistencies to occur in situations such as occlusion, out-of-plane rotation, and other complex scenarios. Unfortunately, the conventional DCF framework does not capitalize on this information, and when such inconsistencies arise, no further action can be taken, resulting in the loss of the tracked object.
source_text: In detection process, traditional DCF framework generates a response map and the object is believed to be located where its value is the largest. Information hidden in response map is crucial as its quality to some extent reflects the similarity between object appearance model learned in previous frames and the actual object detected in current frame. Aberrances are omnipresent in occlusion, in/out of the plan rotation and many other challenging scenarios. However, traditional DCF framework fails to utilize this information and when aberrances take place, no action can be further taken and the tracked object is simply lost.

suspicious_text: The trend of reduced stripping enhancement at higher halo masses in v-d models suggests minimal distinguishable differences between SIDM and CDM for haloes exceeding $10^{10}\,\mathrm{M_{{\sun}}}$. A comparative analysis of stripped haloes via shared particle IDs across simulations confirms negligible variation in stripping among those with similar orbital paths. For SIDM1, an inverse trend emerges, with stripping increasing at higher masses. Consequently, while some discrepancies may arise for haloes above $10^{10}\,\mathrm{M_{{\sun}}}$, the SIDM effect remains secondary to orbital parameters and infall timing. Efforts to identify a distinct SIDM-related effect by matching large subhaloes in SIDM1 and CDM simulations were inconclusive. The broader implications of stellar stripping in these massive systems for the stellar halo are addressed in Section 7.3.
source_text: Extending the trend of diminishing enhancement of stripping towards higher halo masses in the v-d models, there should be little difference between SIDM and CDM for haloes above $10^{10}\,\mathrm{M_{{\sun}}}$ . Indeed, by matching individual haloes to each other via identifying common particle IDs between simulations, we find no significant difference in stripping in the few haloes that follow similar orbits. For SIDM1, the opposite effect of enhanced stripping increasing towards higher masses exists. We therefore expect some difference to exist for haloes above $10^{10}\,\mathrm{M_{{\sun}}}$ , but the SIDM effect is sub-dominant to the more critical orbital parameters and infall time. By matching large subhaloes to each other in SIDM1 and CDM, we could not isolate a clear SIDM related effect. The implications of stellar stripping in these largest systems on the stellar halo are discussed in Section 7.3 .

suspicious_text: The physical behavior of double glass is highly intricate. Consider a system composed of elliptical particles with a moderate aspect ratio and smaller circular impurities. We posit that both repulsive and attractive interactions are orientation-dependent, with attraction specifically occurring between the ellipses and the impurities. Consequently, the impurities significantly disrupt the orientations and positions of the surrounding ellipses, akin to how ions are hydrated by neighboring water molecules (19). As the impurity concentration $c$ increases from zero, the orientational domains and crystalline structures of the ellipses become progressively fragmented and disordered (18). In this study, we demonstrate double glass formation at low $T$ with an impurity concentration of $20\%$.
source_text: The physical picture of double glass is thus very complex. To give a clear example, we consider a mixture of elliptic particles with a mild aspect ratio and smaller circular particles (impurities). We assume orientation-dependent repulsive and attractive interactions, where the attractive part is between the ellipses and the impurities. Then, the impurities can strongly disturb the orientations and the positions of the surrounding ellipses. This is analogous to hydration of ions by surrounding water molecules (19) . If the impurity concentration $c$ is increased from zero, orientational domains and crystalline grains of the ellipses are gradually fragmented and disordered (18) . In this paper, we realize double glass at low $T$ at an impurity concentration of $20\%$ .

source_text: Since the edge degrees of freedom of an IHF form a system in 1+1 dimensions, we can make use of bosonization techniques. A current carried by gapless quasi-particles can be decomposed into left- and right-moving currents with opposite propagation directions, $J_{L}$ and $J_{R}$ . The vector current, $J=J_{L}+J_{R}$ , is always conserved. This means that the anomalous edge current, $J_{edge}$ , must be chiral , i.e., there is an imbalance between left- and right-moving modes.

suspicious_text: The size of the Strömgren sphere, a region around a luminous source where the gas is ionized, can be calculated by balancing the rate at which the source emits ionizing photons with the rate at which the gas recombines. This calculation yields a radius that depends on the source's photon emission rate and the density of the surrounding gas. As the shockwave generated by the source expands, the density of the gas it encounters decreases, causing the Strömgren radius to increase. Eventually, the shockwave and the ionization front, which marks the boundary of the Strömgren sphere, coincide, and the ionization front begins to move outward more rapidly, a transition that marks the point at which the front becomes detached from the shockwave and evolves into a radiation-dominated front.

suspicious_text: We focus exclusively on finite-type cluster algebras, which are characterized by a finite collection of distinct generators (cluster variables). These generators are organized into a finite number of clusters (sets) of identical size, with clusters related through exchange relations. In these relations, one cluster variable is replaced (mutated) by another variable not originally in the cluster. These exchange relations can be represented by oriented quivers associated with each cluster. From the quiver corresponding to a cluster, we can define $\mathcal{X}$-coordinates associated with each cluster variable within that cluster.
source_text: We are only interested in the finite type cluster algebras. Such cluster algebras have a finite set of distinct generators (cluster variables), that is grouped in a finite number of clusters (sets) of equal size and that relate to each other by exchange relations where one of the cluster variables is replaced by (mutates to) another cluster variable outside the cluster. These exchange relations can be codified within each cluster by associating them with oriented quivers. From the quiver associated to a cluster we can define $\mathcal{X}$ -coordinates related to each cluster variable in that cluster.

This method involves iteratively advancing the ionization front until either the target particle is reached or the source lifetime expires, thereby assigning a unique photon arrival time to each particle. Subsequently, particles with arrival times preceding the source lifetime are deemed to be irradiated by the radiation source at their respective arrival times.

source_text: Figure 4.1 shows the radial profiles for density, velocity, and temperature at $t=0$ and $t=3$ Myrs. Thermal pressure quickly suppresses the steep density profile and turns it to a nearly flat one within the stellar lifetime. The ionized region has a high temperature of $T\sim 30000$ K in the case with helium. For reference, we also show the temperature profile from a run in which we include only hydrogen. Additional heating by helium ionization is clearly seen. Throughout the evolution, the peak gas velocity is about 30 km/sec, which is nearly 10 times larger than the escape velocity of the host halo. Hence it is expected that almost all the gas will be evacuated within a few tens of million years (for a halo with virial radius $\sim 100$ pc).

suspicious_text: For a line arrangement in the complex projective plane $\mathbb{P}^{2}$, we examine the compactification $\overline{F}$ of the affine Milnor fiber within $\mathbb{P}^{3}$ and its minimal resolution $\widetilde{F}$. We express the Chern numbers using the combinatorial properties of the line arrangement and demonstrate that the minimal resolution cannot be a quotient of a ball. Furthermore, we establish that $\widetilde{F}$ is of general type when the arrangement has only nodes or triple points as singularities.
source_text: For a line arrangement in the complex projective plane $\mathbb{P}^{2}$ , we investigate the compactification $\overline{F}$ of the affine Milnor fiber in $\mathbb{P}^{3}$ and its minimal resolution $\widetilde{F}$ . We compute the Chern numbers in terms of the combinatorics of the line arrangement, then we show that the minimal resolution is never a quotient of a ball; in addition, we also prove that $\widetilde{F}$ is of general type when the arrangement has only nodes or triple points as singularities.

u$ increases. The optimal resetting rate, denoted as $r^{\star}$, emerges as a pivotal observable for investigating the resetting transition, given its sensitivity to changes in $
u$. Consequently, our analysis will focus on elucidating the resetting transition through the lens of the optimal resetting rate, providing a nuanced understanding of this complex phenomenon.

This is true in the sense that there is a trade-off between the performance of a model and its transparency Došilovićet al.[2018] . suspicious_text: The V-D models exhibit negligible influence due to their minimal interaction cross-sections in haloes with mass scales exceeding $10^{10}\,\mathrm{M_{\sun}}$. The host halo develops a very small core, causing minor disruptions to satellite orbits and the spherically averaged dark matter dynamics in the outer regions. As detailed in Sections 5 and 6, satellites with $M_{\rm{infall}}>10^{10}\,\mathrm{M_{\sun}}$ exhibit a low $r_{\rm{core}}/r_{\rm{t}}$ ratio, resulting in minimal enhancement of stellar stripping and no subhalo evaporation. However, approximately $90\%$ of the stellar mass accreted by the host originates from these satellites. Consequently, the stellar halo is predominantly shaped by a few large satellites that remain largely unaffected by SIDM, within a host halo potential that undergoes minimal alteration, leading to no significant global modifications to the stellar halo.
source_text: The v-d models make such little influence due to their small interaction cross-sections in haloes of mass scales above $10^{10}\,\mathrm{M_{\sun}}$ . The host halo forms only a very small core, leading to minimal perturbations of the orbits of satellites and the spherically averaged DM dynamics of the host halo in the outer regions. As discussed in Sections 5 and 6 , satellites with $M_{\rm{infall}}>10^{10}\,\mathrm{M_{\sun}}$ have a low $r_{\rm{core}}/r_{\rm{t}}$ ratio, experiencing minimal enhancement in stellar stripping, and have no subhalo evaporation. Yet $90\%$ of the stellar mass accreted on to the host comes from such satellites. Thus the stellar halo is dominated by of the order of $10$ large satellites which are not substantially affected by SIDM, in a host halo potential which is minimally changed, resulting in no global changes to the stellar halo.

Still, one can say that a time-reversal symmetry for a 1d system cannot be gauged if all states are odd under time-reversal. On the other hand, if such a 1d system is a boundary of a 2d SPT phase, then time-reversal can be gauged, because a spatial slice of a 2d SPT phase always looks like a collection of intervals, and all 1d edges come in pairs.

source_text: The nonlocal spin resistance is measured as a function of temperature in a Fe/GaAs spin-injection device. For nonannealed samples that show minority-spin injection, the spin resistance is observed up to room temperature and decays exponentially with temperature at a rate of 0.018 K \textsuperscript{-1} . Post-growth annealing at 440 K increases the spin signal at low temperatures, but the decay rate also increases to 0.030 K \textsuperscript{-1} . From measurements of the diffusion constant and the spin lifetime in the GaAs channel, we conclude that sample annealing modifies the temperature dependence of the spin transfer efficiency at injection and detection contacts. Surprisingly, the spin transfer efficiency increases in samples that exhibit minority-spin injection.

suspicious_text: To improve the traditional Discriminative Correlation Filters (DCF), several methods have been proposed to expand the search area. However, these methods introduce unwanted noise due to the excessive background data. The model can then learn from the surrounding environment more than the target object, leading to potential errors in the detection process. To tackle this issue, this study presents an innovative solution called Aberrance Repressed Correlation Filter (ARCF). By imposing a constraint on the rate of change of response maps during the detection phase, the ARCF tracker effectively mitigates aberrances, making it more reliable and accurate for object tracking. Experiments were conducted on various UAV datasets such as UAV123, UAVDT, and DTB70, using 243 challenging image sequences with over 90 thousand frames to assess the ARCF tracker's performance. suspicious_text: This research utilizes an optical multimodal sensing methodology, whereby soft materials are exposed to laser light, generating a complex interference pattern known as a speckle pattern. The speckle pattern, highly sensitive to the scattering process, contains valuable information regarding material deformation. While speckle-based techniques have been applied in various sensing applications, including single physical signal sensing, deformation field measurement, and three-dimensional shape measurement, this study introduces a novel approach to multimodal sensing in soft materials, expanding the capabilities of speckle-based sensing methodologies.
source_text: Optical multimodal sensing methodology. The proposed sensing approach is based on optical scattering from soft materials when the material is irradiated with laser light [Fig. 1 ]. The optical scattering produces a complex interference pattern, referred to as a speckle pattern Goodman (2000) , on an observation plane. The speckle pattern is highly sensitive to the scattering process in a material; thus, it can contain various information regarding the deformation of a soft material. Although speckle-based techniques have been used in sensing a single physical signal Murray et al. (2019) , deformation field measurement Fricke-Begemann (2003) , three-dimensional shape measurement Schaffer et al. (2010) , and in spectrometers Redding et al. (2013) , this study presents the first demonstration of multimodal sensing in soft materials.

This rate is based on the optically-thin approximation, assuming the flux decreases with the inverse square of the distance from the source. While photo-dissociation is negligible within H ii regions, where collisional dissociation and charge transfer dominate, it plays a role outside these regions. Given that our simulations exclusively track stars formed within accreting satellites, we concentrate our predictions on the outer halo, where accreted stars constitute the majority of the stellar population, surpassing those formed within the host halo itself. DCF based trackers have achieved state-of-the-art performance in multiple datasets specified for UAV object tracking [10] .

But it is proposed by Veselago that the simultaneous change of positive $\varepsilon$ and $\mu_{M}$ to negative $-\varepsilon$ and $-\mu_{M}$ corresponds to the transformation of the refraction index from one branch $n=\sqrt{\varepsilon(\omega,k)\mu_{M}(\omega,k)}$ to the other $n=-\sqrt{\varepsilon(\omega,k)\mu_{M}(\omega,k)}$ , ie, the turn from the general refraction index to the negative one. The physical nature of the negative refraction is that the electromagnetic phase velocity propagates opposite to the energy flow. suspicious_text: We propose a modified version of the architecture depicted in Fig. 1. Rather than incorporating an external knowledge base (KB) to enhance the model, our approach involves two key steps: i) directly extracting symbolic rules from an initial black box model, and ii) integrating these rules into a secondary black box model by imposing constraints during the learning process based on observed properties, such as adjusting initialization protocols, loss functions, or hyperparameters. Consequently, the model's final output is derived from the reasoner but is directly shaped by both the black box and the KB, ensuring that the black box's potential is preserved while revealing the biases learned by the model. This process also facilitates performance improvement and provides natural language explanations for its predictions.
source_text: We propose an adaptation of the architecture in Fig. 1 . Instead of externally providing a KB to complement the model, we propose to i) directly extract symbolic rules from a first black box and ii) reflect those rules in a second black box by constraining learning according to perceived properties, e.g., by modifying initialization protocols, loss functions or hyperparameters. Therefore, the model’s ultimate output would come from the reasoner but would be directly influenced by both the black box and the KB, i.e., it would not truncate the black box potency but reveal, as expected by an explanation, the biases learned by the model and lead to performance improvement while explaining in natural language its prediction.

In the equivparticle model, quarks are considered as quasi-free particles with mass dependent on the baryon number density. Both linear confinement and leading order perturbative interactions are taken into account to derive the quark mass scaling equation as follows:

$m_{i}(n)=m_{i0}+\frac{D}{n^{1/3}}+Cn^{1/3}.$ (13)

Here, $m_{i0}$ represents the current mass of quark flavor $i$ ($i=u,d,s$) as per Particle Data Group (2014). The baryon number density is denoted by $n$. The confinement parameter $D$ is related to the string tension $\sigma_{0}$, the chiral restoration density $\rho^{*}$, and the sum of the vacuum chiral condensates $\sum_{q}\langle\bar{q}q\rangle_{0}$. The perturbative strength parameter $C$ is linked to the strong coupling constant $\alpha_{\mathrm{s}}$. Due to the inherent uncertainties in relevant quantities, the exact values of $D$ and $C$ remain unknown. However, it has been estimated that $\sqrt{D}$ falls within the range of (147, 270) MeV (Wenet et al., 2005)

source_text: In this paper we attempt to refine the classification based on group cohomology. Related ideas have been considered in [6] . We propose that in space-time dimension $d$ bosonic SPT phases with a finite internal symmetry group $G_{0}$ and vanishing thermal Hall response are classified by the Pontryagin-dual of the torsion subgroup of the degree- $d$ oriented bordism group of $BG_{0}$ .

Notably, the inclusion of helium yields a significantly elevated temperature of approximately 30,000 K within the ionized region, with comparative analysis to a hydrogen-only model highlighting the substantial additional heating effect attributable to helium ionization. Furthermore, the gas velocity peaks at around 30 km/sec throughout the evolutionary process, exceeding the host halo's escape velocity by nearly an order of magnitude, suggesting that the gas will be largely expelled from the halo, with a virial radius of about 100 pc, within a relatively short timeframe of several tens of millions of years.
source_text: Figure 4.1 shows the radial profiles for density, velocity, and temperature at $t=0$ and $t=3$ Myrs. Thermal pressure quickly suppresses the steep density profile and turns it to a nearly flat one within the stellar lifetime. The ionized region has a high temperature of $T\sim 30000$ K in the case with helium. For reference, we also show the temperature profile from a run in which we include only hydrogen. Additional heating by helium ionization is clearly seen. Throughout the evolution, the peak gas velocity is about 30 km/sec, which is nearly 10 times larger than the escape velocity of the host halo. Hence it is expected that almost all the gas will be evacuated within a few tens of million years (for a halo with virial radius $\sim 100$ pc).

suspicious_text: By leveraging the Schrödinger equations for time-dependent Hamiltonians in their most general form, this study yields novel sets of time-energy uncertainty relations, distinct from those presently found in scholarly discourse. Notably, our innovative approach to deriving these relations, as well as the ensuing outcomes, diverge from established methodologies. A key aspect of our formulation is its reliance solely on initial states and Hamiltonians, eschewing the need for instantaneous eigenstates or the comprehensive time-dependent wave functions that would typically necessitate a complete solution to the time-dependent Hamiltonian. This distinctive feature confers upon our time-energy uncertainty relations a broader scope of applicability and enhanced utility.
source_text: In this paper we derive different sets of TEURs directly from the Schrödinger equations for time-dependent Hamiltonians in the general case. Our employed method of derivation as well as the results are new and different to those in the existing literature. It is important to note that only the initial states and the Halmiltionians, and neither the instantaneous eigenstates nor the full time-dependent wave functions, which would demand a full solution for a time-dependent Hamiltonian, are required for our time-energy relations. This hallmark enables wider applicability and usefulness of our TEURs.

source_text: Recall that a manifold $M$ , whether oriented or not, carries a canonical principal ${\mathbb{Z}}_{2}$ bundle $\xi_{M}$ called the orientation bundle of $M$ . The holonomy of the associated ${\mathbb{Z}}_{2}$ -connection assigns $-1$ to all closed loops which reverse orientation and assigns $+1$ to all closed loops which do not. A closed manifold $M$ , whether oriented or not, has the fundamental class living in $H_{d}(M,{\mathbb{Z}}^{T})$ , where ${\mathbb{Z}}^{T}$ is a local system with fiber ${\mathbb{Z}}$ twisted by the orientation bundle. Its Pontryagin-dual is $H^{d}(M,U(1)^{T})$ , where $U(1)^{T}$ is a local system over $M$ with fiber $U(1)$ twisted by the orientation bundle.

This construction interprets the algebra as a convex polytope, where clusters correspond to vertices and mutation relations manifest as edges connecting these vertices. For a cluster algebra of rank \( r \), each vertex is uniquely identified by the \( r \) \(\mathcal{X}\)-coordinates of the associated cluster, and from each vertex, exactly \( r \) edges emanate, reflecting the algebra's combinatorial structure.
source_text: A useful construction associated to a finite type cluster algebra is a generalized associahedron. Such construction represents the cluster algebra as a polytope with clusters being represented by vertices and mutations between clusters being represented by edges connecting vertices. For a rank $r$ cluster algebra, each vertex is parametrized by the $r$ $\mathcal{X}$ -coordinates of the represented cluster and from each vertex departs $r$ edges.

suspicious_text: Theoretical models based on quantum chromodynamics anticipate a phase transition to a quark-gluon plasma state at elevated temperatures and densities, prompting substantial research efforts at the Relativistic Heavy Ion Collider and Large Hadron Collider to verify this phenomenon. Notably, experiments at RHIC have yielded two key observations: firstly, the deconfined medium exhibits characteristics of a near-perfect fluid with low viscosity, which has been effectively modeled using viscous hydrodynamics to replicate observed outcomes such as elliptic flow and particle spectra; secondly, the significant suppression of jet formation, considered a potential indicator of quark-gluon plasma creation, has been observed. Furthermore, preliminary results from LHC experiments corroborate the findings from RHIC, providing additional evidence to support these conclusions.
source_text: Quantum chromodynamics (QCD) predicts that deconfined phase transition will take place at high temperature and/or high density, as a result the nuclear matter will undergo a transition to quark-gluon plasma (QGP). One main goal for Relativistic Heavy Ion Collider (RHIC) and Large Hadronic Collider (LHC) is to seek this new state of matter. There are two striking findings at RHIC. One is that the deconfined hot medium behaviors as a nearly perfect fluid with a small viscosity (1) . Several groups have applied viscous hydrodynamics to simulate the evolution of the produced matter in heavy ion collisions. The simulations successfully fit the observables at RHIC, such as the elliptic flow, the particle spectra, etc (5) . The other is the strong jet quenching, which is believed to be a potential signal for the QGP formation (14) . It should be stressed that the first LHC results also strongly support similar conclusions as seen at RHIC (15) .

Here, the SPARSE mode can be either analog or digital types, with the threshold levels determined by the analog voltage or the digital ADC value, respectively. Furthermore, the TT02 detects a common-mode noise as described in detail in Section 3.3 . The ADC value of the measured common-mode noise can be read out for either mode.

Approximation functions for these perturbation fields are then incorporated into a finite element formulation to augment those of the macroscopic fields. This plays a role in determining the tidal radius, $r_{\rm{t}}$ , which defines an approximate distance beyond which mass is no longer bound to a subhalo. source_text: Mixtures of anisotropic particles with mild differences in sizes and shapes such as (KCN) \textsubscript{x} (KBr) \textsubscript{1-x} form a cubic crystal without orientational order (plastic solid) at relatively high $T$ . With further lowering $T$ , they undergo a structural phase transition in dilute cases and become orientational glass in nondilute cases (7) , where the crystal structure is preserved. On the other hand, if the two species have significantly different sizes or shapes, translational glass without crystal order can emerge from liquid at low $T$ . For rodlike molecules with relatively large aspect ratios, liquid crystal phase transitions occur with lowing $T$ , but their glass transitions have not yet well understood. In recent experiments on colloidal ellipsoids in monolayers, the aspect ratio was 6 (8) or 2.1 (9) with considerable size dispersities. In glassy states, these ellipsoids exhibited mesoscopic nematic or smectic order.

suspicious_text: Within the context of viscous chromohydrodynamics, a theoretical framework is applied to derive the gluon self-energy in a quark-gluon plasma characterized by shear viscosity. This derivation enables the calculation of viscous electric permittivity and magnetic permeability, which are subsequently used to examine the refractive index. Numerical results show that the refractive index exhibits negative values over a specific frequency range, with the onset of this range coinciding with the pole of the electric permittivity and the termination point determined by the magnetic permeability pole. Furthermore, an increase in the ratio of shear viscosity to entropy density, denoted as $\eta/s$, is found to broaden the frequency range over which negative refraction occurs.

We start by studying the classical mechanics of a 2DEG exhibiting the Hall effect. In a steady state, where the electrons in the 2DEG have a constant velocity, the total force on an electron must vanish. Hence 
 
 $\vec{F}_{e^{-}\parallel}=-e[\vec{E}_{\parallel}+\frac{\vec{v}}{c}\wedge\vec{B}_{0\perp}]=0\,.$  (4) 
 It follows that the velocity of the electrons, $\vec{v}$ , is perpendicular to the in-plane electric field $\vec{E}_{\parallel}$ , i.e., 
 
 $\vec{E}_{\parallel}\cdot\vec{v}=0\,.$  (5) 
 Using ( 4 ), the electric current density is given by 
 
 $\vec{j}=-en\vec{v}=\sigma_{H}(\vec{e}_{z}\wedge\vec{E})\,,$  (6) 
 and the Hall conductivity , $\sigma_{H}$ , is apparently given by 
 
 $\sigma_{H}=R_{H}^{-1}=\frac{enc}{|\vec{B}_{0\perp}|}=\frac{e^{2}}{h}\,\nu\,.$  (7) 
 We observe that classical theory predicts a linear relation between the Hall conductivity and the filling factor $\nu$ , with a factor of proportionality given by $R_{K}^{-1}=e^{2}/h$ .

source_text:
An important quantity often used in simulations and theoretical models of reionization is the ionizing photon escape fraction. Our three-dimensional radiation hydrodynamics calculation allows to directly measure this quantity. Since the mass resolution of our simulations is sufficient to resolve the smallest gas clumps within early mini-halos, the effect of gas clumping is self-consistently included in our calculations. Therefore, we do not need to assume the so-called photon escape fraction nor gas clumping factor . The photon escape fraction is indeed obtained as a result of our simulations. We define the escape fraction as the number fraction of photons that escape out of the host halo’s virial radius into the intergalactic medium. Following Alvarez et al. (2006a), we evaluate the escape fraction along a photon ray as 
 
 $f_{\rm esc}(t)=1-\frac{4\pi\alpha_{\rm B}}{\dot{N}_{\rm ph}}\int_{0}^{r_{\rm vir}}n^{2}(r,t)\;r^{2}\;{\rm d}r\,,$  (26) 
 where $r_{\rm vir}$ is the virial radius

suspicious_text: In this study, we build upon the findings from [26] and integrate them with those from [12]. Our contributions are twofold: we develop i) waves characterized by an arbitrary number of critical layers, and ii) wave families that bifurcate from kernels involving two distinct Fourier modes. The second category of waves exhibits a more intricate surface pattern compared to Stokes waves (refer to Figure 2, p. 2). This distinct behavior does not stem from complex rotational flows, as the vorticity functions in this context are linear. Consequently, this work serves as a natural progression from [26], which focused on flows with constant vorticity. Our primary objective here is to establish the existence of such waves. A comprehensive analysis of small-amplitude waves associated with the first Fourier mode will be presented in a subsequent study [11].
source_text: In this paper we extend the results from [26] and combine them with those from [12] . The novelty is two-fold: we construct i) waves with arbitrarily many critical layers, and ii) families of waves bifurcating from kernels with two different Fourier modes. The latter type of wave displays a more complicated surface profile than those of Stokes waves (cf. Figure 2 , p. 2 ). This qualitatively different behavior arises not as a result of involved rotational flow, but the vorticity functions are linear maps. Thus, the paper at hand is a natural extension of [26] , which dealt with flows of constant vorticity. We settle here for the mere existence of such waves. A qualitative investigation of the small-amplitude waves corresponding to the first Fourier mode will be given in a separate study [11] .

These SPT phases exhibit a non-trivial thermal Hall response. If $G_{0}$ is trivial, the related action must solely depend on the metric and display odd behavior under orientation-reversal. source_text: Consider the functional derivative of $S^{\alpha}_{M}$ with respect to the metric on $M$ . If this derivative does not vanish identically, this means that there is a nontrivial vacuum expectation value of the stress-energy tensor when the system is placed into a nontrivial geometric background and/or coupled to a background gauge field. Such SPT phases have a nontrivial thermal Hall response. In the case when $G_{0}$ is trivial, the corresponding action must depend only on the metric and be odd under orientation-reversal. This implies that it must be a gravitational Chern-Simons term. Such terms exist in dimensions $d$ of the form $4n-1$ . More generally, one can have also mixed terms which involve the gauge field $A$ as well.

source_text: There is another interesting question about the formation of the so-called second-generation stars. Unlike the somewhat simple and ‘clean’ initial conditions for the first stars, second generation stars are likely born from a gas that has been disturbed by earlier feedback effects. Among the most important of these effects is photo-ionization by the first stars. An ionized gas cools faster than it recombines, and thus many free electrons are left over (Shapiro & Kang 1987; Susa et al. 1998). When the temperature drops below $\sim 8000$ K, atomic hydrogen Lyman- $\alpha$ cooling becomes inefficient but then hydrogen molecules are rapidly formed using the abundant free electrons as a catalyst. The gas cools by H \textsubscript{2} line cooling to $\sim 100$ K. In a primordial gas, HD molecules act as an efficient coolant at such low temperatures, enabling the gas to cool down to a few tens Kelvin (Flower et al. 2000). This radiation, which permeates the universe, disrupts the ability of gas to coalesce and form molecules, a crucial step in the creation of stars and galaxies. As a result, the baryon fraction, a measure of the amount of ordinary matter within these halos, is substantially lower than in regions without such radiation. This reduction in baryon fraction leads to a lower mean gas density, making it difficult for molecules to form and for self-shielding to protect them from the destructive effects of radiation. suspicious_text: The comparative analysis of cooling mechanisms reveals that the processes mediated by H ${}_{2}^{+}$ molecules have a comparable impact on the overall cooling rate to that of two predominant cooling channels, namely hydrogen Lyman- $\alpha$ emission and H \textsubscript{2} cooling, particularly within the temperature range of approximately 5000-7000 K. Furthermore, our investigation also encompasses the consideration of two additional ionic species, H ${}_{3}^{+}$ and HeH \textsuperscript{+}, whose formation and destruction pathways are incorporated into an expanded chemical network. Through the examination of an isobaric test scenario, it is determined that the abundances of these species remain negligible, thereby justifying the omission of reactions involving H ${}_{3}^{+}$ and HeH \textsuperscript{+} from further analysis, as elaborated upon in the supplementary material.
source_text: We find that the above cooling processes by H ${}_{2}^{+}$ molecules contribute roughly equally to the total cooling rate as two other dominant cooling processes, hydrogen Lyman- $\alpha$ cooling and H \textsubscript{2} cooling at temperatures $\sim 5000-7000$ K. We also consider two other ionic molecules, H ${}_{3}^{+}$ and HeH \textsuperscript{+} . In addition to the fiducial chemistry network, we include reactions for formation and destruction of these molecules. By calculating an isobaric test case, we find that their fractions are always very small, and conclude that we may ignore reactions involving H ${}_{3}^{+}$ and HeH \textsuperscript{+} . (See Appendix for details.)
