To reiterate, the critical reflecting radius $r^{\text{max}}_{\text{c}}({\bar{a}})$ holds a key role in the physical context. This radius represents the boundary for a stably spinning object with a specific dimensionless angular momentum parameter ${\bar{a}}$. When the reflecting surface of an object with this parameter value is situated beyond $r^{\text{max}}_{\text{c}}({\bar{a}})$, the object remains stable. On the other hand, if the reflecting surface falls within the region $r_{\text{c}}<r^{\text{max}}_{\text{c}}({\bar{a}})$, the object becomes unstable to scalar perturbation modes, leading to superradiant instabilities in the system consisting of exotic compact objects, massless scalar fields, and the object's own spin.

Reference:
(13) Schnittman, J. W. (2004). The stability of the Kerr-de Sitter metric with respect to scalar perturbation modes. Classical and Quantum Gravity, 21(21), 5573-5582.
source_text: It is worth emphasizing again that the physical significance of the critical reflecting radius $r^{\text{max}}_{\text{c}}({\bar{a}})$ stems from the fact that, for a given value of the dimensionless angular momentum parameter ${\bar{a}}$ , this supporting radius corresponds to a marginally-stable spinning object which marks the onset of superradiant instabilities in the composed exotic-compact-object-massless-scalar-field system. In particular, as nicely demonstrated numerically in (13) , spinning compact objects of dimensionless angular momentum ${\bar{a}}$ whose reflecting surfaces are located in the region $r_{\text{c}}>r^{\text{max}}_{\text{c}}({\bar{a}})$ are stable, whereas spinning objects whose reflecting surfaces are located in the region $r_{\text{c}}<r^{\text{max}}_{\text{c}}({\bar{a}})$ are superradiantly unstable to scalar perturbation modes.

suspicious_text: Our objective is to tag a sufficient number of particles to achieve high-resolution results and to explore a realistic range of stellar distributions, with a focus on targeting the core formation in SIDM. For field haloes at $z=0$, we observe that cores extend to approximately $3\%$ of the most bound particles in $10^{8}\,\mathrm{M_{\sun}}$ haloes. In $10^{10}\,\mathrm{M_{\sun}}$ haloes, cores range from $0.5\%$ in vdSIDMb on the lower end to $1.5\%$ in SIDM10 on the higher end, based on our models (see Section 6 for detailed calculations of core sizes). To effectively probe satellite cores and ensure optimal resolution, we aim to tag all stars up to the $\sim 2\%$ most bound particle in all satellites, including satellites of satellites, across the entire cosmic timeline.

As a special case for time-independent Hamiltonians, and as a necessary but not sufficient condition, is a lower bound on the time $\Delta t_{\forall}$ it takes for an initial state to evolve into any arbitrary state allowed by the Hamiltonian dynamics: 
 
 $\displaystyle{2\hbar}\leq\Delta t_{\forall}\times{\sqrt{{\Delta E_{0}}^{2}+(E_{0})^{2}}},$  (1) 
 where $E_{0}$ and $\Delta E_{0}$ are, respectively the energy expectation value and the energy spread of the initial state.

source_text: Braid statistics is an interesting phenomenon only encountered in two-dimensional systems. In dimensions larger than two, quantum statistics is always described by representations of the permutation group; see, e.g., [37] and refs. given there. The theoretical possibility of braid statistics in 2D systems appears to be realized in IHFs at certain fractional plateaux.

suspicious_text: In our examination of the effective action, we have eliminated the geometric terms, leading us to inquire as to its possible dependence on the topology of the manifold $M$. To elucidate this relationship, let us initially consider the scenario where the initial gauge group $G_{0}$ is trivial. Under such conditions, the action is solely contingent on the topology of $M$. Given that it is also local, it can only be expressed as an integral of the characteristic classes of $M$. In the oriented scenario (absent time-reversal symmetry), there are three types of such classes: the Pontryagin classes, which arise in dimensions that are multiples of four, the Stiefel-Whitney classes, which exist in all dimensions, and the Euler class, which appears in the top dimension, though it vanishes if the dimension is odd. source_text: Having disposed of the geometric terms in the effective action, we now ask how it can depend on the topology of $M$ . It is instructive to look at the case of trivial $G_{0}$ first. Then the action depends only on the topology of $M$ . Since it is also local, it must be an integral of products of characteristic class of $M$ . In the oriented case (i.e. without time-reversal symmetry) there are three kinds of such classes: the Pontryagin classes, with exist in dimensions divisible by $4$ , the Stiefel-Whitney classes, which exist in all dimensions, and the Euler class, which exists in top dimension (but vanishes if the dimension is odd). The Euler class is ruled out, because it is odd under orientation-reversal, and thus its integral over $M$ will be even, while the action is supposed to be odd. Thus $S^{\alpha}_{M}$ must be a linear combination of integrals of products of Pontryagin and Stiefel-Whitney classes over $M$ . These integrals are called Pontryagin and Stiefel-Whitney numbers

suspicious_text: The results of Section 4, excluding subhalo evaporation, can be explained through the fundamental physics of tidal stripping. The gravitational tidal field's strength, external to subhaloes, dictates the amount of mass stripped from satellites, influencing the tidal radius, $r_{\rm{t}}$. This radius marks the boundary beyond which mass is no longer gravitationally bound to a subhalo, with a smaller radius leading to greater stripping. Internally, the dynamics at the halo's center determine which stellar particles are stripped. Comparing CDM and SIDM necessitates examining the halo core size, $r_{\rm{core}}$, as it reflects dynamical differences caused by self-interactions. SIDM reduces central density and increases velocity dispersion within the core, lowering particle binding energy. Consequently, stellar mass loss increases in two distinct ways.

source_text: Lipton [2018] call transparency the opposition of black box-ness, the search for a direct understanding of the mechanism by which a model works. The contrast with post-hoc methods, all those that do not clarify the model, is not exclusive, as a post-hoc method could also be considered as transparent if it provides an intuitive explanation of the model parameters. The latter definition is also assumed by Montavonet al.[2018] , and states, as a further remark, that the goal of post-hoc methods is to understand what the system predicts given a trained model. In Došilovićet al.[2018] , however, the authors add two sub-approaches to transparent methods: pure transparent ones, i.e., those that use model families considered as transparent such as linear models or decision trees, and hybrid ones that combine transparent model families with black box methods. However, it does not yield a physically accurate solution for the arrival time $t_{\rm a,i}$ in the case of D-type I-fronts, where the front's progression is restricted to the velocity of the preceding hydrodynamic shock. In such scenarios, the I-front merges with the shock once the latter reduces the internal density. When an I-front is of D-type, it can move forward only at the velocity of the foregoing hydrodynamic shock. Namely, the I-front catches up with the the foregoing shock as soon as the shock reduces the density inside it (see Shu 1992 and KYSU for a detailed description of D-type and R-type ionization fronts). source_text: The TT02APD32 ASIC (henceforth TT02) is designed to read out 32 APD signal channels. Its main function is to condition the signal with a good signal-to-noise ratio and feed them into a Wilkinson-type analog-to-digital converter (ADC). The TT02 is fabricated based on the XFAB 0.35 $\mu$ m CMOS technology with 4-metal and MIM (Metal-Insulator-Metal) options, and assembled in a ceramic package of 160 pins (QC-160360-WZ, KYOCERA). The electrical architecture of the TT02 utilizes a property of the Open IP project, which has amassed technologies and the development of several radiation detectors (e.g., [5] ). In particular, since the large APD capacitance of $\sim$ 100 pF leads to a corresponding large capacitive noise, the TT02 implements an ingenious noise reduction scheme (see Section 2.5 ).

suspicious_text: In the initial discourse, it is noted that SPT phases in d dimensions are linked to ’t Hooft anomalies in (d-1) dimensions. To illustrate, time-reversal-invariant SPT phases in dimension d are equivalent to ’t Hooft anomalies for time-reversal in dimension (d-1). Implementing time-reversal symmetry involves establishing the theory on a non-oriented manifold. Consequently, the presence of an ’t Hooft anomaly for time-reversal implies that a theory possesses a time-reversal symmetry in flat spacetime, but fails to be consistently defined on a non-oriented manifold. This particular scenario can be viewed as a specific instance of global gravitational anomaly.
source_text: As remarked in the introduction, SPT phases in $d$ dimensions are related to ’t Hooft anomalies in $d-1$ dimensions. For example, time-reversal-invariant SPT phases in dimension $d$ correspond to ’t Hooft anomalies for time-reversal in dimension $d-1$ . Gauging time-reversal means defining the theory on an unoriented manifold, so an ’t Hooft anomaly for time-reversal means that a theory has a time-reversal symmetry on a flat space-time, but nevertheless cannot be consistently defined on an unoriented manifold. This can be regarded as a special case of global gravitational anomaly.

suspicious_text: In a retrospective analysis, Adadi and Berrada (2018) introduced a categorization that distinguishes between methodologies. These methods are divided into two groups: those that analyze both the model's operational mechanics and its results, and those that solely focus on the outcomes. The former are termed model-based methods, which aim to elucidate the inner workings of the prediction process as a means of achieving transparency. Conversely, the latter are classified as post-hoc methods, where the explanation is generated independently of the model. Post-hoc explanations are derived from a secondary process that interprets the model's outputs without delving into the underlying decision-making mechanisms.

suspicious_text: The Adiabatic Quantum Computation (AQC) process commences with the easily preparable ground state of an initial Hamiltonian, which is then gradually transformed into the target Hamiltonian through a time-dependent Hamiltonian. This time-dependent Hamiltonian, defined over a time interval, enables a continuous interpolation between the initial and final Hamiltonians, allowing the system to evolve towards the desired solution. The interpolation is achieved through a linear or non-linear schedule, governed by continuous functions that satisfy specific boundary conditions, ensuring a smooth transition between the initial and final states. The time evolution of the system is described by the Schrödinger equation, with the initial condition set as the ground state of the initial Hamiltonian, and the goal is to obtain the ground state of the target Hamiltonian with high probability.

source_text: After completing this procedure, we verify that our tagging did not introduce significant biases. One particular test is shown in the right-hand panel of Fig. 2 . This shows the normalized distribution of the boundedness percentile at which particles were tagged for all tagged particles in all subhaloes. We present the cases of vdSIDMa (solid green line), SIDM1 (dashed magenta line), and each of their corresponding CDM instances (solid black and dashed black lines respectively). Whereas the left-hand panels shows the unnormalized distribution of unscattered particles, this panel shows the normalized distribution of actually tagged particles. Importantly, the CDM simulation is tagged in different ways to mimic the features of the different SIDM cases. The dashed CDM line mirrors that of SIDM1, and the solid CDM line mirrors that of vdSIDMa. Imperfections in the match are due mostly to slight differences in merger history.

Similarly, $\Omega_{SO}^{d}(X,{\mathbb{R}})={\rm Hom}(\Omega_{SO,d}(X),{\mathbb{R}})$ is defined. The map $e:{\mathbb{R}}\rightarrow U(1)$ which sends $x$ to $\exp(2\pi ix)$ induces a map $e:{\rm Hom}(\Omega_{SO,d}(X),{\mathbb{R}})\rightarrow{\rm Hom}(\Omega_{SO,d}(X),U(1))$. The image of this map consists of elements of $\Omega_{SO}^{d}(X,U(1))$ which vanish on the torsion subgroup of $\Omega_{SO,d}(X)$. Therefore, the Pontryagin-dual of the torsion subgroup of $\Omega_{SO,d}(X)$ can be identified with the quotient $\Omega_{SO}^{d}(X,U(1))/{\rm im}\ e$.

source_text: However, tagging particles in SIDM simulations introduces a new obstacle. Dark matter particles can elastically scatter, whereas stars should never scatter. We deal with this problem by only assigning mass to particles which do not scatter between their time of tagging and the final $z=0$ snapshot. Consequently, only a fraction of the central $2\%$ most bound particles can be tagged. In order to avoid resolution problems, we resort to still tag $2\%$ of the total bound particles, tagging all unscattered ones in order of boundedness until we reach our $2\%$ . This, however, creates an additional problem. In haloes with high rates of interaction, stellar mass would be assigned at unrealistically large distances from the halo’s centre. We solve this new issue by enforcing a cut-off at $5\%$ . No particles that are greater than $5\%$ in their boundedness ranking can be tagged. suspicious_text: The evolution of a cooling gas under isobaric conditions is illustrated in Figure 2, which depicts the gas temperature and molecular fractions over time. Initially, the gas is fully ionized with a temperature of 50,000K, and two scenarios are considered with initial hydrogen densities of 1 cm^-3 and 100 cm^-3. A comparison of the results with and without HD cooling reveals a noticeable impact on the temperature and chemical composition of the gas after 10^5 years, as evidenced by the dashed lines in the left panel. Additionally, the right panel shows the fraction of H2+ ionic molecules, providing further insight into the gas's chemical evolution.
source_text: Figure 2.— Evolution of the gas temperature (left) and molecular fractions (right) for an isobarically cooling gas. The gas is assumed to be fully ionized initially, with a temperature of $T=50000K$ . We run two cases with initial densities of $n_{\rm H}=1,\;100{\rm cm}^{-3}$ . The dashed lines in the left panel are for runs without HD cooling. The effect of HD cooling can be seen in the temperature and chemical evolution at $t>10^{5}$ years. In the right panel, we also show the fraction of H ${}_{2}^{+}$ ionic molecules.

suspicious_text: Our objective is to tag a sufficient number of particles to achieve high-resolution results and to explore a realistic range of stellar distributions, with a focus on targeting the core formation in SIDM. For field haloes at $z=0$, we observe that cores extend to approximately $3\%$ of the most bound particles in $10^{8}\,\mathrm{M_{\sun}}$ haloes. In $10^{10}\,\mathrm{M_{\sun}}$ haloes, cores range from $0.5\%$ in vdSIDMb on the lower end to $1.5\%$ in SIDM10 on the higher end, based on our models (see Section 6 for detailed calculations of core sizes). To effectively probe satellite cores and ensure optimal resolution, we aim to tag all stars up to the $\sim 2\%$ most bound particle in all satellites, including satellites of satellites, across the entire cosmic timeline.
source_text: We aim to tag enough particles for good resolution, and to probe a realistic range of where stars reside, particularly making sure to target where a core forms in SIDM. For field haloes at $z=0$ , we find that cores extend to $\sim 3\%$ of the most bound particles in $10^{8}\,\mathrm{M_{\sun}}$ haloes. In $10^{10}\,\mathrm{M_{\sun}}$ haloes, cores extend to $0.5\%$ in vdSIDMb on the low end, and $1.5\%$ in SIDM10 on the high end for our models (see Section 6 for our calculation of core sizes). Thus, based on previous studies of tagging, to probe satellite cores, and to ensure good resolution, we would ideally tag all stars up to the $\sim 2\%$ most bound particle in all satellites, satellites of satellites, etc. throughout cosmic history.

source_text: We investigate the effects of self-interacting dark matter (SIDM) on the tidal stripping and evaporation of satellite galaxies in a Milky Way-like host. We use a suite of five zoom-in, dark-matter-only simulations, two with velocity-independent SIDM cross-sections, two with velocity-dependent SIDM cross-sections, and one cold dark matter (CDM) simulation for comparison. After carefully assigning stellar mass to satellites at infall, we find that stars are stripped at a higher rate in SIDM than in CDM. In contrast, the total bound dark matter mass-loss rate is minimally affected, with subhalo evaporation having negligible effects on satellites for viable SIDM models. Centrally located stars in SIDM haloes disperse out to larger radii as cores grow. Consequently, the half-light radius of satellites increases, stars become more vulnerable to tidal stripping, and the stellar mass function is suppressed. suspicious_text: When $G$ is an abelian variety, a trivialization of the $G_{m}$-torsor $\theta(L)$ must satisfy compatibility conditions expressed in terms of biextensions, a notion introduced by Mumford for the study of formal groups and further developed by Grothendieck in a more general context. Specifically, consider the $G_{m}$-torsor $\Lambda = \Lambda(L)$ on $G \times G$ defined by $\Lambda(L) = m^*L \otimes (p_1^*L)^{-1} \otimes (p_2^*L)^{-1}$. A trivialization $t$ of $\theta(L)$ induces two laws on the fibers of $\Lambda$ over a generic point of $G \times G$, which satisfy properties of associativity, commutativity, and compatibility, making $\Lambda$ a biextension of $G \times G$ by $G_{m}$. Furthermore, $\Lambda$ is equipped with a symmetry isomorphism $s: \tau^*\Lambda \simeq \Lambda$, making it a symmetric biextension.
source_text: Having disposed of the geometric terms in the effective action, we now ask how it can depend on the topology of $M$ . It is instructive to look at the case of trivial $G_{0}$ first. Then the action depends only on the topology of $M$ . Since it is also local, it must be an integral of products of characteristic class of $M$ . In the oriented case (i.e. without time-reversal symmetry) there are three kinds of such classes: the Pontryagin classes, with exist in dimensions divisible by $4$ , the Stiefel-Whitney classes, which exist in all dimensions, and the Euler class, which exists in top dimension (but vanishes if the dimension is odd). The Euler class is ruled out, because it is odd under orientation-reversal, and thus its integral over $M$ will be even, while the action is supposed to be odd. Thus $S^{\alpha}_{M}$ must be a linear combination of integrals of products of Pontryagin and Stiefel-Whitney classes over $M$ . These integrals are called Pontryagin and Stiefel-Whitney numbers

suspicious_text: In a counterintuitive scenario, one might inquire if bosonic topological phase transitions (SPT phases) exist that are nontrivial from the perspective of group cohomology but trivial from the cobordism standpoint. Initially, this seems improbable as a nontrivial element in $H^{d}(BG,U(1))$ typically corresponds to a nontrivial topological action $S_{M}^{\alpha}(A)$. However, such a situation may arise due to the more restrictive nature of the topology of smooth orientable manifolds compared to general topological spaces. In these instances, there might be nontrivial elements in $H^{d}(BG,U(1))$ that vanish when pulled back to any closed oriented d-manifold. As demonstrated in [9], this can occur only for d>6 (with the example provided in [9] being for G=G0=Z3×Z3 and d=7), implying that for d≤6, the cobordism classification of SPT phases is stricter than the group cohomology classification, making such phenomena largely academic.
source_text: Going in the opposite direction, one may ask whether there exist bosonic SPT phases which are nontrivial from the point of view of group cohomology but are trivial from the cobordism point of view. At first sight this appears unlikely, since a nontrivial class in $H^{d}(BG,U(1))$ corresponds to a nontrivial topological action $S_{M}^{\alpha}(A)$ . Nevertheless, this might happen because the topology of smooth orientable manifolds is more constrained than the topology of general topological spaces. That is, there may exist nontrivial classes in $H^{d}(BG,U(1))$ which integrate to zero when pulled back to any closed oriented $d$ -manifold. As shown in [9] , this can happen only for $d>6$ (the example given in [9] corresponds to $G=G_{0}={\mathbb{Z}}_{3}\times{\mathbb{Z}}_{3}$ and $d=7$ ), so the issue is largely academic. That is, for $d\leq 6$ the cobordism classification of SPT phases is strictly finer than the group cohomology classification.

suspicious_text: Given the assumption of cobordism-invariant property of $S^{\alpha}_{M}(A)$, the effective action transforms into a homomorphism from $\Omega_{d,SO}(BG,\rho)$ to the unit circle, U(1). For nontrivial $\rho$, all elements in $\Omega_{d,SO}(BG,\rho)$ have an order of 2, implying a lack of continuous parameters in the action. Subsequently, we deduce that SPT phases in this scenario can be indexed by the elements of $\Omega^{d}_{SO}(BG,U(1)^{\rho})$.
source_text: If we assume that $S^{\alpha}_{M}(A)$ is cobordism-invariant, then the effective action becomes a homomorphism from $\Omega_{d,SO}(BG,\rho)$ to $U(1)$ . If $\rho$ is nontrivial, all elements in $\Omega_{d,SO}(BG,\rho)$ have order $2$ , hence the action does not contain continuous parameters. Thus we conclude that SPT phases in this case can be labeled by elements of $\Omega^{d}_{SO}(BG,U(1)^{\rho})$ .

Similarly, O'Shea et al., in their 2005 study, reported an overall positive feedback effect, although the magnitude of this effect remains uncertain due to the omission of photo-evaporation effects from their calculations. Conversely, Oh and Haiman's 2003 findings suggest that the residual entropy of relic HII regions leads to the collapse of gas into low-mass halos with diminished central densities, thereby rendering molecular hydrogen more susceptible to ultraviolet photo-dissociation and resulting in a negative feedback loop. suspicious_text: Let $\mathcal{A} = \{L_1, \dots, L_d\}$ denote a line arrangement in the complex projective plane $\mathbb{P}^2$, where each $L_i$ is defined by the equation $\ell_i(x,y,z) = 0$ for $i = 1, \dots, d$. The defining polynomial of $\mathcal{A}$ is given by $Q = \ell_1 \ell_2 \cdots \ell_d$. In his work [12], F. Hirzebruch examines the Kummer covers of $\mathbb{P}^2$ that are branched along $\mathcal{A}$ and, through desingularization, constructs smooth algebraic surfaces of general type. Moreover, he demonstrates that certain line arrangements yield associated surfaces that are ball quotients. Subsequently, Hirzebruch's method was expanded to a broader framework, as illustrated in [14].

Later, Hirzebruch’s method was extended to a more general setup, see for instance [14] .

Additionally, we know that $b^{\prime}\beta \equiv -1 \pmod{\alpha}$, leading to the conclusion that $\gamma = b^{\prime} - \alpha$. This is because both $\gamma$ and $b^{\prime} - \alpha$ lie within the interval $(-\alpha, 0]$, and the congruence $\beta x \equiv -1 \pmod{\alpha}$ has a unique solution in this range. Assuming the claim, note that by definition, 
 
 $\det\mathbf{G}=\alpha\delta-\beta\gamma=1,$

In addition, 
 
 $\delta=\frac{1+\beta\gamma}{\alpha}=\frac{1+\beta(b^{\prime}-\alpha)}{\alpha}=\frac{1+b^{\prime}\beta}{\alpha}-\beta.$

We have performed simulation of a mixture of elliptic particle with a mild aspect ratio ( $=1.23$ ) and smaller circular impurities with $\sigma_{2}/\sigma_{1}=0.6$ at $20\%$ . We have assumed an angle-dependent attractive interaction between the ellipses and the impurities ( $\propto\zeta$ ), which leads to the homeotropic anchoring of the ellipses around each impurity. These discrepancies have renewed interest in the idea that dark matter may not be collisionless, but could scatter with particles of the same species. The idea of self-interacting dark matter (SIDM) was first proposed by Carlson
et al. (1992) , Machacek
et al. (1993) , de
Laix et al. (1995) , and further explored by Spergel &
Steinhardt (2000) and Firmani et al. (2000) in part to explain the cusp/core issue. In these models, the local particle scattering rate scales as 
 
 $\Gamma(r)\propto\rho(r)\frac{\sigma}{m_{x}}v_{\mathrm{rms}}(r)$  (1) 
 where $\rho$ is the local density, $\frac{\sigma}{m_{x}}$ is the interaction cross-section per unit mass, and $v_{\mathrm{rms}}$ is the rms speed of dark matter particles. Increased scattering in high-density regions transfers energy from the outer halo to the inner halo, increasing velocity dispersion and decreasing density Burkert (2000) . Thus SIDM naturally leads to lower density cores that may better fit observations.

source_text: The perturbation fields are determined by employing the equivalent inclusion method for a single heterogeneity embedded in a matrix and then extended here for multiple heterogeneities. In the equivalent inclusion method , the heterogeneous solid is replaced by an equivalent homogeneous solid with uniform material stiffness $\mbox{\boldmath$\mathbf{C}$}^{0}$ everywhere (Fig. 1 a, b) and suitable stress-free eigenstrains $\mbox{\boldmath$\varepsilon$}^{\tau}_{i}$ applied in the inclusions $\Omega^{i}$ so that the homogeneous equivalent solid has the same mechanical fields as the original heterogeneous solid.

suspicious_text: To further refine our calculations, we take into account the charge transfer mechanism involving hydrogen and helium, wherein a helium ion captures an electron from a neutral hydrogen atom, resulting in the formation of a neutral helium atom and a proton, as described by the reaction ${\rm He}^{+}+{\rm H}\rightarrow{\rm He}+{\rm H}^{+}$. The inverse process, where a neutral helium atom donates an electron to a proton, yielding a helium ion and a neutral hydrogen atom, is also considered. By incorporating these reactions into our analysis, we can accurately determine the abundance of ionized helium. Notably, these processes have a subtle yet discernible impact on the abundance of He$^{+}$ in a partially ionized gas, particularly at temperatures below 5000 K, where their influence becomes more pronounced.

Thermal pressure quickly suppresses the steep density profile and turns it to a nearly flat one within the stellar lifetime. The ionized region has a high temperature of $T\sim 30000$ K in the case with helium. For reference, we also show the temperature profile from a run in which we include only hydrogen. Additional heating by helium ionization is clearly seen. Throughout the evolution, the peak gas velocity is about 30 km/sec, which is nearly 10 times larger than the escape velocity of the host halo. Hence it is expected that almost all the gas will be evacuated within a few tens of million years (for a halo with virial radius $\sim 100$ pc).

source_text: We have studied the formation and structure of early cosmological H ii regions using three-dimensional radiation hydrodynamics simulations. We have also examined in detail the formation of second-generation stars, by following cooling and condensation of primordial gas in relic H ii regions. With the three-dimensional simulations, we can eliminate a number of uncertain assumptions regarding the formation of early H ii regions. First, the simulations allow us to directly calculate the ionizing photon escape fraction from cosmological minihalos. With enough mass resolution, our calculation self-consistently includes the effect of gas clumping. We obtain photon escape fractions of order unity, for the cases with rather massive ( $M_{*}>60M_{\odot}$ ) central stars. While our radiation tranfer scheme is based on a few approximations, the accuracy of the method is tested against the results of previous one-dimensional calculations (KYSU; Whalen et al. 2004). suspicious_text: The hypothesis we propose is that a matter state with short-range interactions can be situated on a curved space-time of any topology, with the partition function being calculable at long distances through field theory. This theory may well be applicable to an extensive array of gapped systems, beyond merely SPT phases. In the immediate vicinity, the system is typically structured on a regular lattice with short-range interactions. However, upon introducing disorder, lattice dislocations and more intricate triangulations become feasible. If the system can be formulated in an Euclidean lattice framework, this applies to both spatial and temporal directions.
source_text: Our basic assumption is that a gapped state of matter with short-range interactions can be put on a curved space-time of arbitrary topology, and that at long distances the partition function can be computed using field theory. This is likely to apply to a wide range of gapped systems, not just SPT phases. At short distances a system is usually defined on a regular lattice, with short-range interactions. However, if we allow for disorder, then dislocations in the lattice are possible, and more general triangulations also become possible. If the system admits a Euclidean lattice formulation, this applies both to space and time directions.

suspicious_text: The graded group $\Omega^{*}_{O}(pt,U(1))$, specifically its dual $\Omega_{O,*}(pt)$, has a simplified structure, as detailed by R. Thom [9]. This group can be recognized as a graded ring, equivalent to the ring of polynomials with ${\mathbb{Z}}_{2}$ coefficients in an infinite number of variables $x_{j}$, where $j > 0$ and $x_{j}$ is not of the form $2^{i}-1$ for any natural number $i$. The degree of the variable $x_{j}$ is $j$. Consequently, the unoriented bordism ring contains generators in degrees $2,4,5,6,8,\ldots$. In lower dimensions, the unoriented bordism groups are as follows: $\Omega_{1,O}(pt)=\Omega_{3,O}(pt)=0$, $\Omega_{2,O}(pt)=\Omega_{5,O}(pt)={\mathbb{Z}}_{2}$, and $\Omega_{4,O}(pt)={\mathbb{Z}}_{2}\times{\mathbb{Z}}_{2}$.

The graded group $\Omega^{*}_{O}(pt,U(1))=\oplus_{d}\ \Omega^{d}_{O}(pt,U(1))$ (or rather its dual $\Omega_{O,*}(pt)$ ) has been computed by R. Thom [9] and has a simple structure. $\Omega_{O,*}(pt)$ is actually a graded ring, and can be identified with the ring of polynomials with ${\mathbb{Z}}_{2}$ coefficients in an infinite number of variables $x_{j}$ for all $j>0$ which are not of the form $2^{i}-1$ for some natural $i$ . The degree of the variable $x_{j}$ is $j$ . Thus the unoriented bordism ring has generators in degree $2,4,5,6,8,\ldots$ . In low dimensions the unoriented bordism groups are 
 
 $\Omega_{1,O}(pt)=\Omega_{3,O}(pt)=0,\quad\Omega_{2,O}(pt)=\Omega_{5,O}(pt)={\mathbb{Z}}_{2},\quad\Omega_{4,O}(pt)={\mathbb{Z}}_{2}\times{\mathbb{Z}}_{2}.$

suspicious_text: We utilized viscous chromohydrodynamics, a framework derived from quantum chromodynamic kinetic theory and viscosity-modified distribution functions, to examine the polarization tensor and refractive index in viscous quark-gluon plasma (QGP). Although this approach may omit certain dynamical details inherent in the kinetic theory, the use of effective parameters in hydrodynamic formalisms can help mitigate discrepancies between kinetic theory and chromohydrodynamics. Despite its phenomenological nature, viscous chromohydrodynamics can still capture essential aspects of QGP physics, making it a valuable tool for investigating the viscous effects on QGP's color electromagnetic properties, which are challenging to study using microscopic kinetic theory descriptions.
source_text: Due to simplicity and applicability to describe polarization effect, we have applied the viscous chromohydrodynamics, which is derived from the QGP kinetic theory and the viscosity-modified distribution function, to determine the polarization tensor and investigate the refraction index in the viscous QGP. It should be noted that some dynamical information will be lost during the derivation from the kinetic theory to the chromohydrodynamics (34) . However, in many cases the discrepancies between both approaches of the kinetic theory and the chromohydrodynamics can be alleviated by using effective parameters as inputs in the hydrodynamic formalisms (34) . Nevertheless, such phenomenological model of the chromohydrodynamics could capture the some correct physics of the QGP (37) . In fact, any two topological phase transition systems displaying the same thermal Hall response can be distinguished by a system with a vanishing thermal Hall response. Therefore, the categorization of general topological phase transition systems is analogous to the categorization of systems with vanishing thermal Hall response, combined with the classification of gravitational Chern-Simons terms, including the mixed ones. The former classification task is relatively uncomplicated.

suspicious_text: The key aspect of the training procedure involves setting the eigenvalue, denoted as the error function \( E \), to zero and subsequently training the neural network using equidistant points within the specified interval of the problem. Ideally, this approach should result in the energy function (eigenvalue) reaching zero or demonstrating a trend toward convergence to zero. If convergence is not achieved, this indicates that the chosen eigenvalue is incorrect. In such a scenario, the eigenvalue must be adjusted appropriately, and the training process must be resumed. This iterative process should continue until the energy (error) function successfully converges to zero.

suspicious_text: The criterion of a negative effective refractive index ($n_{eff}<0$) is commonly employed to determine the presence of negative refraction in a medium, and it is noteworthy to examine the interplay between mode propagation, characterized by the real part of the refractive index (${\rm Re}\,n$), and dissipation, represented by the imaginary part (${\rm Im}\,n$), within the frequency range where negative refraction occurs. Notably, in strongly coupled systems, such as those described by the AdS/CFT correspondence framework, significant dissipation is observed in the frequency range associated with negative refraction, except at very low frequencies ($\omega\rightarrow 0$), where propagation can dominate over dissipation. Further in-depth studies are warranted to understand the impact of shear viscosity on mode propagation in quark-gluon plasmas (QGP).
source_text: The criterion ( 9 ) $n_{eff}<0$ has been widely used to judge the existence of the negative refraction in a medium. It is interesting to study the interplay between the modes propagation ( ${\rm Re}\,n$ ) and dissipation ( ${\rm Im}\,n$ ) in the frequency region for negative refraction. For instance ,in strongly coupled system with the framework of AdS/CFT correspondence,it was found that there exists strong dissipation in the frequency region for negative refraction, only around $\omega\rightarrow 0$ , propagation may dominate over dissipation (25) . How the shear viscosity impacts the mode propagation in QGP deserves further comprehensive studies.

source_text: In the present paper we shall use analytical techniques in order to explore the physical properties of the critical (marginally-stable) exotic compact objects. In particular, below we shall explicitly prove that horizonless spinning compact objects with reflective boundary conditions are characterized by the existence of a discrete set $\{r_{\text{c}}({\bar{a}};n)\}^{n=\infty}_{n=1}$ of critical surface radii that can support spatially regular static (marginally-stable) massless scalar field configurations (17) [Here ${\bar{a}}$ is the dimensionless angular momentum of the exotic compact object, see Eq. ( 12 ) below].

suspicious_text: The destiny of out-flowing gas is a crucial factor in determining the prompt triggering of star formation following the initial star-formation epoch, as it significantly influences the star-formation rate in more massive systems that form at later stages. The efficient re-incorporation of halo gas is essential for effective gas cooling, and our findings indicate that the recollapse of ionized gas occurs through a complex process. This complexity is attributed to the hierarchical assembly of dark matter halos, which can lead to changes in the mass hierarchy of halos over time. Our simulation illustrates this phenomenon, where the primary halo hosting the first star-forming cloud exhibits slow growth, while smaller halos grow more rapidly, and gas cooling and condensation occur at the center of a nearby halo, approximately 0.5 kpc away, at a later epoch.
source_text: The fate of the out-flowing gas is of considerable interest, because it determines whether or not star-formation can be triggered promptly after the epoch of first star-formation. In other words, the gas cooling efficiency (and hence star-formation rate) in higher mass systems that are formed later on is critically dependent on how efficiently the halo gas is re-incorporated. As we show below, recollapse of the ionized gas occurs in a very complex manner. First, hierarchical assembly of dark matter halos is itself a complex process, and it is often found that the descendant of an early massive halo at one epoch is not the most massive one at a later epoch (e.g. Yoshida et al. 2003; Gao et al. 2005). We find exactly such a case in our simulation, where the main halo at $z=26$ , which is the host of the first star-forming cloud, grows rather slowly, and a few other smaller halos (at $z=26$ ) grow more quickly. suspicious_text: This study does not directly calculate the shear viscosity coefficient in viscous chromohydrodynamics, instead treating it as a variable input to investigate its impact on the refraction index of the quark-gluon plasma (QGP). A low ratio of shear viscosity to entropy density, $\eta/s \leq 0.2$, has been inferred from comparisons between viscous hydrodynamic simulations and RHIC data, which falls below three times the theoretical lower bound of $\eta/s = \frac{1}{4\pi}$ derived from the AdS/CFT correspondence for strongly coupled conformal field theories. The numerical results for the refraction index are presented for specific values of $\eta/s$, and the analysis utilizes scales of $k=0.2\omega_{p}$ and $T=\omega_{p}$ to examine the frequency-dependent behavior of the refraction index.
source_text: In this paper, we can not determine shear viscosity coefficient itself in viscous chromohydrodynamics, but regard it as an input parameter to study the viscous effect on the refraction index of the QGP. A small value of the ratio for shear viscosity to entropy density $\eta/s\leq 0.2$ has been deduced from comparison of casual viscous hydrodynamic simulation results with the RHIC data (13) , which is less than three times of the famous bound result $\eta/s=\frac{1}{4\pi}$ of the strongly couple conformal field theory determined by the AdS/CFT correspondence (49) . Numerical results of the refraction index are presented with those explicit values of $\eta/s$ . In addition, in numerical analysis, such scales $k=0.2\omega_{p}$ and $T=\omega_{p}$ are used to study the $\omega$ -dependent behavior of the refraction index.

source_text: In the case when the symmetry group $G$ involves time-reversing elements, it has been proposed that bosonic SPT phases are classified by elements of the degree- $d$ cohomology group of $BG$ with coefficients in a certain flat $U(1)$ bundle over $BG$ [2] . However, it was noticed later that certain 4d bosonic SPT phases with time-reversal symmetry do not fit into this classification [3] .

Based on the findings of KYSU, a shock velocity of $v_{\rm shock}=25{\rm km}{\rm s}^{-1}$ is adopted, which, although dependent on density profile and infalling gas velocity, remains relatively consistent across the considered density profiles, ranging from $25-35{\rm km}{\rm s}^{-1}$, as also supported by Shu et al. (2002). For photons arriving within $R_{\rm s}$, their arrival times coincide with the shock arrival time, whereas following the transition to an R-type I-front, photon arrival times are computed using Equation (15) as outlined in the preceding section.
source_text: In order to reproduce the D-to-R transition in three-dimensional calculations, we first evaluate the initial Strömgren radius using Equation ( 17 ) for the density profile around the source, and assume that the I-front moves at a constant shock velocity while it is of D-type. From the results of KYSU, we set the shock velocity $v_{\rm shock}=25{\rm km}{\rm s}^{-1}$ . Although the exact value of $v_{\rm shock}$ is, in principle, dependent on the density profile and the infalling gas velocity, it is a weak function of the density slope over the range of gas density profiles considered in KYSU, and is about $25-35{\rm km}{\rm s}^{-1}$ (see also Shu et al. 2002). Photon arrival times within $R_{\rm s}$ are simply given by the time when the shock arrives. Once the I-front transforms to R-type, we can use Equation ( 15 ) to compute photon arrival times as described in the previous section.

suspicious_text: -based classification systems (FRBCS) are a type of fuzzy inference system that combines the principles of fuzzy logic with machine learning techniques to perform classification tasks. In these systems, fuzzy rules are used to model the relationships between input variables and output classes, allowing for the representation of uncertainty and imprecision inherent in many real-world problems. FRBCS has been widely applied in various domains, such as pattern recognition, medical diagnosis, and financial analysis, due to its ability to handle vague and ambiguous information effectively. The development of FRBCS often involves the use of techniques like fuzzy c-means clustering, genetic algorithms, and particle swarm optimization to optimize the fuzzy rule base and improve classification accuracy.

suspicious_text: SIDM presents a potential solution to the "too big to fail" problem and the cusp/core issues observed in satellite and field galaxies within the Local Group, as demonstrated by Vogelsberger et al. (2012). Particle scattering reduces the central density of dark matter halos, forming lower-density cores. Current constraints on velocity-independent SIDM suggest an interaction cross-section range of $0.1\leq\sigma/m_{x}\leq 1\,\mathrm{cm^{2}/g}$, as reported by Rocha et al. (2013). However, velocity-dependent SIDM models, where the cross-section scales approximately as $\sigma\propto 1/v^{\alpha}$ for some $\alpha$, remain largely unconstrained. This study investigates new signatures of SIDM in satellite galaxies and their interactions with host halos to aid in identifying or constraining SIDM.
source_text:
SIDM may offer a resolution to the too big to fail problem and cusp/core issues of satellite and field galaxies in the Local Group Vogelsberger et al. (2012) . Scattering between particles drives mass away from dark matter halo central density cusps, creating lower density cores. Currently, constraints on velocity-independent SIDM places the interesting range of the interaction cross-section at $0.1\leq\sigma/m_{x}\leq 1\,\mathrm{cm^{2}/g}$ Rocha
et al. (2013) . However, the class of velocity-dependent SIDM models, in which the cross-section scales approximately as $\sigma\propto 1/v^{\alpha}$ for some $\alpha$ , remain largely unconstrained. In this paper, we search for new signatures of SIDM on satellite galaxies and their interactions with a host halo to ultimately aid in identifying or constraining SIDM.

source_text: QNMs are single frequency modes dominating the time evolution of perturbations of systems which are subject to damping, either by internal dissipation or by radiating away energy. Due to the damping, the frequency of a QNM must be complex, its imaginary part being inversely proportional to the typical damping time. Recall that, in general relativity, damping occurs even without friction, since energy may be radiated away towards infinity by gravitational waves (1) . Thus, they could lead to the direct identification of the existence of the BH through gravitational wave observation, which might be realized in the near future. QNMs, which are believed to be characteristic sounds of perturbed black hole (BH) spacetimes have been investigated for a long time and their physical properties have been presented in various studies (see for example (3) ). It is worth noting that similar to the BH geometries, QNMs can also arise from other spacetimes including the wormholes (33) .

suspicious_text: For time-independent Hamiltonians, we can derive an additional implication, focusing on the probability that a system remains in its initial state over time. This probability, denoted as $P(t)$, is related to the square of the absolute value of the inner product of the system's state at time $t$ and its initial state. By exploring this relationship, we find that the distance between the system's state and its initial state, represented as $d^2(t)$, is connected to $P(t)$ through a series of inequalities. Ultimately, this leads to the conclusion that $P(t)$ is bounded by an expression involving $d^2(t)$, which can further be related to the energy uncertainty of the initial state and time. This relationship reveals that for short times, the probability of the system remaining in its initial state decreases more slowly than an exponential function of time, specifically bounded by a Gaussian-like expression dependent on the energy uncertainty and time.

suspicious_text: A degree sequence is defined as a finite sequence $\textbf{d}_{n}=(d_{n,1},\ldots,d_{n,n})$ that satisfies two conditions: the sum of its elements equals $n$ and all elements are non-negative integers. A random functional graph with a given degree sequence $\textbf{d}_{n}$ is constructed by randomly selecting a function $F$ from the set $\mathfrak{F}(\textbf{d}_{n})$, which consists of all functions $f$ from the set $[n]$ to itself such that the pre-image of each element $i$ in $[n]$ has a cardinality of $d_{n,i}$. Note that the term "degree sequence" refers to the indegree sequence of the resulting directed graph, as the outdegree of each node is uniformly equal to 1.

suspicious_text: We expand the applicability of diffraction theory beyond numerical propagation of matter waves by deriving approximate analytical expressions for the parameters of the standing-wave (SW) field lens. The Fresnel-Kirchhoff integral, accounting for the lowest-order spherical aberration, provides a simplified form for the atomic wave function near the focal plane and SW intensity extrema. As the atom-field pulse areas increase, this integral yields a universal atomic density profile, elucidating the dependence of the focal distance, peak atomic density, focal spot size (half-width at half-maximum, or HWHM, of the atom density at the focus), depth of focus, and background density on the pulse area. The analytical asymptotic results can be compared to either numerical asymptotic approximations of the Fresnel integral or to the precise atomic distributions and lens parameters, which can be calculated more efficiently using a Fourier approach.
source_text: Rather than using the diffraction theory simply for numerical propagation of the matter waves, we extend its usefulness by deriving approximate analytical expressions for the SW field lens parameters. The Fresnel-Kirchhoff integral, incorporating the lowest-order spherical aberration, gives a relatively simple form for the atomic wave function near the focal plane and SW intensity extrema. Asymptotically for large atom-field pulse areas, this integral leads to a universal atomic density profile, revealing the behavior of the focal distance, peak atomic density, focal spot size [half-width at half-maximum (HWHM) of the atom density at the focus], depth of focus, and background density as a function of the pulse area. The analytical asymptotic results can then be compared to either numerical asymptotic approximations of the Fresnel integral or to the exact atomic distributions and lens parameters, most easily evaluated using a Fourier approach.

In the realm of condensed matter theory, a significant conundrum lies in the elucidation of symmetry-protected topological (SPT) phases of matter. These phases, characterized by a symmetry group G and an absence of long-range entanglement, constitute distinct equivalence classes, akin to the concepts of homotopy theory. The designation 'symmetry-protected' implies that these phases are differentiated based on their equivalence, rather than their intrinsic properties.

The classification of SPT phases for free fermionic systems, whether translationally invariant or not, has been substantially elucidated. However, the landscape becomes more intricate when dealing with interacting systems. In this discourse, we shall focus on the interacting scenario for the sake of simplicity, and for the avoidance of doubt, we will restrict our discussion to theories devoid of fermions. The SPT phases under consideration are resilient to disorder, as we do not assume translational invariance.

This is achieved by introducing $\alpha = \alpha(n, k) = \frac{k}{n}$, which allows $g_{n}(k)$ to be expressed in an alternative form. By defining a binomially distributed variable $B_{n}$ as $B(n, \alpha)$ and independent Bernoulli distributed random variables $X_{1}, \ldots, X_{n}$ with probabilities $\mathbb{P}(X_{i} = 1) = \frac{\alpha d_{i}}{\alpha d_{i} + 1}$, the sum $S_{n} = X_{1} + \cdots + X_{n}$ can be utilized to derive an equivalent expression for $g_{n}(k)$. This yields $g_{n}(k) = (1 - \alpha)^{n - k} \prod_{j = 1}^{n} (\alpha d_{j} + 1) \frac{\mathbb{P}(S_{n} = k)}{\mathbb{P}(B_{n} = k)}$, providing a representation that is amenable to Poisson approximation.
source_text: At the very early stage of the relativistic heavy ion collisions, named glasma stage (17) , and the late stage of the evolution process in the near $T_{c}$ region in the so-called magnetic scenario for the QGP (18) , there are color-electric flux tubes which contain strong color-electric fields in them. Therefore, the color electromagnetic properties may play an important role in the evolution of hot and dense matter produced in heavy ion collisions. In addition, color electromagnetic properties could reflect the response of QGP to external color current, so the study of them may be helpful for understanding the nature of QGP. However, to the best of our knowledge, the study on the color electromagnetic properties of viscous QGP is scare in literature. It makes sense to study how the viscosity affects them and how the viscous electromagnetic properties affect the evolution of produced matter in heavy ion collisions.

suspicious_text: In this study, we employ locally randomized measurements to directly quantify entanglement through the multiparticle concurrence (45), a measure of entanglement in multipartite systems. We derive an exact expression for the multiparticle concurrence of pure states and establish a suitable lower bound for mixed states, both obtained from the second moments of random measurement outcomes. Additionally, we thoroughly analyze the required measurement resources for statistically reliable estimation of these quantities by examining their variances. The developed methodology is applied to assess the entanglement of typical multipartite states and ensembles generated by distinct classes of random quantum circuits (see Fig. 1). Lastly, we examine the entanglement of mixed states resulting from noisy quantum circuits characterized by gates susceptible to non-negligible depolarization errors.
source_text: In this work we use locally randomized measurements in order to directly extract information about the amount of entanglement in terms of the multiparticle concurrence (45) , a quantifier for multiparticle entanglement. In particular, we derive an exact formula for the multiparticle concurrence of pure states, as well as for an appropriate lower bound in the case of mixed states, from second moments of the outcomes of random measurements. Furthermore, we analyze in detail the measurement resources required for a statistically confident estimation of the involved quantities by analysing the respective variances. We apply the developed toolbox to evaluate the multiparticle entanglement of typical multiparticle states as well as of ensembles of states produced by different classes of random quantum circuits (see Fig. 1 ). Finally, we investigate the multiparticle entanglement of mixed states produced by noisy quantum circuits consisting of gates prone with non-vanishing depolarization err

To analyze systems at low densities, the virial expansion offers a convenient framework. In this approach, various thermodynamic quantities are expressed as power series in terms of the fugacity parameter \( z = e^{\beta \mu} \), where \( T = 1/\beta \) represents the temperature and \( \mu \) is the chemical potential. The particle density \( n \) and pressure \( P \) can be written as:

\[
n = \frac{1}{\lambdabar^{3}} \left( b_{1}z + 2b_{2}z^{2} + 3b_{3}z^{3} + \cdots \right),
\]

\[
P = \frac{T}{\lambdabar^{3}} \left( b_{1}z + b_{2}z^{2} + b_{3}z^{3} + \cdots \right).
\]

This formulation provides a systematic way to express thermodynamic properties in terms of fugacity expansions, as shown in equations (1).

suspicious_text: The TT02 ASIC, referred to hereafter as TT02, is engineered to handle 32 APD signal channels. Its primary function is to enhance the signal-to-noise ratio and transmit the conditioned signals to a Wilkinson-type analog-to-digital converter (ADC). The TT02 is manufactured using XFAB 0.35 micron CMOS technology, featuring 4-metal and MIM (Metal-Insulator-Metal) options, and housed in a 160-pin ceramic package (QC-160360-WZ, KYOCERA). The TT02's electrical design leverages a technology from the Open IP project, which has accumulated various radiation detector technologies, such as [5]. Notably, due to the significant APD capacitance of approximately 100 pF resulting in substantial capacitive noise, the TT02 incorporates a clever noise reduction mechanism, as detailed in Section 2.5.
source_text: The TT02APD32 ASIC (henceforth TT02) is designed to read out 32 APD signal channels. Its main function is to condition the signal with a good signal-to-noise ratio and feed them into a Wilkinson-type analog-to-digital converter (ADC). The TT02 is fabricated based on the XFAB 0.35 $\mu$ m CMOS technology with 4-metal and MIM (Metal-Insulator-Metal) options, and assembled in a ceramic package of 160 pins (QC-160360-WZ, KYOCERA). The electrical architecture of the TT02 utilizes a property of the Open IP project, which has amassed technologies and the development of several radiation detectors (e.g., [5] ). In particular, since the large APD capacitance of $\sim$ 100 pF leads to a corresponding large capacitive noise, the TT02 implements an ingenious noise reduction scheme (see Section 2.5 ).

As explained above, such SPT phases are classified by the group $\Omega_{SO}^{d}(B{\mathbb{Z}}_{2},E{\mathbb{Z}}_{2})$ . By definition, a twisted oriented bordism to $(B{\mathbb{Z}}_{2},E{\mathbb{Z}}_{2})$ is a map $f$ from a closed $d$ -manifold $M$ to $B{\mathbb{Z}}_{2}$ such that the pull-back of the universal ${\mathbb{Z}}_{2}$ -bundle is $\xi_{M}$ , the orientation bundle of $M$ . Since the map to $BG$ is determined up to homotopy by the pull-back of the universal bundle, this means that up to homotopy $f$ is determined by the orientation bundle of $M$ . Hence the set of equivalence classes of twisted oriented bordisms in this case is the same as the set of equivalence classes of unoriented bordisms to a point. The investigation of gluon refraction in QGP is pertinent due to its potential to elucidate the properties of QGP, particularly in the context of jet quenching, which has been an active area of research in heavy ion collisions over the past three decades. As viscosity alters the distribution functions of QGP constituents, it subsequently influences the gluon self-energy, thereby affecting the electric permittivity and magnetic permeability, and ultimately impacting the refractive index, as suggested by previous research.
source_text: There is another interesting question about the formation of the so-called second-generation stars. Unlike the somewhat simple and ‘clean’ initial conditions for the first stars, second generation stars are likely born from a gas that has been disturbed by earlier feedback effects. Among the most important of these effects is photo-ionization by the first stars. An ionized gas cools faster than it recombines, and thus many free electrons are left over (Shapiro & Kang 1987; Susa et al. 1998). When the temperature drops below $\sim 8000$ K, atomic hydrogen Lyman- $\alpha$ cooling becomes inefficient but then hydrogen molecules are rapidly formed using the abundant free electrons as a catalyst. The gas cools by H \textsubscript{2} line cooling to $\sim 100$ K. In a primordial gas, HD molecules act as an efficient coolant at such low temperatures, enabling the gas to cool down to a few tens Kelvin (Flower et al. 2000). suspicious_text: The topological actions associated with these SPT phases can be written as integrals of polynomial functions of the Stiefel-Whitney classes (SWCs). These SWCs, $w_j \in H^j(M, \mathbb{Z}_2)$, exist for all integers $j$ within the range $0 < j \leq d$, where $d$ is the dimension of the manifold $M$. The lower SWCs have a straightforward geometrical interpretation. For instance, $w_1$ represents an orientation connection on $M$, while $w_2$ denotes an obstruction to establishing a spin structure on $M$. The integral of the top SWC, $w_d(M)$, equals the Euler characteristic modulo 2. There are dependencies among the product of SWCs that rely on $d$. Thom's theorem indicates that the unoriented bordism class of $M$ is determined by the SW numbers of $M$ (integrals of polynomial functions of SWCs over $M$). Considering the relationships among SWCs results in the stated result for the unoriented cobordism groups of a point.

source_text: Figure 4.1 shows the radial profiles for density, velocity, and temperature at $t=0$ and $t=3$ Myrs. Thermal pressure quickly suppresses the steep density profile and turns it to a nearly flat one within the stellar lifetime. The ionized region has a high temperature of $T\sim 30000$ K in the case with helium. For reference, we also show the temperature profile from a run in which we include only hydrogen. Additional heating by helium ionization is clearly seen. Throughout the evolution, the peak gas velocity is about 30 km/sec, which is nearly 10 times larger than the escape velocity of the host halo. Hence it is expected that almost all the gas will be evacuated within a few tens of million years (for a halo with virial radius $\sim 100$ pc).

source_text: An exclusion from this rule is the appearance of internal stagnation points —fluid particles with zero velocity relative to the steady flow—and critical layers . By a critical layer we mean a connected part of the fluid domain consisting only of closed streamlines. The existence of such waves within linear theory was established in [13] , where the streamlines and particle paths of waves with constant vorticity were studied. For irrotational flows such behavior is not encountered, cf. [3] . Also earlier studies had indicated that internal stagnation should be possible (see, e.g., [16] ), but it was first with [26] that waves with critical layers were given a firm mathematical foundation (see also [9] ). A different approach in dealing with the free boundary allowed for including internal stagnation, a phenomenon which for technical reasons had not been possible to see with earlier methods for rotational waves (cf., e.g., [7] ).

source_text: Only on special circumstances the thermodynamic functions of a system can be evaluated starting from the microscopic interactions. However, in many situations only a few characteristics of the microscopic interactions are relevant. All systems sharing these same microscopic characteristics have then the same macroscopic behavior, that is, there is a certain degree of universality. An example of such a system is a gas of particles with short range interactions. As long as the density and temperature are such that the typical wavelength $\lambda$ of the particles is much larger than the range $R$ of the forces, the details of the interaction potential is largely irrelevant and the thermodynamics is determined by the two-body scattering length $a$ , up to corrections of order $\lambda/R$ . source_text: Stochastic resetting implies a situation, where an ongoing dynamical process is stopped at random time epochs to start anew (2) . It has gained overwhelming attention in recent times because of its spontaneous ubiquity in numerous natural and man made systems. For example, stock market crashes may reset the asset prices by drastically reducing those to some prior values (7) . Epidemics and natural disasters may have a similar effect on the population of a living species in a certain locality (8) . Search processes may also reset (9) ; examples include foraging animals returning to their habitats (13) due to fatigue or extreme weather. In computer science, it has long been known that resetting certain algorithms may significantly enhance their performance by reducing the effective run times (15) . For $d=4$ there are two independent topological actions, since $w_{3}w_{1}=w_{2}w_{1}^{2}=0$ , and $w_{4}+w_{2}^{2}+w_{1}^{4}=0$ . A possible choice of generators for $\Omega_{SO,d}^{4}(pt,U(1))$ is $w_{1}^{4}$ and $w_{2}^{2}$ . Thus we expect three nontrivial bosonic SPT phases in $d=4$ , in agreement with [4] . Note that group cohomology classification sees only one of these three phases. We can figure out which action corresponds to this special SPT phase by noting that the group cohomology approach is based on classifying actions which depend only on the gauge field on $M$ , which in this case is the first Stiefel-Whitney class $w_{1}\in H^{1}(M,{\mathbb{Z}}_{2})$ . Thus the group cohomology approach detects the action 
 
 $S_{M}=\frac{1}{2}\int_{M}w_{1}^{4},$ 
 but is unable to see $w_{2}^{2}$ . Note that on spin manifolds $w_{2}=0$ , so the additional SPT phases are possible only in theories without fermions.

source_text: In our simulation, there is a ‘second’ halo at $\sim 2$ kpc away, in which the gas cools and condenses after the first star dies (see Halo B in Fig. 4.1 ). Interestingly, Halo B has a slightly larger mass than the main halo (Halo A) at $z=26$ , but the growth of Halo B has been so rapid that significant dynamical heating associated with progenitor mergers prevented the gas in Halo B from cooling, as often found in simulations of early structure formation (Yoshida et al. 2003; Reed et al. 2005; O’Shea & Norman 2007). Hence the first star is formed earlier in Halo A. At the position of Halo B, the dissociation (owing to the stellar radiation from Halo A) timescale is just about 1 Myrs (from equation [ 23 ]). The H \textsubscript{2} fraction first slightly decreases, but rapid re-formation of H \textsubscript{2} leads to efficient cooling and condensation within a few million years after the first star dies. source_text: We start from a low-resolution simulation of boxsize $1.4$ Mpc on a side. We identify one of the most massive halos in the simulation volume at a redshift of 20, and then make zoomed initial conditions around this object with a much higher mass resolution (see Fig. 4.1 ). In the highest resolution region, the gas particle mass is $1.82M_{\odot}$ and the dark matter particle mass is $11.8M_{\odot}$ . We use the parallel Tree $N$ -body/SPH solver GADGET2 (Springel 2005) to evolve the system from $z=100$ to the epoch when the first collapsed gas cloud is identified. The code includes all the relevant atomic and molecular processes as described in Yoshida et al. (2003, 2006). Figure 4.1 shows the simulated high-resolution region and a boundary low-resolution region. We locate a cold ( $T<500$ K) and dense ( $n>10^{3}\;{\rm cm}^{-3}$ ) cloud whose mass exceeds $300M_{\odot}$ as a site for star-formation. The critical mass is obtained from simulations of Abel et al. (2002) and Yoshida et al.

suspicious_text: In a more comprehensive cosmological framework, the presence of external radiation from other stars and galaxies formed prior to the first star in the simulation must be considered, as it can significantly impact the intergalactic medium. The intergalactic medium is largely transparent to far-UV radiation that can dissociate molecules, allowing for the rapid establishment of an early UV background radiation as stars and galaxies form. However, the net effect of this feedback on relic H ii regions remains uncertain, with some studies suggesting a positive feedback and others indicating a negative feedback relative to primordial neutral regions. The strength of the background Lyman-Werner radiation appears to be a crucial factor in determining the outcome, with a critical value existing below which H2 cooling in relic H ii regions is substantially suppressed. Nevertheless, existing simulations may underestimate this critical value due to the omission of gas self-shielding effects.

To further understand the impact of radiative feedback, an additional simulation was conducted where the first star's radiative feedback was omitted, allowing the assessment of star formation in its absence. The results showed that the normalized baryon fraction in Halo A remained steady at approximately 0.9, while the mass of cold and dense gas increased substantially, from 300 $M_{\odot}$ at $z=26$ to over 10,000 $M_{\odot}$ by $z=18$. This suggests that, without radiative feedback from the first star, a significant amount of cold gas would be available to fuel subsequent star formation. Consequently, it can be inferred that the first star's radiative feedback has a net negative effect on the efficiency of star formation in the same location.

suspicious_text: The formation of second-generation stars is a complex process that differs significantly from that of the first stars, which formed in relatively simple and pristine environments. The gas from which second-generation stars are born has been disturbed by the feedback effects of earlier stars, including photo-ionization, which leaves behind a gas rich in free electrons. As this gas cools, it undergoes a series of chemical reactions, including the formation of hydrogen molecules, which enables it to cool further to temperatures around 100 K. At these low temperatures, deuterium hydride molecules become an important coolant, allowing the gas to cool to just a few tens of Kelvin. This cooling process is thought to play a crucial role in the formation of primordial brown dwarfs and may even influence the final stages of collapse in the formation of second-generation stars. suspicious_text: Our model employs the H₂ line cooling rate from Galli & Palla (1998) and the HD line cooling rate from Flower et al. (2000) at low densities. The contribution of HD molecules to cooling is significant only under specific conditions, namely low temperatures (below 200 K) and low densities (less than 10⁸ cm⁻³). At elevated temperatures, H₂ cooling predominates due to the decreasing abundance of HD relative to H₂. Although the HD cooling function of Flower et al. excludes transitions between high vibrational levels, the associated contribution to the total cooling rate is negligible at relevant densities, as demonstrated by Lipovka et al. (2005), and HD cooling remains effective at temperatures as low as approximately 30 K.
source_text: We use the cooling rate of Galli & Palla (1998) for H \textsubscript{2} line cooling, and that of Flower et al. (2000) for HD line cooling at low densities. Cooling by HD molecules is important only at low temperatures ( $T<200{\rm K}$ ) and low densities ( $n_{\rm H}<10^{8}{\rm cm}^{-3}$ ). At high temperatures, H \textsubscript{2} cooling dominates because the HD abundance decreases relative to H \textsubscript{2} (see section 2.4 ). The HD cooling function of Flower et al. does not include transitions between high vibrational levels but the contribution to the total cooling rate at the relevant densities are unimportant, as shown by Lipovka et al. (2005). HD cooling is effective at temperatures as low as $\sim 30$ K.

source_text: In the analysis of materials with complex microstructures, full resolution of the heterogeneities using classical numerical approaches such as the Finite Element method can be computationally prohibitive. To overcome this, one option is to model the macroscale problem using equivalent properties; however, this can lead to a critical loss of information about the finer scale behaviour and poor understanding of the heterogeneities’ influence on the macroscale response. Numerical approaches such as computational homogenization (often called $\textrm{FE}^{2}$ ) provide an alternative strategy [1] . These techniques comprise nested Finite Element analyses, where each macroscopic material point response is determined via the numerical solution of an RVE subject to the macroscopic strains. Although such approaches have significant potential for certain classes of problems, they are still computationally demanding and are restricted to situations involving clear separation of scales.

suspicious_text: In the context of functional graphs, a specific measurement known as the six-length of a vertex is determined by a function's repetitive application. This measurement, denoted as $\mathfrak{s}_{f}(v)$ for a vertex $v$, essentially calculates the minimum number of times a function $f$ must be applied to $v$ such that the result is a vertex that has been previously visited during these applications. The process involves composing the function $f$ with itself multiple times, starting from the identity function, and tracking the sequence of vertices obtained. Interestingly, this six-length can be broken down into two components: the tail-length, which represents the initial sequence of unique vertices before a cycle is entered, and the cycle-length, which is the size of the cycle itself. These components provide insight into the structure of the graph by identifying how a vertex is connected within a cycle and how it enters that cycle.
source_text: Since the PZC circuit does not include a time constant, the shaping amplifier is configured as a second-order low-pass filter, and the entire transfer function, including the PZC circuit, is equivalent to an ordinary CR–RC filter. The peaking time of the slow shaper is determined by a set of $R_{\rm 1}$ , $R_{\rm 2}$ , $C_{\rm 1}$ , and $C_{\rm 2}$ . To obtain critical damping in order to reduce undershooting of the output signal, the peaking time $\tau_{\rm peak,slow}$ = $C_{\rm 1}R_{\rm 1}$ = 4 $C_{\rm 2}R_{\rm 2}$ must be satisfied for the slow shaper. Similarly for the fast shaper, we impose $\tau_{\rm peak,fast}$ = $C_{\rm 1}^{\prime}R_{\rm 1}^{\prime}$ = 4 $C^{\prime}_{\rm 2}R_{\rm 2}^{\prime}$ on the peaking time. At the output stage of the slow shaper, the pulse height is acquired on a capacitor $C_{\rm H}$ at the timing of the HOLD signal, called the sample and hold (S/H).

suspicious_text: In a given manifold M, whether oriented or not, there exists a principal Z2 bundle, referred to as the orientation bundle of M. The holonomy of the associated Z2-connection assigns a value of -1 to loops that reverse orientation and 1 to those that do not. For a closed manifold M, regardless of orientation, the fundamental class resides in Hd(M,ZT), where ZT is a local system with fiber Z twisted by the orientation bundle. The Pontryagin-dual of this is Hd(M,U(1)T), where U(1)T is a local system over M with fiber U(1) twisted by the orientation bundle.
source_text: Recall that a manifold $M$ , whether oriented or not, carries a canonical principal ${\mathbb{Z}}_{2}$ bundle $\xi_{M}$ called the orientation bundle of $M$ . The holonomy of the associated ${\mathbb{Z}}_{2}$ -connection assigns $-1$ to all closed loops which reverse orientation and assigns $+1$ to all closed loops which do not. A closed manifold $M$ , whether oriented or not, has the fundamental class living in $H_{d}(M,{\mathbb{Z}}^{T})$ , where ${\mathbb{Z}}^{T}$ is a local system with fiber ${\mathbb{Z}}$ twisted by the orientation bundle. Its Pontryagin-dual is $H^{d}(M,U(1)^{T})$ , where $U(1)^{T}$ is a local system over $M$ with fiber $U(1)$ twisted by the orientation bundle.

suspicious_text: This study examines random directed graphs, referred to as functional graphs or random mappings, where all out-degrees are uniformly equal to 1. The primary motivation behind this research stems from its potential to enhance the understanding of Pollard's $\rho$-algorithm and its subsequent improvement by Brent and Pollard for integer factorization. Specifically, the runtime of these algorithms is influenced by the $\rho$-length of a polynomial in $\mathbb{F}_{p}[x]$. Assuming that polynomials modulo $p$ exhibit behavior similar to that of random mappings, this investigation focuses on determining the $\rho$-length of random mappings. Building on the work of Martins and Panario, who explored the $\rho$-length of polynomials in $\mathbb{F}_{p}[x]$ under various random models, our objective is to establish baseline results for the $\rho$-length of random functional graphs with specified in-degree sequences, thereby providing a comparative framework for assessing random polynomial models.
source_text: We consider random directed graphs with all out-degrees equal to 1, which we call functional graphs (see Section 2 for further notation) or random mappings. The motivation in most of the related literature is a better understanding of Pollard’s $\rho$ -algorithm [7] for integer factorisation, or the improved version by Brent and Pollard [3] . The runtime depends on the six-length (also called $\rho$ -length) of a polynomial in $\mathbb{F}_{p}[x]$ . (Pollard’s first version used $x^{2}-1$ .) Under the assumption that a polynomial mod $p$ ‘behaves like’ a random mapping (supported by some research listed below), we are interested in the six-length of random mappings in particular. Martins and Panario [6] studied polynomials in $\mathbb{F}_{p}[x]$ , in particular the six-length in several random models. source_text: Thirdly, we combine all satellites regardless of infall time. Satellites with an earlier infall time have systematically higher rates of stripping in both SIDM and CDM. This is not physical, but instead due to particles being tagged further from the subhalo centre as compared to recently accreted satellites. Fewer unscattered particles remain in satellites that fall in early since there is more time for scattering to occur between infall and $z=0$ . Consequently, we must go to larger distances on average to find unscattered particles. In spite of this effect, the relative difference between SIDM and CDM stripping remains the same independent of infall time. It does, however, mean that the fraction of stars stripped in our simulation data is likely an overestimate of the fraction that would be stripped in a real Milky Way satellite.

The anisotropic particles are modeled as ellipses. For two such particles, \(i\) and \(j\), the potential energy \(U_{ij}\) attains its minimum value at a separation distance \(r_{ij} = 2^{1/6}(1 + A_{ij})^{1/6}\sigma_{1}\), considering fixed orientations. The parameter \(A_{ij}\) reaches its minimum when the unit vectors \({\mbox{\boldmath$n$}}_{i}\) and \({\mbox{\boldmath$n$}}_{j}\) are orthogonal to the line connecting the particles, \(\hat{\mbox{\boldmath$r$}}_{ij}\), and it reaches its maximum when these vectors are aligned with \(\pm\hat{\mbox{\boldmath$r$}}_{ij}\). The minor and major axes of these ellipses are defined as:

\[
a_{s} = 2^{1/6}\sigma_{1}, \quad a_{\ell} = (1 + 2\chi)^{1/6}2^{1/6}\sigma_{1}. \quad (6)
\]

Consequently, the aspect ratio is expressed as:

\[
\frac{a_{\ell}}{a_{s}} = (1 + 2\chi)^{1/6}. \quad (7)
\]

These ellipses possess an area \(S_{1} = \pi{a_{s}}a_{\ell}/4\) and a moment of inertia \(I = (a_{\ell}^{2} + a_{s}^{2})m_{1}/16\).

We regard the anisotropic particles as ellipses. For two anisotropic particles $i$ and $j$ , $U_{ij}$ is minimized at $r_{ij}=2^{1/6}(1+A_{ij})^{1/6}\sigma_{1}$ as a function of $r_{ij}$ for fixed orientations. Then $A_{ij}$ is minimum for ${\mbox{\boldmath$n$}}_{i}$ and ${\mbox{\boldmath$n$}}_{j}$ being perpendicular to ${\hat{\mbox{\boldmath$r$}}}_{ij}$ , while it is maximum for ${\mbox{\boldmath$n$}}_{i}$ and ${\mbox{\boldmath$n$}}_{j}$ being parallel to $\pm{\hat{\mbox{\boldmath$r$}}}_{ij}$ . The shortest and longest diameters are given by 
 
 $a_{s}=2^{1/6}\sigma_{1},\quad a_{\ell}=(1+2\chi)^{1/6}2^{1/6}\sigma_{1}.$  (6) 
 The aspect ratio is thus 
 
 $a_{\ell}/a_{s}=(1+2\chi)^{1/6}.$  (7) 
 These ellipses have the area $S_{1}=\pi{a_{s}}a_{\ell}/4$ and the momentum of inertia $I=(a_{\ell}^{2}+a_{s}^{2})m_{1}/16.$

By defining a binomially distributed variable $B_{n}$ as $B(n, \alpha)$ and independent Bernoulli distributed random variables $X_{1}, \ldots, X_{n}$ with probabilities $\mathbb{P}(X_{i} = 1) = \frac{\alpha d_{i}}{\alpha d_{i} + 1}$, the sum $S_{n} = X_{1} + \cdots + X_{n}$ can be utilized to derive an equivalent expression for $g_{n}(k)$. This yields $g_{n}(k) = (1 - \alpha)^{n - k} \prod_{j = 1}^{n} (\alpha d_{j} + 1) \frac{\mathbb{P}(S_{n} = k)}{\mathbb{P}(B_{n} = k)}$, providing a representation that is amenable to Poisson approximation.

source_text: In this paper we derive different sets of TEURs directly from the Schrödinger equations for time-dependent Hamiltonians in the general case. Our employed method of derivation as well as the results are new and different to those in the existing literature. It is important to note that only the initial states and the Halmiltionians, and neither the instantaneous eigenstates nor the full time-dependent wave functions, which would demand a full solution for a time-dependent Hamiltonian, are required for our time-energy relations. This hallmark enables wider applicability and usefulness of our TEURs.
